# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> Define the term *hazard rate* in reliability engineering. Why is it also called the *instantaneous failure rate*?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The hazard rate $h(t)$ is defined as the conditional probability density of failure at time $t$, given that the component has survived up to time $t$. Mathematically:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}$$

It is called the *instantaneous failure rate* because it represents the rate of failure at the *exact* moment $t$, conditional on survival to that moment, and not an average over any interval. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for interpretation)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> State the *memoryless property* of the exponential distribution. What constant hazard assumption does it imply?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The memoryless property states that the conditional probability of survival for an additional time $t$, given survival up to time $t_{0}$, depends only on $t$ and not on $t_{0}$:

$$P(T > t + t_{0} \mid T > t_{0}) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

It implies that the failure rate $h(t) = \lambda$ is **constant in time** — the component has no memory of its past operating history. The probability of failure in the next instant is the same regardless of the component's age. (3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for formula, 1 for implication)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **(a)** Derive the reliability function $R(t)$ for a system with a constant hazard rate $\lambda$. Hence obtain the expressions for the CDF $F(t)$, PDF $f(t)$, and the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF). **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A non-repairable electronic module used in a satellite has a constant failure rate of $0.0002$ failures per hour. For a mission duration of 1000 hours, calculate: (i) the mission reliability, (ii) the MTTF, (iii) the probability that it fails in the first 500 hours, and (iv) the design life for 99% reliability. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The hazard rate is defined as:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

Since $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$, we have $f(t) = -dR(t)/dt$. Substituting:

$$h(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Separating variables and integrating from 0 to $t$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\, du = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{1}{R(u)}\, dR(u) = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)$$

Since $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\, du = -\ln R(t)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Solving for $R(t)$:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\, du\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** For the constant hazard model, $h(u) = \lambda$, so:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The CDF and PDF are:

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}, \quad f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** MTTF is the first moment of $f(t)$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

(by integration by parts, as shown in Section 3.1 above).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hour, $T_{\text{mission}} = 1000$ hours.

**(i) Mission Reliability (2 marks):**

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2} = 0.8187$$

**(ii) MTTF (1 mark):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Probability of failure in first 500 hours (2 marks):**

$$F(500) = 1 - e^{-0.0002 \times 500} = 1 - e^{-0.1} = 1 - 0.9048 = 0.0952$$

**(iv) Design life for 99% reliability (2 marks):**

$$0.99 = e^{-0.0002 L} \implies \ln(0.99) = -0.0002 L \implies L = \frac{-\ln(0.99)}{0.0002} = \frac{0.01005}{0.0002} = 50.25 \text{ hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the negative sign** in the natural log: $\ln(0.99) = -0.01005$, not $+0.01005$. Sign errors cost **2 full marks** in design life problems.
> - **Mixing MTTF and MTBF**: For non-repairable systems they are equal, but if the question says "repairable," you must add the Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) to MTTF to get MTBF.
> - **Not writing units**: Hours, days, years — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units on every numerical answer.
> - **Skipping Step 1 of the derivation**: Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for writing down the *starting equation* $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$. Do not jump to the answer.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**
> **(a)** Explain the *memoryless property* of the exponential distribution. Prove mathematically that for a constant hazard rate system, the conditional reliability $R(t \mid t_{0})$ does not depend on $t_{0}$. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A complex avionics system consists of 4 identical subsystems connected in parallel redundancy, each with constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ per hour. The system fails only when all 4 subsystems fail. Calculate: (i) the reliability of the system for 100 hours, (ii) the MTTF of the system, (iii) the hazard rate of the parallel system at $t = 50$ hours, and (iv) comment on whether the parallel system's hazard rate is constant. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Memoryless Property (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The memoryless property states that a component's future failure probability depends only on the *length* of the future interval, not on how long it has already been operating. In probability terms:

$$P(T > t + t_{0} \mid T > t_{0}) = P(T > t)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** By the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(T > t + t_{0} \mid T > t_{0}) = \frac{P(T > t + t_{0})}{P(T > t_{0})} = \frac{R(t + t_{0})}{R(t_{0})}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** For a constant hazard system, $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{e^{-\lambda(t + t_{0})}}{e^{-\lambda t_{0}}} = e^{-\lambda(t + t_{0}) + \lambda t_{0}} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** This equals $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, which depends only on $t$ and *not* on $t_{0}$. This proves the memoryless property. **(1 mark for the physical interpretation):** The component "forgets" its past — its age is irrelevant to its future survival probability.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude: the constant hazard model and the memoryless property are mathematically equivalent statements.

---

**Part (b) — Parallel System Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: 4 identical subsystems in parallel, each with $\lambda = 0.001$ /hr. The system fails when all 4 fail, so system reliability is:

$$R_{\text{system}}(t) = 1 - (1 - R(t))^{4} = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{4}$$

**(i) System reliability at 100 hours (2 marks):**

$$R(100) = e^{-0.001 \times 100} = e^{-0.1} = 0.9048$$

$$R_{\text{system}}(100) = 1 - (1 - 0.9048)^{4} = 1 - (0.0952)^{4} = 1 - 0.0000820 = 0.99992$$

**(ii) MTTF of the system (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{system}} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R_{\text{system}}(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty}\left[1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{4}\right] dt$$

Using the binomial expansion $(1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{4} = 1 - 4e^{-\lambda t} + 6e^{-2\lambda t} - 4e^{-3\lambda t} + e^{-4\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{system}} = \int_{0}^{\infty}\left[4e^{-\lambda t} - 6e^{-2\lambda t} + 4e^{-3\lambda t} - e^{-4\lambda t}\right] dt$$

$$= \frac{4}{\lambda} - \frac{6}{2\lambda} + \frac{4}{3\lambda} - \frac{1}{4\lambda} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(4 - 3 + \frac{4}{3} - \frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(1 + \frac{16 - 3}{12}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(\frac{25}{12}\right)$$

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{system}} = \frac{25}{12 \times 0.001} = 2083.33 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Hazard rate of the parallel system at $t = 50$ hours (2 marks):**

$$f_{\text{system}}(t) = -\frac{dR_{\text{system}}}{dt} = 4(1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{3} \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$h_{\text{system}}(t) = \frac{f_{\text{system}}(t)}{R_{\text{system}}(t)} = \frac{4\lambda e^{-\lambda t}(1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{3}}{1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^{4}}$$

At $t = 50$ hours, $e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.05} = 0.9512$, so $1 - e^{-\lambda t} = 0.0488$:

$$h_{\text{system}}(50) = \frac{4 \times 0.001 \times 0.9512 \times (0.0488)^{3}}{1 - (0.0488)^{4}} = \frac{4.426 \times 10^{-6}}{0.999995} \approx 4.43 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per hour}$$

**(iv) Comment on whether the hazard rate is constant (1 mark):**

The expression for $h_{\text{system}}(t)$ is a *function of $t$*, not a constant. Therefore, **even though each individual subsystem has a constant hazard rate, the parallel system as a whole does NOT have a constant hazard rate.** The system's hazard rate *increases* with time, because as more subsystems fail, the system becomes progressively closer to total failure, and the conditional failure rate rises. This is a critical KTU concept tested in higher-mark questions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls (Question B):**
> - **Forgetting the binomial expansion** in MTTF calculation. Many students incorrectly compute $\text{MTTF} = 4/\lambda$ thinking parallel systems simply divide the rate by 4. This loses **2 marks**.
> - **Saying "the parallel system has constant hazard"**: This is the *most common conceptual error*. Even though components are constant-hazard, redundancy makes the *system* hazard rate *time-dependent*. KTU examiners specifically test this misconception.
> - **In Part (a), failing to use the definition of conditional probability** explicitly. Marks are awarded for showing the formula $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B)/P(B)$, not just the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Constant Hazard Model:**

- The four pillars are $R(t)$, $F(t)$, $f(t)$, and $h(t)$. The master identity is $R(t) + F(t) = 1$.
- The hazard rate is the conditional probability density of failure: $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$.
- The master reliability equation is $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\, du\right)$, valid for *any* hazard model.
- For constant hazard $h(t) = \lambda$, we get $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$, $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$.
- MTTF $= 1/\lambda$ and $\text{Var}(T) = 1/\lambda^{2}$. These two are direct quotients — memorize them.
- The **memoryless property** is the defining identity of the constant hazard model: the future reliability does not depend on past operating time.
- The constant hazard assumption is valid during the **Useful Life** (flat middle region) of the **Bathtub Curve**.
- A complex system made of constant-hazard components does *not* necessarily have a constant system-level hazard rate. Parallel systems have *increasing* hazard with time.
- **Mission reliability** $R(T_{\text{mission}}) = e^{-\lambda T}$ is the most commonly tested numerical in KTU 2024 papers.
- For design life problems, $L = -\ln(R_{\text{desired}})/\lambda$. Always carry the negative sign through.
- Always include **units** in numerical answers (hours, days, years, failures/hour, FIT).

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH) Model

**Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
The **Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH)** model is a non-constant failure rate model in which the **instantaneous hazard (failure) rate** increases *linearly* with operating time. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$h(t) = \lambda t, \quad t \ge 0$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is the proportionality constant with units of **failures per unit time squared (1/time²)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The LIH model is a **special case of the Weibull distribution** where the shape parameter $\beta = 2$. This is a high-frequency KTU board exam question — always remember the link $\beta = 2 \implies \text{LIH}$.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition
Imagine the filament of an **incandescent bulb**. As time passes, the filament gets thinner due to evaporation, vibration, and thermal stress. So, the *older* the bulb gets, the *more dangerous* every additional second becomes — failure does not strike randomly, it becomes more likely. This "wear-in" pattern is precisely the Linearly Increasing Hazard. The failure rate is not constant (unlike an exponential model), but grows proportionally with age.

---

## 1.2 The Weibull Model

**Definition:**
The **Weibull distribution** is a versatile, continuous probability distribution widely used in reliability engineering to model time-to-failure data. It is governed by two parameters:
- **Shape parameter** $\beta > 0$ (dimensionless) — controls the *form* of the hazard rate.
- **Scale parameter** $\alpha > 0$ (units of time) — controls the *spread* of the distribution.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of $\beta$ as the **"personality"** of a component and $\alpha$ as its **"lifespan scale"**:
- If $\beta < 1$: the component is a *burn-in failure* type (infant mortality) — like a cheap electronic gadget.
- If $\beta = 1$: behaves like a *constant hazard* (exponential) — random failures, like a well-designed resistor.
- If $\beta > 1$: the component *ages* — like a ball bearing or a filament bulb.
- If $\beta = 2$: specifically, **Linearly Increasing Hazard** (LIH).
- If $\beta = 3$: approximately *Normally distributed* failures.

> [!NOTE]
> The Weibull distribution is the **Swiss Army Knife** of reliability engineering. Almost any monotonic hazard rate can be approximated by choosing the right $\beta$.

---

## 1.3 Density Function and Distribution Function

**Probability Density Function (PDF):**
$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha} \left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right], \quad t \ge 0$$

**Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):**
$$F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right], \quad t \ge 0$$

**Reliability Function (Survival Function):**
$$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

### Intuitive Explanation
- $F(t)$: "What is the probability the component has *failed* by time $t$?"
- $R(t)$: "What is the probability the component is *still alive* at time $t$?"
- $f(t)$: "At exactly time $t$, how densely packed are the failures?" — a rate of probability accumulation.

---

## 1.4 Reliability Analysis

**Definition:**
Reliability analysis is the systematic study of the probability that a system or component performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time, $R(t) = P(T > t)$.

**Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** for the Weibull model:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \alpha \,\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

where $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the **Gamma function**.

---

## 1.5 Important Distributions and Their Choice

| Distribution | Hazard Shape | Best For Modeling |
|--------------|--------------|-------------------|
| Exponential | Constant | Random failures, memoryless systems |
| Weibull (β<1) | Decreasing | Infant mortality, debugging period |
| Weibull (β=1) | Constant | Equivalent to Exponential |
| Weibull (β=2) | Linearly Increasing | Wear-out phase onset |
| Weibull (β>1) | Increasing | Aging components |
| Normal | Bathtub upper | Wear-out, end-of-life |
| Lognormal | Skewed increasing | Repair times, fatigue cracks |

> [!TIP]
> The **bathtub curve** is the *grand unified theory* of reliability: decreasing hazard (infant mortality) → constant hazard (useful life) → increasing hazard (wear-out).

---

## 1.6 Standard Deviation and Variance

**Definition:**
For a random variable $T$ representing time-to-failure, the **variance** measures the average squared spread of failure times about the mean, and the **standard deviation** is its square root.

$$\text{Var}(T) = E[T^2] - \{E[T]\}^2, \qquad \sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(T)}$$

For the Weibull distribution:

$$\sigma = \alpha \sqrt{\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \left[\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **LIH ($\beta=2$)**: $\text{MTTF} = \alpha \Gamma(3/2) = \alpha \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$, and $\sigma = \alpha\sqrt{\Gamma(2) - \pi/4} = \alpha\sqrt{1 - \pi/4}$.

---

## 1.7 Conditional Probability — Introduction

**Definition:**
The **conditional probability** of an event $A$ given that event $B$ has occurred is:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0$$

### Intuitive Explanation
"Given that $B$ has already happened, what is the *revised* chance of $A$?" For example: "Given that it is cloudy (B), what is the probability it will rain (A)?" — the prior information *restricts* our sample space.

---

## 1.8 Multiplication Rule

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B) = P(B \mid A)\,P(A)$$

This generalizes to $n$ events:
$$P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n) = P(A_1)\,P(A_2 \mid A_1)\,P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2) \cdots$$

---

## 1.9 Independent Events

Two events $A$ and $B$ are **independent** if and only if:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A)\,P(B)$$

Equivalently, $P(A \mid B) = P(A)$ — knowing $B$ gives *no information* about $A$.

> [!NOTE]
> *Mutually exclusive* events ($A \cap B = \emptyset$) are **NOT** independent (except in the trivial case $P(A) = 0$ or $P(B) = 0$). A common KTU board trap!

---

## 1.10 Venn Diagram

A **Venn diagram** is a pictorial representation of a sample space $S$ as a rectangle with events as overlapping circles inside. It is used to visualize:
- Unions $A \cup B$
- Intersections $A \cap B$
- Complements $A^c$
- Conditional probability $P(A \mid B)$ as the *ratio* of the intersection area to the area of the conditioning event.

---

## 1.11 Hazard Rate as Conditional Probability

**KTU Board Standard Definition:**
The **hazard rate** $h(t)$ is the conditional probability density of failure at exactly time $t$, *given* that the component has survived up to time $t$:

$$h(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the *bridge concept* connecting probability theory to reliability theory. Every hazard function is, fundamentally, a *conditional* probability per unit time.

---

## 1.12 Bayes' Theorem

**Statement (KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight):**
For a set of mutually exclusive and exhaustive events $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$ and an event $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_i)\,P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j)\,P(A_j)}$$

### Intuitive Explanation
Bayes' theorem lets us *invert* conditional probabilities. It answers: "I observed $B$ — what is the most likely *cause* $A_i$?"

In reliability, it is the foundation of **Bayesian reliability updating** — combining prior beliefs about a component with observed failure data to produce updated posterior beliefs.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### A. The Weibull Hazard Rate — Derivation Logic

The Weibull hazard rate is derived from the relationship $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$:

1. Start with $R(t) = \exp[-(t/\alpha)^\beta]$.
2. Differentiate to get $f(t) = -R'(t)$.
3. Form the ratio: $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$.
4. Simplify using chain rule — all exponential terms cancel except the polynomial factor.

**Result:**

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha} \left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

The *Why*: $\beta$ controls the *shape* of failure rate (constant / increasing / decreasing) and $\alpha$ controls the *time-scale* at which failures occur.

### B. Hazard Rate Behavior — The Three Regimes

| Regime | Condition | Behavior | Engineering Example |
|--------|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| Infant mortality | $\beta < 1$ | $h(t)$ **decreases** | Burn-in screening, software debugging |
| Useful life | $\beta = 1$ | $h(t) = 1/\alpha$ **constant** | Random shocks, exponential regime |
| Wear-out | $\beta > 1$ | $h(t)$ **increases** | Bearings, filaments, fatigue |
| LIH | $\beta = 2$ | $h(t) = 2t/\alpha^2$ **linear** | Early wear-out phase |

### C. Linearly Increasing Hazard — Origin

Setting $h(t) = \lambda t$ and solving the differential equation $R'(t)/R(t) = -\lambda t$:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda t^2}{2}\right)$$

Comparing with $R(t) = \exp[-(t/\alpha)^2]$ gives $\alpha = \sqrt{2/\lambda}$.

> The LIH model is sometimes called the **Rayleigh distribution** (a Weibull with $\beta = 2$).

### D. Conditional Probability — Logical Steps

1. **Restrict** the sample space from $S$ to $B$.
2. **Re-normalize** — since $B$ is now our universe, divide $P(A \cap B)$ by $P(B)$.
3. This is exactly $P(A \mid B)$.

### E. Bayes' Theorem — Logical Derivation Outline

Starting from the definition of conditional probability twice:

$$P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(A_i \cap B)}{P(B)} = \frac{P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)}{P(B)}$$

Then using the **Law of Total Probability** for the denominator:

$$P(B) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) P(A_j)$$

This yields the final Bayes' formula.

### F. Why Hazard Rate is a Conditional Probability

For an infinitesimal interval $[t, t+\Delta t]$:

$$P(\text{fail in } [t,t+\Delta t] \mid T > t) = \frac{F(t+\Delta t) - F(t)}{1 - F(t)}$$

Dividing by $\Delta t$ and taking the limit $\Delta t \to 0$ gives $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ — *the formal conditional probability per unit time of failure, given survival to $t$.*

### G. Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Aerospace**: Weibull fits turbine blade fatigue life.
- **Electronics**: Weibull models solder joint failure.
- **Civil**: Lognormal models concrete crack propagation.
- **Nuclear**: Bayesian updating of pump failure probabilities from operational data.
- **Software**: Decreasing Weibull models debugging bug-detection rate.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Weibull PDF | $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ | $t \ge 0,\ \alpha,\beta > 0$ |
| 2 | Weibull CDF | $F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ | $t \ge 0$ |
| 3 | Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ | $t \ge 0$ |
| 4 | Weibull Hazard | $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $t \ge 0$ |
| 5 | LIH Hazard | $h(t) = \lambda t$ | $t \ge 0,\ \lambda > 0$ |
| 6 | LIH Reliability | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lambda t^2}{2}\right)$ | $t \ge 0$ |
| 7 | LIH PDF | $f(t) = \lambda t \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\lambda t^2}{2}\right)$ | $t \ge 0$ |
| 8 | Weibull MTTF | $E[T] = \alpha\,\Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | $\Gamma$ is gamma function |
| 9 | Weibull Variance | $\text{Var}(T) = \alpha^2\!\left[\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{2}{\beta}\right) - \left(\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)\right)^2\right]$ | Always $\ge 0$ |
| 10 | Weibull Std. Dev. | $\sigma = \alpha\sqrt{\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{2}{\beta}\right) - \left[\Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]^2}$ | units of time |
| 11 | Conditional Probability | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | $P(B) > 0$ |
| 12 | Multiplication Rule | $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B)$ | General |
| 13 | Independence | $P(A \cap B) = P(A)\,P(B)$ | $A, B$ independent |
| 14 | Total Probability | $P(B) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) P(A_j)$ | $A_j$ mutually exclusive, exhaustive |
| 15 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(A_i \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) P(A_j)}$ | $P(B) > 0$ |
| 16 | Hazard from PDF & R | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$ | All continuous models |
| 17 | R from h(t) | $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right)$ | Cumulative hazard |
| 18 | MTTF from R | $\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$ | For non-repairable items |
| 19 | LIH — $\alpha$ to $\lambda$ | $\alpha = \sqrt{2/\lambda}$ | LIH = Weibull $\beta=2$ |
| 20 | LIH — $\lambda$ to $\alpha$ | $\lambda = 2/\alpha^2$ | Inverse relationship |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always write the **boundary conditions** ($t \ge 0$, parameter ranges) alongside your formula. Examiners reward completeness.

---

## 2.3 Special-Value Gamma Function Shortcuts (KTU High-Yield)

| $\beta$ | $\Gamma(1+1/\beta)$ | MTTF / $\alpha$ |
|---------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 0.5 | $\Gamma(3) = 2!$ | $2\alpha$ |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Weibull Hazard Rate from First Principles

**Goal:** Derive $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ from the reliability function.

**Starting Point:** Reliability function of Weibull:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

**Step 1 — Compute the PDF using** $f(t) = -R'(t)$:

Differentiate $R(t)$ with respect to $t$. Using the chain rule on the exponent $-(t/\alpha)^\beta$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right] = -\beta \left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha}$$

Therefore:

$$R'(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right] \cdot \left[-\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\right]$$

$$f(t) = -R'(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Form the hazard ratio** $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$:

$$h(t) = \frac{\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]}{\exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the exponentials** (they are identical and nonzero):

$$\boxed{\,h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\,}$$

This is the **Weibull hazard rate** — derived rigorously.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — LIH Reliability from $h(t) = \lambda t$

**Step 1 — Use the cumulative hazard relationship:**

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(u)\,du\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $h(u) = \lambda u$:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda u\,du\right) = \exp\!\left(-\lambda \cdot \frac{u^2}{2}\bigg|_{0}^{t}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda t^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Weibull form** $R(t) = \exp[-(t/\alpha)^2]$:

$$\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{2} = \frac{\lambda t^{2}}{2} \implies \frac{1}{\alpha^{2}} = \frac{\lambda}{2} \implies \alpha = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\lambda}}$$

This proves the equivalence of **LIH ↔ Weibull with $\beta=2$**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — MTTF of Weibull

**Step 1 — Write MTTF in terms of** $R(t)$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right] dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $u = (t/\alpha)^{\beta}$ so that $t = \alpha u^{1/\beta}$ and $dt = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta} u^{(1/\beta)-1} du$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\alpha}{\beta} u^{(1/\beta)-1}\, du = \frac{\alpha}{\beta}\int_{0}^{\infty} u^{(1/\beta)-1} e^{-u}\, du$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Gamma function** $\Gamma(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{s-1} e^{-u} du$ with $s = 1/\beta$:

$$\boxed{\,\text{MTTF} = \alpha\,\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — LIH MTTF Numerical

**Problem:** A ball bearing has LIH failure with $\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour². Find MTTF and standard deviation.

**Step 1 — Find $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\lambda}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{2 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{10^{6}} = 1000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Compute MTTF** (since $\beta = 2$):

$$\text{MTTF} = \alpha\,\Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) = 1000 \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = 1000 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$$

$$\text{MTTF} = 500\sqrt{\pi} \approx 500 \times 1.7725 \approx 886.23 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Variance:**

$$\text{Var}(T) = \alpha^{2}\!\left[\Gamma\!\left(1+\frac{2}{2}\right) - \left(\Gamma\!\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\right)\right)^{2}\right] = (1000)^{2}\!\left[\Gamma(2) - (\Gamma(1.5))^{2}\right]$$

$$= 10^{6}\!\left[1 - \left(\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\right)^{2}\right] = 10^{6}\!\left[1 - \frac{\pi}{4}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Compute Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma = 1000\sqrt{1 - \frac{\pi}{4}} = 1000\sqrt{0.2146} \approx 1000 \times 0.4633 \approx 463.3 \text{ hours}$$

**Result:** $\text{MTTF} \approx 886.2$ hr, $\sigma \approx 463.3$ hr.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Conditional Probability & Bayes Examples

### Example A — Multiplication Rule

A system has two stages. $P(\text{Stage 1 OK}) = 0.95$. Given stage 1 OK, $P(\text{Stage 2 OK}) = 0.90$. Find $P(\text{both OK})$.

**Solution:**

$$P(\text{both OK}) = P(\text{Stage 1}) \cdot P(\text{Stage 2} \mid \text{Stage 1}) = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855$$

---

### Example B — Independent Events

If $P(A) = 0.6$, $P(B) = 0.4$, and $A, B$ are independent, find $P(A \cap B)$.

**Solution:**

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B) = 0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.24$$

---

### Example C — Total Probability & Bayes' Theorem

A factory has 3 machines: M1 (50%), M2 (30%), M3 (20%) of production. Defect rates: M1=2%, M2=3%, M3=5%. A defective item is found. Find the probability it came from M2.

**Step 1 — Define events:**

Let $A_i$ = "item from machine $i$", $B$ = "item is defective".

Given: $P(A_1) = 0.5,\ P(A_2) = 0.3,\ P(A_3) = 0.2$ and $P(B \mid A_1) = 0.02,\ P(B \mid A_2) = 0.03,\ P(B \mid A_3) = 0.05$.

**Step 2 — Total probability of defect:**

$$P(B) = (0.02)(0.5) + (0.03)(0.3) + (0.05)(0.2) = 0.010 + 0.009 + 0.010 = 0.029$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(A_2 \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_2) P(A_2)}{P(B)} = \frac{(0.03)(0.3)}{0.029} = \frac{0.009}{0.029} \approx 0.3103$$

**Result:** About 31.03% chance the defective item came from M2.

---

### Example D — Hazard Rate as Conditional Probability

A component has Weibull $R(t) = e^{-(t/1000)^{2}}$, so $\alpha = 1000$, $\beta = 2$ (LIH). Find $h(500)$.

**Solution:**

$$h(500) = \frac{2}{1000}\left(\frac{500}{1000}\right)^{2-1} = \frac{2}{1000} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{1000} = 0.001 \text{ per hour}$$

Interpretation: At $t=500$ hr, the *conditional* failure probability density is $10^{-3}$ per hour, *given* survival to 500 hr.

---

## 3.6 Variance Derivation — General Weibull

**Starting from** $E[T^k] = \int_0^{\infty} t^k f(t)\, dt$:

$$E[T^k] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^{k} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} t^{\beta-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right] dt$$

Substituting $u = (t/\alpha)^{\beta}$:

$$E[T^k] = \alpha^{k} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{k/\beta} e^{-u} du = \alpha^{k} \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{k}{\beta}\right)$$

**Step 1:** $E[T] = \alpha \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.

**Step 2:** $E[T^{2}] = \alpha^{2}\Gamma(1 + 2/\beta)$.

**Step 3:** Variance:

$$\text{Var}(T) = E[T^{2}] - (E[T])^{2} = \alpha^{2}\!\left[\Gamma\!\left(1+\frac{2}{\beta}\right) - \left(\Gamma\!\left(1+\frac{1}{\beta}\right)\right)^{2}\right]$$

This is the **standard KTU result** for Weibull variance.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Reliability Function Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Time to Failure T] --> B[Choose Distribution Family]
    B --> C{Is hazard constant?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Exponential Beta=1]
    C -- No --> E[Weibull Model]
    E --> F{Shape parameter beta}
    F -- beta less than 1 --> G[Infant Mortality Decreasing Hazard]
    F -- beta equals 1 --> H[Constant Hazard Memoryless]
    F -- beta equals 2 --> I[LIH Linearly Increasing]
    F -- beta greater than 1 --> J[Wear Out Increasing Hazard]
    D --> K[Compute R of t = exp minus t by alpha]
    G --> L[Compute R of t = exp minus t by alpha to the beta]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    L --> M[Derive f of t from minus derivative of R]
    M --> N[Derive h of t equals f of t divided by R of t]
    N --> O[Compute MTTF equals alpha times Gamma of 1 plus 1 by beta]
    O --> P[Compute Variance and Std Deviation]
    P --> Q[Final Reliability Report]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Conditional Probability & Bayes Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Space S] --> B[Event A occurs]
    A --> C[Event B occurs]
    B --> D[Intersection A and B]
    C --> D
    D --> E{P of B greater than 0?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Conditional P of A given B equals P of A and B divided by P of B]
    E -- No --> G[Undefined]
    F --> H[Multiplication Rule: P of A and B equals P of A given B times P of B]
    H --> I{A and B independent?}
    I -- Yes --> J[P of A and B equals P of A times P of B]
    I -- No --> K[Keep conditional form]
    F --> L[Bayes Inverse: P of A given B equals P of B given A times P of A divided by Total P of B]
    L --> M[Use Law of Total Probability in denominator]
    M --> N[Posterior P of A given B for reliability update]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Bathtub Curve & Hazard Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time t equals 0] --> B[Infant Mortality Phase]
    B --> C[Hazard h of t is decreasing]
    C --> D[Useful Life Phase]
    D --> E[Hazard h of t is approximately constant]
    E --> F[Wear Out Phase]
    F --> G[Hazard h of t is increasing - LIH to Weibull beta greater than 1]
    G --> H[End of Life Failure]
    B -.->|Weibull beta less than 1| I[Mathematical Model]
    D -.->|Exponential beta equals 1| I
    F -.->|Weibull beta greater than 1| I
    I --> J[Unified Reliability Function R of t]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — LIH ↔ Weibull Equivalence Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LIH Model: h of t equals lambda t] --> B[Integrate: integral of h of u du equals lambda t squared by 2]
    B --> C[R of t equals exp of minus lambda t squared by 2]
    C --> D[Compare with Weibull: R of t equals exp of minus t by alpha to the beta]
    D --> E[Match exponents: beta equals 2 and alpha equals sqrt of 2 by lambda]
    E --> F[Conclusion: LIH is Weibull with beta equals 2]
    F --> G[Apply MTTF formula: MTTF equals alpha times Gamma of 3 over 2 equals alpha sqrt pi by 2]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Flow — Probability Axiom Hierarchy (Conditional → Bayes)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Probability Axioms Kolmogorov] --> B[Conditional Probability Definition]
    B --> C[Multiplication Rule]
    C --> D[Independence: P of A and B equals P of A times P of B]
    C --> E[Dependence: Keep conditional form]
    B --> F[Law of Total Probability]
    F --> G[Bayes Theorem for n mutually exclusive causes]
    G --> H[Bayesian Reliability Updating]
    H --> I[Posterior Distribution of Component Life]
    I --> J[Engineering Decision: Replace or Maintain]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Linearly Increasing Hazard (LIH) model. Show that it is a special case of the Weibull distribution.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The LIH model assumes a hazard rate that increases linearly with time:

$$h(t) = \lambda t, \quad t \ge 0$$

The corresponding reliability function, obtained by integrating $h(t)$, is:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\lambda t^2}{2}\right) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{2}\right] \quad \text{with } \alpha = \sqrt{2/\lambda}$$

This is precisely the **Weibull reliability function** with shape parameter $\beta = 2$. Hence LIH = Weibull with $\beta = 2$. **(1 mark each for definition, derivation, and conclusion.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain Bayes' theorem for two events. Why is it important in reliability engineering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Statement:** For two events $A_1, A_2$ (partition of $S$) and an event $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_i)\,P(A_i)}{P(B \mid A_1) P(A_1) + P(B \mid A_2) P(A_2)}, \quad i = 1,2$$

**Explanation:** It allows inversion of conditional probabilities. **Importance (1 mark):** It enables *Bayesian reliability updating* — combining prior failure-rate beliefs with observed test data to compute posterior component reliability, which is critical when failure data is scarce (e.g., aerospace, nuclear systems).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the probability density function, reliability function, and hazard rate of the **Weibull distribution**. Discuss the three regimes of the shape parameter $\beta$ with engineering examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mechanical component has a Weibull time-to-failure distribution with $\alpha = 5000$ hours and $\beta = 2$ (Linearly Increasing Hazard). Compute:
- (i) MTTF
- (ii) Variance and standard deviation
- (iii) Reliability at $t = 2000$ hours
- (iv) Hazard rate at $t = 2000$ hours. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The Weibull reliability function is given by:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right], \quad t \ge 0$$

**PDF derivation** (3 marks): Using $f(t) = -R'(t)$:

$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

**Hazard rate derivation** (2 marks): Using $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

**Three regimes** (2 marks):

| Regime | $\beta$ | Behavior | Example |
|--------|---------|----------|---------|
| Infant mortality | $\beta < 1$ | Decreasing | Debugging electronics |
| Constant / useful life | $\beta = 1$ | Constant | Random resistor failure |
| Wear-out | $\beta > 1$ | Increasing | Ball bearings, filaments |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given $\alpha = 5000$ hr, $\beta = 2$ (LIH).

**(i) MTTF** (2 marks):

$$\text{MTTF} = \alpha \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) = 5000 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} = 2500\sqrt{\pi} \approx 4431.13 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) Variance and standard deviation** (2 marks):

$$\text{Var}(T) = \alpha^{2}\left[\Gamma(2) - \left(\Gamma(1.5)\right)^{2}\right] = (5000)^{2}\left[1 - \frac{\pi}{4}\right]$$

$$\text{Var}(T) = 2.5 \times 10^{7} \times 0.2146 \approx 5.366 \times 10^{6} \text{ hr}^{2}$$

$$\sigma = 5000\sqrt{1 - \pi/4} \approx 5000 \times 0.4633 \approx 2316.5 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Reliability at t = 2000 hours** (1.5 marks):

$$R(2000) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{2000}{5000}\right)^{2}\right] = \exp(-0.16) \approx 0.8521$$

**(iv) Hazard rate at t = 2000 hours** (1.5 marks):

$$h(2000) = \frac{2}{5000}\left(\frac{2000}{5000}\right)^{1} = \frac{2}{5000} \times 0.4 = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define **conditional probability**. State and prove the **multiplication rule**. Explain with a Venn diagram how $P(A \mid B)$ is interpreted geometrically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a manufacturing plant, three machines M1, M2, M3 produce 50%, 30%, and 20% of the total output respectively. The defect rates are 2%, 3%, and 4% respectively. A defective item is found during inspection.
- (i) Find the probability that it came from M1.
- (ii) Given that the defective item came from either M1 or M3, find the posterior probability it came from M1. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The conditional probability of $A$ given $B$ is:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad P(B) > 0$$

**Multiplication Rule — Proof (3 marks):**

Starting from the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

Multiply both sides by $P(B)$:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) P(B)$$

Symmetrically:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) P(A)$$

**Venn Diagram Interpretation (2 marks):**
- The full sample space $S$ is the rectangle.
- $B$ is a circle inside $S$.
- $A \cap B$ is the *overlap* between circle $A$ and circle $B$.
- $P(A \mid B)$ is the *ratio* of the area of the overlap to the area of $B$ — i.e., we restrict our universe to $B$ and ask what fraction of $B$ is also $A$.

---

**Part (b) — Bayes Application (7 marks):**

Define: $A_i$ = "item from machine $i$", $B$ = "item defective".

Given:
- $P(A_1) = 0.5,\ P(A_2) = 0.3,\ P(A_3) = 0.2$
- $P(B \mid A_1) = 0.02,\ P(B \mid A_2) = 0.03,\ P(B \mid A_3) = 0.04$

**(i) Total probability of defect (1.5 marks):**

$$P(B) = (0.02)(0.5) + (0.03)(0.3) + (0.04)(0.2) = 0.010 + 0.009 + 0.008 = 0.027$$

**Posterior for M1 (2 marks):**

$$P(A_1 \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_1) P(A_1)}{P(B)} = \frac{(0.02)(0.5)}{0.027} = \frac{0.010}{0.027} \approx 0.3704$$

**Result:** About **37.04%** chance the defective item came from M1.

**(ii) Posterior given M1 or M3 (3.5 marks):**

We want $P(A_1 \mid A_1 \cup A_3)$ *within the context of defect*, i.e., effectively $P(A_1 \mid B,\ A_1 \cup A_3)$.

Restricted probabilities: Given item is from M1 or M3,
$$P(A_1 \mid A_1 \cup A_3) = \frac{P(A_1)}{P(A_1)+P(A_3)} = \frac{0.5}{0.7} \approx 0.7143$$

Defect probability given M1 or M3:

$$P(B \mid A_1 \cup A_3) = \frac{P(B \mid A_1)P(A_1) + P(B \mid A_3)P(A_3)}{P(A_1)+P(A_3)} = \frac{0.010 + 0.008}{0.7} = \frac{0.018}{0.7} \approx 0.02571$$

Posterior probability:

$$P(A_1 \mid B,\ A_1 \cup A_3) = \frac{P(B \mid A_1) P(A_1 \mid A_1 \cup A_3)}{P(B \mid A_1 \cup A_3)} = \frac{(0.02)(0.7143)}{0.02571} \approx 0.5556$$

**Result:** Approximately **55.56%** chance the defective item (known to be from M1 or M3) came from M1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting boundary conditions** — Always specify $t \ge 0$ and parameter ranges ($\alpha, \beta > 0$). **(−0.5 to −1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing LIH ($\beta=2$) with exponential ($\beta=1$)** — KTU examiners set traps; double-check which $\beta$ is being used. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Using wrong Gamma function values** — $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.8862$, NOT $0.5\sqrt{\pi}$. Memorize or derive. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **In Bayes' theorem, forgetting the Total Probability denominator** — Students often write $P(A_i \mid B) = P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)$, which is wrong. **(−2 marks)**
> 5. **Treating "mutually exclusive" and "independent" as synonyms** — They are NOT the same. M.E. implies $P(A \cap B) = 0$; independence implies $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always write "hours," "per hour," etc. **(−0.5 mark)**
> 7. **Wrong sign in hazard integral** — $R(t) = \exp(-\int h(u) du)$, NOT $+\int$. **(−1 mark)**
> 8. **In Venn diagram questions, not labeling the regions** $A$, $B$, $A \cap B$, $A \cup B$ — examiners expect clear labels. **(−0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2):**

- [x] **LIH model** $h(t) = \lambda t$ is a **Weibull with $\beta=2$** and $\alpha = \sqrt{2/\lambda}$.
- [x] **Weibull PDF**: $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\exp[-(t/\alpha)^\beta]$.
- [x] **Weibull CDF**: $F(t) = 1 - \exp[-(t/\alpha)^\beta]$.
- [x] **Weibull Reliability**: $R(t) = \exp[-(t/\alpha)^\beta]$.
- [x] **Weibull Hazard**: $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$.
- [x] **Weibull MTTF**: $\alpha \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
- [x] **Weibull Variance**: $\alpha^{2}\left[\Gamma(1 + 2/\beta) - (\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta))^{2}\right]$.
- [x] **LIH MTTF** (β=2): $\alpha\sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.886\alpha$.
- [x] **LIH σ** (β=2): $\alpha\sqrt{1 - \pi/4} \approx 0.463\alpha$.
- [x] **Distribution choice guide**: $\beta < 1$ infant, $\beta = 1$ constant, $\beta = 2$ LIH, $\beta > 1$ wear-out.
- [x] **Conditional probability**: $P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$.
- [x] **Multiplication rule**: $P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) P(B) = P(B \mid A) P(A)$.
- [x] **Independence condition**: $P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)$.
- [x] **Bayes' Theorem**: $P(A_i \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A_i) P(A_i)}{\sum_j P(B \mid A_j) P(A_j)}$.
- [x] **Total Probability Law**: $P(B) = \sum_j P(B \mid A_j) P(A_j)$.
- [x] **Hazard as conditional probability**: $h(t) = f(t)/R(t) = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$.
- [x] **Reliability from hazard**: $R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t h(u)\, du\right)$.
- [x] **Venn diagram regions**: Always label $A$, $B$, $A \cap B$, $A \cup B$, $A^c$, $B^c$, and the universal set $S$.
- [x] **Mutually exclusive ≠ Independent** — common KTU trap.
- [x] **Gamma function values to memorize**: $\Gamma(1)=1$, $\Gamma(1.5)=\sqrt{\pi}/2$, $\Gamma(2)=1!=1$.
- [x] **Bayesian reliability updating** is the engineering application of Bayes' theorem.

> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *Weibull = Universal Reliability Tool. Bayes = Universal Inference Tool. Conditional Probability = The Bridge Between Them.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series, Parallel and Mixed Configurations, Complex Systems, Logic Diagrams, Markov Models; Reliability Improvement and Repairable Systems: Redundancy, Element, Unit and Standby Redundancy, Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT525 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Series, Parallel and Mixed Configurations, Complex Systems, Logic Diagrams, Markov Models; Reliability Improvement and Repairable Systems: Redundancy, Element, Unit and Standby Redundancy, Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 System Configurations — The Building Blocks of Reliability

In **Reliability Engineering**, no component exists in isolation. Individual elements are interconnected to form a **system**, and the overall system reliability depends not only on the reliability of its parts but also critically on **how those parts are interconnected**. The configuration (or topology) of interconnections fundamentally changes the mathematical modeling of system reliability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **System Reliability ($R_s$)** is defined as the probability that a system performs its intended function under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time. The configuration describes the logical/physical arrangement of components that determines whether the system succeeds or fails.

### 1.2 The Three Fundamental Configurations

| Configuration | Functional Philosophy | Intuitive Analogy |
|---------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Series** | System fails if **any one** element fails | A chain — the chain breaks if **any single link** snaps |
| **Parallel** | System works if **at least one** element works | A multi-lane highway — traffic flows if **at least one lane** is open |
| **Mixed (Series-Parallel)** | Combination of both in hierarchical structure | A vehicle — engine, wheels, brakes in **series** for motion; multiple brake pads in **parallel** within the brake subsystem |

### 1.3 Concept of Complex Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complex System Definition**
> A system whose reliability cannot be expressed as a simple series or parallel product of its component reliabilities is termed a **complex system**. These systems contain non-series-parallel topologies such as bridges (lattice), $k$-out-of-$n$ configurations, or cross-linked networks. They require techniques like **Conditional Reliability**, **Decomposition**, or **Bayesian cut-set / tie-set methods**.

**Real-world example:** A power grid with a parallel redundancy link between two otherwise series substations — a classic **bridge configuration** that is neither purely series nor purely parallel.

### 1.4 Logic Diagrams — Visualizing Reliability

A **Logic Diagram** (often a Reliability Block Diagram, RBD) is a pictorial representation of the functional relationships between components. Two key types exist:

- **Success Block Diagram (RBD)** — Shows paths for *successful* operation (used in series/parallel analysis).
- **Fault Tree (FT)** — Shows combinations of *failure* events (Boolean logic gates, used in safety/reliability analysis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction**
> RBDs answer: *"What combinations of working components make the system work?"*
> Fault Trees answer: *"What combinations of component failures cause the system to fail?"*
> Both are **dual** representations of the same physical system.

### 1.5 Markov Models — The State-Space Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Markov Reliability Model**
> A **Markov Model** represents a system as a collection of discrete **states** (e.g., working, failed, degraded) with **transition rates** (failure rates $\lambda$, repair rates $\mu$) between them. It assumes the **memoryless (Markovian) property**: the future state depends only on the present state, not on the history of how the system arrived there.

**Intuition:** Think of a repairable system as a patient in a hospital. The patient is either "healthy" (state 1) or "sick" (state 2). The probability of getting sick tomorrow depends only on being healthy today — not on how many times they were sick last month.

### 1.6 Reliability Improvement — The Role of Redundancy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Redundancy Definition**
> **Redundancy** is the existence of more than one means (component, path, or subsystem) for accomplishing a given function. It is the **single most powerful technique** to improve system reliability without changing individual component qualities.

The three principal types are:

1. **Element (Component) Redundancy** — Spare parts built into the same functional block.
2. **Unit Redundancy** — Complete functional units operating in parallel.
3. **Standby Redundancy** — Inactive spares that are switched in only upon failure of the active unit.

### 1.7 Optimization in Reliability

Reliability optimization determines the optimal **redundancy allocation** and **component reliability** assignment to maximize system reliability subject to constraints on **cost**, **weight**, **volume**, or **power budget** — a classic constrained non-linear programming problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Series Configuration — Operational Logic

A **series system** functions only when **all** components function. The failure of any single component causes system failure.

**Operational steps (in plain language):**
1. Identify all $n$ components in the series string.
2. Each component $i$ has reliability $R_i(t)$ — the probability it survives time $t$.
3. For the system to succeed, **all** components must succeed **simultaneously** and **independently**.
4. Therefore, multiply the individual reliabilities (intersection of independent events).
5. The system unreliability $Q_s = 1 - R_s = 1 - \prod R_i$ (de Morgan's law for complements).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Series configuration is the **weakest-link** topology. The system reliability is **always less than the reliability of the weakest component** ($\min R_i$). Adding more series components **reduces** reliability.

## 2.2 Parallel Configuration — Operational Logic

A **parallel system** functions as long as **at least one** component functions. All components must fail for the system to fail.

**Operational steps:**
1. The system fails only if **all** $n$ parallel components fail simultaneously.
2. Component $i$ fails with probability $Q_i = 1 - R_i$.
3. All components failing = intersection of failure events = $\prod Q_i$.
4. System reliability $R_s = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$.
5. Symmetric form for equal components: $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Parallel configuration is the **strongest-link** topology. System reliability is **always greater than the reliability of any single component**. Adding more parallel paths **increases** reliability but with **diminishing returns** (concave curve).

## 2.3 Mixed (Series-Parallel) Configuration

Mixed configurations arise when subsystems themselves are configured in series or parallel. The analysis is performed **hierarchically**:

1. Compute reliability of each **sub-block** (series or parallel).
2. Treat each sub-block as a single equivalent "component" with the computed reliability.
3. Reduce the overall topology by repeated application of series and parallel rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption for Mixed Systems:** All components are **statistically independent** (failure of one does not influence failure of another). KTU exam questions almost always assume independence unless stated otherwise.

## 2.4 Complex Systems — The Bridge Configuration

The **bridge (lattice) system** is the canonical non-series-parallel system. It has 5 components: 2 in the top/bottom of a square plus one diagonal (bridge element).

**Two solution methods:**

| Method | Core Idea |
|--------|-----------|
| **Conditional Reliability (Decomposition)** | Condition on the state of the bridge element. If it works, the system reduces to a series-parallel network. If it fails, the system reduces to another series-parallel network. Combine using total probability. |
| **Path / Cut-Set Enumeration** | Enumerate all **minimal paths** (combinations that guarantee success) and **minimal cut-sets** (combinations that guarantee failure). Apply inclusion-exclusion or exact Boolean expansion. |

**Conditional (Decomposition) Method — Logic Steps:**
1. Select a "key" component $j$ (typically the bridge element).
2. Let $A_j$ = "component $j$ works" with probability $R_j$.
3. Let $\bar{A_j}$ = "component $j$ fails" with probability $1 - R_j$.
4. Compute $R_s \vert A_j$ — reliability of the reduced network when $j$ is shorted (always working).
5. Compute $R_s \vert \bar{A_j}$ — reliability of the reduced network when $j$ is open (always failed).
6. Apply Total Probability: $R_s = R_j \cdot (R_s \vert A_j) + (1-R_j) \cdot (R_s \vert \bar{A_j})$.

## 2.5 Logic Diagrams — Reliability Block Diagrams (RBD)

**RBD Construction Rules (exam-critical):**
1. Draw blocks in **series** when **all** must work for system success.
2. Draw blocks in **parallel** when **at least one** must work for system success.
3. A system is failed when there is **no continuous path** of working blocks from input to output.

**Fault Tree Construction Rules (dual of RBD):**
1. Use **AND gates** to represent parallel branches (all inputs must occur).
2. Use **OR gates** to represent series connections (any input occurring causes the event).
3. Top event = undesired system failure.

## 2.6 Markov Models — Transition Rate Analysis

A **continuous-time Markov chain** for reliability has:
- **States** $S_0, S_1, \dots, S_n$ representing system condition.
- **Transition rates** $\lambda_{ij}$ from state $i$ to state $j$ (typically $\lambda$ for failure, $\mu$ for repair).
- **State probabilities** $P_i(t)$ — probability of being in state $i$ at time $t$.

**Governing equations (Kolmogorov forward equations):**
$$\frac{dP_i}{dt} = \sum_{j \neq i} \left( P_j \lambda_{ji} - P_i \lambda_{ij} \right)$$

For a 2-state working-failed system with constant failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$:

$$R(t) = P_0(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} e^{-(\lambda + \mu)t}$$

**Availability** (steady-state): $A_{ss} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless Property in Engineering Context:** The exponential failure distribution ($R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$) is the **only continuous distribution** that is memoryless. This is why Markov models use exponential holding times.

## 2.7 Redundancy Techniques — Comparative Analysis

| Redundancy Type | Active Spare? | Switching Required? | Reliability Gain | Cost |
|----------------|---------------|---------------------|------------------|------|
| **Element (Active)** | Yes, parallel within the block | No | Moderate | High (all components powered) |
| **Unit (Active)** | Yes, full unit parallel | No (parallel unit on load) | High | Very High |
| **Standby (Passive)** | No, idle until needed | Yes, with switch | Very High (no operating stress) | Moderate (extra switch) |

**Element Redundancy** — Multiple components within one functional block (e.g., 3 diodes in parallel inside a rectifier).
**Unit Redundancy** — Multiple complete functional units in parallel (e.g., 3 pumps operating in parallel).
**Standby Redundancy** — Backup unit(s) switched in only on failure of the operating unit. Requires a **perfect switch** (or imperfect switch with switching reliability $R_{sw}$).

**Reliability formulas (constant failure rate $\lambda$, $n$ identical active units, exponential):**

| Configuration | Reliability $R_s(t)$ |
|--------------|----------------------|
| 1 active unit, no redundancy | $e^{-\lambda t}$ |
| 2 active units in parallel | $2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$ |
| 2 units, 1 standby (perfect switch) | $e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$ |
| $n$ units, 1 standby (perfect switch) | $e^{-\lambda t}\left[1 + \lambda t + \frac{(\lambda t)^2}{2!} + \dots + \frac{(\lambda t)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}\right]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standby Redundancy Rule:** For $k$ standby units with perfect switch, reliability is
> $$R_s = e^{-\lambda t} \sum_{i=0}^{k} \frac{(\lambda t)^i}{i!}$$
> This is the truncated Poisson series — **incomplete gamma function** form.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Series system | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Independent components, all must work |
| 2 | Parallel system | $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | At least one must work |
| 3 | Equal parallel ($n$ identical) | $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$ | Symmetric redundancy |
| 4 | Mixed (hierarchical) | Reduce block-by-block | Bottom-up evaluation |
| 5 | Bridge (conditional) | $R_s = R_j R_{s \vert A} + (1-R_j) R_{s \vert \bar{A}}$ | Total probability on bridge element |
| 6 | $k$-out-of-$n$ system | $R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | Works if at least $k$ of $n$ work |
| 7 | Series unreliability | $Q_s = 1 - \prod R_i$ | De Morgan expansion |
| 8 | Parallel unreliability | $Q_s = \prod (1-R_i)$ | All must fail |
| 9 | System MTTF (series, constant $\lambda_i$) | $\text{MTTF}_s = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i \right]^{-1}$ | $\lambda_i = 1/\text{MTTF}_i$ |
| 10 | Active parallel MTTF | $\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{i}$ | $n$ identical active units |
| 11 | Standby MTTF ($n$ active + 1 standby) | $\text{MTTF}_s = (n+1)/\lambda$ | Perfect switch |
| 12 | Standby $R(t)$, $k$ standbys | $R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \sum_{i=0}^{k} \frac{(\lambda t)^i}{i!}$ | Perfect switch, exponential |
| 13 | Markov steady-state availability | $A_{ss} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | 1 repairable component |
| 14 | Markov transient reliability (working) | $R(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$ | 1 repairable component |
| 15 | Imperfect switch standby | $R_s = R_{sw} e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$ | 1 standby with switch reliability $R_{sw}$ |
| 16 | Redundancy optimization | Maximize $R_s(R_1, \dots, R_n)$ s.t. $\sum c_i R_i^{\alpha} \le C$ | Standard constrained form |

## 2.9 Engineering Utility — Real-World Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic matters in practice (KTU application perspective):**
> - **Aerospace:** Aircraft have triplex or quadruplex redundant flight computers (active parallel) plus standby inertial reference units.
> - **Nuclear power plants:** Multiple redundant coolant pumps (active unit redundancy) with standby diesel generators (standby redundancy).
> - **Data centers:** $N+1$ power supplies and $2N$ UPS configurations (mixed topologies).
> - **Automotive safety:** Brake systems use dual-circuit hydraulics (parallel); airbag controllers use standby sensors.
> - **Telecommunications:** Fiber ring networks form complex lattice topologies; Markov models characterize call-handoff reliability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Series System Reliability from Probability Axioms

**Given:** A series system with $n$ independent components. Let $S_i$ = event "component $i$ works" with $P(S_i) = R_i$.

**System works** iff all $S_i$ occur simultaneously:
$$R_s = P(S_1 \cap S_2 \cap \cdots \cap S_n)$$

By **independence axiom**:
$$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(S_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**For unreliability** (system fails iff at least one fails):
$$Q_s = P(\bar{S_1} \cup \bar{S_2} \cup \cdots \cup \bar{S_n})$$

Apply **De Morgan's Law**:
$$Q_s = 1 - P(S_1 \cap S_2 \cap \cdots \cap S_n) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**QED.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Parallel System Reliability

System fails iff **all** components fail (intersection of failure events):
$$Q_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$

System works iff it does **not** fail:
$$R_s = 1 - Q_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$

**Special case — $n$ identical components** with reliability $R$ each:
$$R_s = 1 - (1 - R)^n$$

Expand for $n=2$:
$$R_s = 1 - (1 - R)(1 - R) = 1 - (1 - 2R + R^2) = 2R - R^2$$

Expand for $n=3$:
$$R_s = 1 - (1-R)^3 = 3R - 3R^2 + R^3$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bridge Configuration — Conditional Reliability Method

Consider the classic **bridge network** with 5 components arranged as a square with diagonal. Label them $1, 2, 3, 4$ (square perimeter) and $5$ (diagonal). Source at node $A$, sink at node $B$.

**Step 1: Condition on component 5 (the bridge element).**

**Case A — Component 5 works** (probability $R_5$):
- Nodes $A$ and $B$ are connected through the diagonal as a short.
- The reduced network: $A$ connects to $B$ via two parallel paths:
  - Path 1: $1 \to 5 \to 3$ (effectively series 1-3 with 5 shorted)
  - Path 2: $2 \to 5 \to 4$ (effectively series 2-4 with 5 shorted)
- Wait — with 5 working, we have paths: $1-3$ in parallel with $2-4$.
- $R_{s \vert A} = [1 - (1-R_1 R_3)(1 - R_2 R_4)]$

**Case B — Component 5 fails** (probability $1 - R_5$):
- Reduced network: $1$ in series with $2$ in parallel with $3$ in series with $4$? No — $1$ and $2$ are in series from $A$ to the bridge, $3$ and $4$ in series from bridge to $B$, and these two series chains are in parallel.
- $R_{s \vert \bar{A}} = (R_1 R_2) + (R_3 R_4) - (R_1 R_2)(R_3 R_4)$

**Step 2: Combine using Total Probability.**
$$R_s = R_5 \left[ 1 - (1-R_1 R_3)(1-R_2 R_4) \right] + (1-R_5)\left[ R_1 R_2 + R_3 R_4 - R_1 R_2 R_3 R_4 \right]$$

**For identical components** ($R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_5 = R$):
$$R_s = R[1-(1-R^2)^2] + (1-R)[2R^2 - R^4]$$

Expand step by step:
$$1-(1-R^2)^2 = 1-(1 - 2R^2 + R^4) = 2R^2 - R^4$$

$$R(2R^2 - R^4) = 2R^3 - R^5$$

$$2R^2 - R^4$$

$$(1-R)(2R^2 - R^4) = 2R^2 - R^4 - 2R^3 + R^5$$

Sum:
$$R_s = (2R^3 - R^5) + (2R^2 - R^4 - 2R^3 + R^5) = 2R^2 - R^4$$

**Remarkable result:** For identical bridge elements, $R_s = 2R^2 - R^4$. This is the reliability of a single parallel pair $(1-3) \parallel (2-4)$ — which shows that for **identical** components, the bridge is equivalent to a simple parallel-series network!

## 3.4 Derivation 4: $k$-out-of-$n$ System

**Definition:** System works iff at least $k$ of the $n$ components work. This is a generalization of:
- $k=1$: Parallel system
- $k=n$: Series system

**Binomial expansion:**
$$R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$$

where we sum over all states where $i$ components survive ($i \ge k$).

**Example:** 3-out-of-4 system (works if at least 3 of 4 components work).
$$R_s = \binom{4}{3} R^3 (1-R) + \binom{4}{4} R^4 = 4R^3(1-R) + R^4 = 4R^3 - 4R^4 + R^4 = 4R^3 - 3R^4$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Standby Redundancy with Perfect Switch

**Setup:** $n$ identical active components plus 1 standby (idle, no failure during standby). On failure of an active unit, the standby is switched in (instantaneously, perfectly).

**Failure occurs** only when **all** $n+1$ components have failed. The system requires exactly $n+1$ failures, but starts with $n$ active and gains standbys one at a time. Equivalent formulation: a "sum of $n+1$ independent exponential times to failure."

Let $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_{n+1}$ be i.i.d. exponential ($\lambda$) lifetimes. System fails when the sum exceeds operating time $t$:
$$R_s(t) = P(T_1 + T_2 + \cdots + T_{n+1} > t)$$

The sum of $(n+1)$ i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$) random variables is **Erlang-$(n+1)$** distributed. The survival function is:
$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \sum_{i=0}^{n} \frac{(\lambda t)^i}{i!}$$

**Proof by induction on $n$:**
- Base ($n=0$, just 1 active): $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}$ ✓
- Inductive step: Add one standby. The system fails if either:
  - The original $(n+1)$-system fails before the new standby is needed, OR
  - The new standby is needed but the $(n+2)$-system fails after its addition.
- Recursion: $R_{n+1}(t) = R_n(t) + \int_0^t f_n(\tau) R_1(t-\tau) d\tau$ where $f_n$ is the Erlang density.
- This yields the Poisson sum above.

**For 1 active + 1 standby:**
$$R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\left[1 + \lambda t\right]$$

**MTTF for $n+1$ units (1 standby):**
$$\text{MTTF}_s = \int_0^\infty R_s(t)\, dt = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \frac{(\lambda t)^i}{i!} dt$$

Using $\int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} t^i dt = \dfrac{i!}{\lambda^{i+1}}$:
$$\text{MTTF}_s = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{n+1}{\lambda}$$

**Key result:** $\text{MTTF}_{standby} = (n+1)/\lambda$ vs. $\text{MTTF}_{active\,parallel} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \dfrac{1}{i}$

Standby is always superior: $(n+1)/\lambda > \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \dfrac{1}{i}$ for $n \ge 1$.

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Markov Model for One Repairable Component

**States:** $S_0$ = working, $S_1$ = failed.
**Transitions:** $S_0 \xrightarrow{\lambda} S_1$ (failure), $S_1 \xrightarrow{\mu} S_0$ (repair).

**Kolmogorov equations:**
$$\frac{dP_0}{dt} = -\lambda P_0 + \mu P_1$$
$$\frac{dP_1}{dt} = \lambda P_0 - \mu P_1$$

with $P_0 + P_1 = 1$ and initial conditions $P_0(0)=1, P_1(0)=0$.

**Solve:** From the first equation, $P_1 = 1 - P_0$, so:
$$\frac{dP_0}{dt} = -\lambda P_0 + \mu (1 - P_0) = \mu - (\lambda + \mu) P_0$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. Integrating factor: $e^{(\lambda+\mu)t}$.

Solution:
$$P_0(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} e^{-(\lambda + \mu)t}$$

**Steady-state availability** ($t \to \infty$):
$$A_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} P_0(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Reliability** (no repair allowed, $\mu = 0$):
$$R(t) = P_0(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ ✓

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Two-Component Parallel System with Markov Analysis

**States:** $S_2$ = both work, $S_1$ = one works, $S_0$ = both failed.
**System works** in states $S_2$ and $S_1$.

Failure rates from $S_2$: $\lambda_2 = 2\lambda$ (one of two components can fail).
Repair rate from $S_1$ to $S_2$: $\mu$.
Failure rate from $S_1$ to $S_0$: $\lambda$ (the remaining component can fail).
Repair rate from $S_0$ to $S_1$: $2\mu$ (one of two failed components can be repaired).

**Steady-state probabilities:**
By solving $\pi Q = 0$ with $\sum \pi_i = 1$:
$$\pi_2 = \frac{\mu^2}{(\lambda + \mu)^2}, \quad \pi_1 = \frac{2\lambda\mu}{(\lambda + \mu)^2}, \quad \pi_0 = \frac{\lambda^2}{(\lambda + \mu)^2}$$

**Steady-state availability** (system works iff at least one component works):
$$A_{ss} = \pi_2 + \pi_1 = 1 - \pi_0 = 1 - \frac{\lambda^2}{(\lambda + \mu)^2} = \frac{2\lambda\mu + \mu^2}{(\lambda + \mu)^2}$$

## 3.8 Derivation 8: Redundancy Optimization Problem

**Standard Form (KTU Exam Pattern):**
$$\text{Maximize } R_s = \prod_{j=1}^{n} \left[ 1 - (1-R_j)^{k_j} \right]$$
**Subject to:**
$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j(R_j) \le C \quad \text{(cost constraint)}$$
$$g_j^L \le R_j \le g_j^U \quad \text{(feasibility bounds)}$$

**Where:** $k_j$ = number of parallel elements in subsystem $j$, $c_j$ = cost function (often $c_j R_j^{\alpha}$ for some $\alpha \in (0,1)$).

**Solution Methods (KTU Level):**
1. **Lagrangian Multipliers** — Convert to unconstrained problem.
2. **Geometric Programming** — When cost is a posynomial and reliability constraint is a posynomial.
3. **Dynamic Programming** — For sequential allocation across stages.
4. **Heuristics (Greedy / Branch-and-Bound)** — For large combinatorial problems.

**Simple Analytical Result:** For identical components with linear cost $c$ per unit and total budget $C$, the optimal number of redundant units is approximately:
$$n^* \approx \frac{\ln(C/c)}{\ln(\text{reliability gain factor})}$$

This is a **first-order approximation** used in KTU exam problems for the $n$-out-of-$n$ parallel case.

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Numerical Insight

Let $R = 0.9$ (single component reliability), $t = 100$ hours, $\lambda = 0.001$/hr, $\mu = 0.1$/hr.

| Configuration | $R_s$ (t=100h) | MTTF (hours) | $\Delta$ over baseline |
|---------------|----------------|--------------|----------------------|
| 1 component (baseline) | $e^{-0.1} = 0.9048$ | 1000 | 0% |
| 2 series | $0.9048^2 = 0.8187$ | 500 | $-9.5\%$ |
| 2 parallel (active) | $2(0.9048) - 0.9048^2 = 0.9903$ | 1500 | $+9.5\%$ |
| 1 active + 1 standby | $e^{-0.1}(1+0.1) = 0.9953$ | 2000 | $+10.0\%$ |
| 1 active + 2 standby | $e^{-0.1}(1+0.1+0.005) = 0.9990$ | 3000 | $+10.4\%$ |
| 2 active + 1 standby (k-of-3) | $3(0.9048)^2 - 2(0.9048)^3 = 0.9990$ | 1833 | $+10.4\%$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Standby redundancy outperforms active redundancy at equal component count, but requires a perfect switch and assumes zero failure rate in the idle state (often violated in practice).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Series System Reliability Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    sourceA[Source] --> comp1[Component 1]
    comp1 --> comp2[Component 2]
    comp2 --> comp3[Component 3]
    comp3 --> compN[Component n]
    compN --> sinkB[Sink Output]

    style sinkB fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style comp1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style comp2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style comp3 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style compN fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
```

**Interpretation:** Signal must pass through **every** block sequentially. Any single failure breaks the path.

## 4.2 Parallel System Reliability Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    sourceA[Source] --> split{Split}
    split --> comp1[Component 1]
    split --> comp2[Component 2]
    split --> comp3[Component 3]
    comp1 --> merge[Merge]
    comp2 --> merge
    comp3 --> merge
    merge --> sinkB[Sink Output]
    style sourceA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style sinkB fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style split fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
    style merge fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
    style comp1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style comp2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style comp3 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
```

**Interpretation:** Multiple parallel paths from source to sink. As long as **one** path is intact, signal flows.

## 4.3 Mixed (Series-Parallel) System

```mermaid
graph LR
    sourceA[Source] --> blockA[Series Block A]
    blockA --> split{Split}
    split --> branch1[Parallel Branch 1]
    split --> branch2[Parallel Branch 2]
    branch1 --> blockB[Series Block B]
    branch2 --> blockB
    blockB --> sinkC[Sink Output]
    style sourceA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style sinkC fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style blockA fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style blockB fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style branch1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style branch2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style split fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
```

**Interpretation:** Series-parallel hierarchy — Block A → Parallel Branches → Block B in sequence.

## 4.4 Bridge (Complex) System — Lattice Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    sourceA[Node A] --> c1[Component 1]
    sourceA --> c2[Component 2]
    c1 --> nodeT[Node C]
    c2 --> nodeT
    nodeT --> c3[Component 3]
    nodeT --> c4[Component 4]
    c3 --> sinkB[Node B]
    c4 --> sinkB
    nodeT -. Bridge 5 .-> sinkB
    style sourceA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style sinkB fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style nodeT fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
    style c1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style c2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style c3 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style c4 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style c5 fill:#ffccff,stroke:#993399
```

**Interpretation:** Classic bridge — components 1, 2 feed node C; 3, 4 carry to B; component 5 (dotted) is the bridge element connecting C directly to B.

## 4.5 Markov State Diagram — One Repairable Component

```mermaid
graph LR
    state0[State 0: Working] -- lambda --> state1[State 1: Failed]
    state1 -- mu --> state0
    style state0 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style state1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
```

**Interpretation:** Two-state Markov chain. $\lambda$ = failure rate, $\mu$ = repair rate.

## 4.6 Markov State Diagram — Two Identical Components with Repair

```mermaid
graph LR
    state2[State 2: Both Working] -- 2 lambda --> state1[State 1: One Working]
    state1 -- lambda --> state0[State 0: Both Failed]
    state0 -- 2 mu --> state1
    state1 -- mu --> state2
    style state2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style state1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style state0 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
```

**Interpretation:** Three-state chain. $2\lambda$ = one of two can fail; $2\mu$ = one of two can be repaired.

## 4.7 Standby Redundancy Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    sourceA[Source] --> active[Active Unit]
    active --> switch{Switch Perfect}
    switch --> sinkB[Sink Output]
    standby[Standby Unit Idle] -. Activated on failure .-> switch
    style sourceA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style sinkB fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style active fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style standby fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style switch fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
```

**Interpretation:** Active unit is on load. On failure, switch routes standby to output (assumed instantaneous and perfect).

## 4.8 Fault Tree — Two Parallel Pumps in Series

```mermaid
graph TD
    toptop[Top Event: System Failure] --> andgate1{Gate AND}
    andgate1 --> orsub1[Subsystem 1 Fails]
    andgate1 --> orsub2[Subsystem 2 Fails]
    orsub1 --> orgate1{Gate OR}
    orsub2 --> orgate2{Gate OR}
    orgate1 --> pump1[Pump 1 Fails]
    orgate1 --> pump2[Pump 2 Fails]
    orgate2 --> valve1[Valve 1 Fails]
    orgate2 --> valve2[Valve 2 Fails]
    style toptop fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style andgate1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style orgate1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style orgate2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style pump1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style pump2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style valve1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style valve2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style orsub1 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
    style orsub2 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
```

**Interpretation:** System fails only if **both** subsystem 1 AND subsystem 2 fail. Each subsystem fails if **either** of its two components fails (parallel sub-units in series with each other).

## 4.9 Redundancy Allocation Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[System Design Requirement] --> q1{System mission critical?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Continuous operation needed?}
    q1 -- No --> end1[Single Component Sufficient]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Cost of failure extreme?}
    q2 -- No --> activeP[Active Parallel Redundancy]
    q3 -- Yes --> standby[Standby with Perfect Switch]
    q3 -- No --> unitP[Unit Level Redundancy]
    activeP --> opt1[Optimize n via cost-reliability trade-off]
    standby --> opt2[Optimize n and switch reliability]
    unitP --> opt3[Optimize allocation per subsystem]
    opt1 --> end2[Final Design]
    opt2 --> end2
    opt3 --> end2
    end1 --> end2
    style start fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style end1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style end2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style activeP fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style standby fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style unitP fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
```

**Interpretation:** Decision-tree for selecting redundancy strategy based on mission criticality and operating constraints.
