# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Series vs. Parallel Configuration (Conceptual) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Distinguish between series and parallel configurations in reliability engineering. Why is a series system considered a "weakest-link" topology?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Series System | Parallel System |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| Operating rule | All components must work | At least one component must work |
| System reliability | $R_s = \prod R_i$ (product) | $R_s = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$ |
| Failure rule | Any single failure → system failure | All components must fail → system failure |
| Reliability trend on adding components | Decreases | Increases |
| Unreliability trend on adding components | Increases | Decreases |

**Why "Weakest-Link" Topology:** For a series system, $R_s = \prod R_i \le R_{\min}$ (the smallest component reliability). This is because all $R_i \le 1$, so multiplying them can only decrease or maintain the minimum. Thus the weakest component dictates the entire system's reliability — analogous to a chain breaking at its weakest link. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2: Active vs. Standby Redundancy `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Compare active (unit) redundancy and standby redundancy. State the conditions under which standby redundancy is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Active Redundancy | Standby Redundancy |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|
| Operating state of spare | Powered and operating | Idle until needed |
| Failure rate of spare | Full $\lambda$ | ~ 0 (assumed no wear) |
| Switching | Not required | Required (must detect failure) |
| MTTF ($n$ active + 1 standby) | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{n+1} \dfrac{1}{i}$ | $\dfrac{n+1}{\lambda}$ |
| Reliability at $t$ | $1 - (1-e^{-\lambda t})^{n+1}$ | $e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{i=0}^{1} \frac{(\lambda t)^i}{i!}$ |
| Cost | Higher (operating power) | Moderate (extra switch) |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher (switch + detector) |

**Conditions to Prefer Standby:**
1. Spare components suffer **wear-out** during operation (e.g., mechanical fatigue, thermal cycling).
2. Mission duration is **long** (idle spares don't accumulate damage).
3. The **switch is highly reliable** ($R_{sw} \to 1$) — otherwise active redundancy may be safer.
4. Power consumption of active spares is a concern (e.g., battery-operated systems). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — Mixed Configuration + Redundancy Optimization `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A system consists of three subsystems arranged in series. Subsystem 1 has 2 components in parallel, Subsystem 2 has 3 components in parallel, and Subsystem 3 has 2 components in parallel. Each component has reliability $R = 0.95$ and cost $C = ₹100$. The total budget is $₹1500$. Find the **maximum system reliability** achievable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with derivation how the reliability of a 1-out-of-2 system compares with that of a 2-out-of-2 standby system. Use $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ as the basic component reliability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) — Mixed Configuration with Cost Constraint**

**Step 1: Compute reliability per subsystem with equal parallel elements.**

Subsystem 1 (2 parallel, both at 0.95):
$$R_1 = 1 - (1-0.95)^2 = 1 - (0.05)^2 = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975$$

Subsystem 2 (3 parallel, all at 0.95):
$$R_2 = 1 - (1-0.95)^3 = 1 - (0.05)^3 = 1 - 0.000125 = 0.999875$$

Subsystem 3 (2 parallel, both at 0.95):
$$R_3 = 1 - (1-0.95)^2 = 0.9975$$

**Step 2: System reliability (series combination).**
$$R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 = 0.9975 \times 0.999875 \times 0.9975$$

Compute step by step:
$$0.9975 \times 0.9975 = 0.99500625$$
$$0.99500625 \times 0.999875 = 0.994882...$$

$$R_s \approx 0.9949$$

**Step 3: Cost check.**
Total components: $2 + 3 + 2 = 7$ components $\times$ ₹100 = ₹700, which is well within the ₹1500 budget. Therefore, the original configuration is feasible.

**Step 4: Optimization — can we add more parallel elements within budget?**

Remaining budget: $1500 - 700 = ₹800$, can afford **8 more components** at ₹100 each.

The optimal strategy is to add parallel elements to the **weakest subsystem** (those with the lowest $R_j$). Currently, $R_1 = R_3 = 0.9975$ (weakest) and $R_2 = 0.999875$ (strongest).

Adding 1 more parallel element to Subsystem 1:
$$R_1' = 1 - (0.05)^3 = 0.999875$$
This requires +1 component, +₹100.

Adding 1 more to Subsystem 3:
$$R_3' = 1 - (0.05)^3 = 0.999875$$
Cost: +₹100.

New $R_s = 0.999875 \times 0.999875 \times 0.999875 = (0.999875)^3 \approx 0.99963$.

**Optimal answer:** $R_s^{\max} \approx 0.9996$ with 9 components (₹900 total cost). **(7 marks — marks distributed: 3 for reliability calcs, 2 for series combination, 2 for cost optimization)**

**(b) — 1-out-of-2 Active vs. 2-out-of-2 Standby**

**Setup:** Two units, each with reliability $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Configuration 1: 1-out-of-2 Active Parallel** (works if at least 1 of 2 works)
$$R_{1/2}(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2$$
$$= 1 - (1 - 2e^{-\lambda t} + e^{-2\lambda t})$$
$$= 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$$

**Configuration 2: 2-out-of-2 Standby** (1 active + 1 standby, both must work; system fails only after both have failed)

For the standby model, system fails iff the active fails AND the standby (now active) fails. The time to failure of the system is the sum of two exponential($\lambda$) lifetimes. This sum is Erlang-2 distributed:
$$R_{2/2,standby}(t) = P(T_1 + T_2 > t) = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$$

**Derivation of standby formula:**
$$P(T_1 + T_2 > t) = \int_0^\infty f_{T_1}(\tau) P(T_2 > t - \tau) d\tau = \int_0^t \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau} e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)} d\tau + \int_t^\infty \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau} d\tau$$

For the second term: $P(T_1 > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

For the first term:
$$\int_0^t \lambda e^{-\lambda \tau} e^{-\lambda(t-\tau)} d\tau = \int_0^t \lambda e^{-\lambda t} d\tau = \lambda t e^{-\lambda t}$$

Total: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} + \lambda t e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$ ✓

**Comparison:**

For small $\lambda t$ (short mission), expand using Taylor series $e^{-\lambda t} \approx 1 - \lambda t + \frac{(\lambda t)^2}{2}$:

Active: $R_{1/2} = 2(1 - \lambda t + \frac{(\lambda t)^2}{2}) - (1 - 2\lambda t + 2(\lambda t)^2) - \dots$
$\approx 1 - (\lambda t)^2 + O((\lambda t)^3)$

Standby: $R_{2/2,standby} = (1 - \lambda t + \frac{(\lambda t)^2}{2})(1 + \lambda t)$
$= 1 - \lambda t + \frac{(\lambda t)^2}{2} + \lambda t - (\lambda t)^2 + \frac{(\lambda t)^3}{2}$
$= 1 - \frac{(\lambda t)^2}{2} + \frac{(\lambda t)^3}{2} \approx 1 - \frac{(\lambda t)^2}{2}$

**Conclusion:** For identical components with exponential lifetime, **standby redundancy provides higher reliability** than active parallel for all $t > 0$ (with perfect switch). Numerically at $\lambda t = 0.5$:

- $R_{1/2} = 2 e^{-0.5} - e^{-1} = 2(0.6065) - 0.3679 = 0.8452$
- $R_{2/2,standby} = e^{-0.5}(1 + 0.5) = 0.6065 \times 1.5 = 0.9098$

Standby is ~7.5% more reliable. **(7 marks — marks distributed: 2 for active formula, 3 for standby derivation, 2 for comparison)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Markov Model + Bridge Configuration `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A repairable system has two identical components, each with failure rate $\lambda = 0.01$/hr and repair rate $\mu = 0.5$/hr. Components operate in **active parallel**. Draw the **Markov state diagram** and compute the **steady-state availability** of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bridge system has 5 identical components each with reliability $R = 0.85$. Using the **conditional reliability method**, derive the system reliability. Verify whether the bridge configuration offers any improvement over a simple series of 3 or a simple parallel of 2. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) — Markov Model for Two Repairable Components in Active Parallel**

**Step 1: Define states.**
- $S_2$: Both components working (system works)
- $S_1$: One component working, one failed (system works — parallel)
- $S_0$: Both components failed (system fails)

**Step 2: Draw transitions and rates.**
- $S_2 \to S_1$ at rate $2\lambda$ (one of the two can fail)
- $S_1 \to S_0$ at rate $\lambda$ (the remaining one can fail)
- $S_1 \to S_2$ at rate $\mu$ (failed one gets repaired)
- $S_0 \to S_1$ at rate $2\mu$ (one of two failed ones gets repaired)

**Step 3: Steady-state balance equations.**

Let $\pi_i$ be the steady-state probability of state $i$. Inflow = Outflow for each state.

For $S_2$: $\pi_2 \cdot 2\lambda = \pi_1 \cdot \mu$
For $S_1$: $\pi_1 (\lambda + \mu) = \pi_2 \cdot 2\lambda + \pi_0 \cdot 2\mu$
For $S_0$: $\pi_0 \cdot 2\mu = \pi_1 \cdot \lambda$

Normalization: $\pi_0 + \pi_1 + \pi_2 = 1$

**Step 4: Solve.**

From $S_2$ equation: $\pi_1 = \dfrac{2\lambda}{\mu} \pi_2$

From $S_0$ equation: $\pi_0 = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\mu} \pi_1 = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\mu} \cdot \dfrac{2\lambda}{\mu} \pi_2 = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{\mu^2} \pi_2$

Substitute into normalization:
$$\pi_2 \left[ 1 + \frac{2\lambda}{\mu} + \frac{\lambda^2}{\mu^2} \right] = 1$$
$$\pi_2 \left[ \frac{\mu^2 + 2\lambda\mu + \lambda^2}{\mu^2} \right] = 1$$
$$\pi_2 \cdot \frac{(\mu + \lambda)^2}{\mu^2} = 1$$
$$\pi_2 = \frac{\mu^2}{(\lambda + \mu)^2}$$

Similarly:
$$\pi_1 = \frac{2\lambda\mu}{(\lambda + \mu)^2}, \quad \pi_0 = \frac{\lambda^2}{(\lambda + \mu)^2}$$

**Step 5: Steady-state availability** (system works in $S_2$ and $S_1$):
$$A_{ss} = \pi_2 + \pi_1 = 1 - \pi_0 = 1 - \frac{\lambda^2}{(\lambda + \mu)^2}$$

**Step 6: Numerical evaluation** with $\lambda = 0.01$, $\mu = 0.5$:

$$A_{ss} = 1 - \frac{(0.01)^2}{(0.51)^2} = 1 - \frac{0.0001}{0.2601} = 1 - 0.000384 = 0.999616$$

$$\boxed{A_{ss} \approx 0.9996 \text{ or } 99.96\%}$$ **(7 marks — marks distributed: 2 for diagram/rates, 3 for balance equations, 2 for numerical availability)**

**(b) — Bridge System with Conditional Method**

**Topology:** 5 components in a bridge — 4 on the perimeter and 1 diagonal (the "bridge" element).

**Step 1: Condition on the bridge element (component 5).**

Let $A$ = "component 5 works" with $P(A) = R = 0.85$.
Let $\bar{A}$ = "component 5 fails" with $P(\bar{A}) = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$.

**Case A — Component 5 works:**

The bridge is "shorted" — node C (center) connects directly to output B. The reduced network is two parallel paths from input A to output B:
- Path 1: components 1 and 3 in series (1 → C → B via short)
- Path 2: components 2 and 4 in series (2 → C → B via short)

These two paths are in parallel:
$$R_{s \vert A} = 1 - (1 - R_1 R_3)(1 - R_2 R_4)$$

For identical $R_i = 0.85$:
$$R_1 R_3 = R^2 = 0.85^2 = 0.7225$$
$$R_2 R_4 = R^2 = 0.7225$$
$$R_{s \vert A} = 1 - (1 - 0.7225)^2 = 1 - (0.2775)^2 = 1 - 0.077006 = 0.922994$$

**Case B — Component 5 fails:**

The diagonal is open. The reduced network has two parallel branches from input to output:
- Branch 1: components 1 and 2 in series (input → top side → middle, but with 5 gone, we need 1 and 2 in series from A to C)
- Wait, careful: with 5 failed, current must flow through the perimeter. There are 2 series paths:
  - Path via 1 → 3 (top side, then down right side)
  - Path via 2 → 4 (left side down, then bottom)
- These two paths are in parallel.

$$R_{s \vert \bar{A}} = 1 - (1 - R_1 R_3)(1 - R_2 R_4) = 0.922994 \text{ (same as Case A)}$$

**Step 2: Total Probability.**

$$R_s = P(A) R_{s \vert A} + P(\bar{A}) R_{s \vert \bar{A}}$$
$$R_s = 0.85 \times 0.922994 + 0.15 \times 0.922994$$
$$R_s = (0.85 + 0.15) \times 0.922994 = 1.0 \times 0.922994$$
$$R_s = 0.922994$$

**Remarkable observation:** For identical components, the bridge simplifies to $R_s = R_1 R_3 + R_2 R_4 - R_1 R_2 R_3 R_4 = 2R^2 - R^4$. The bridge element's reliability does **not** matter! This is a known result for the symmetric bridge.

Numerically: $R_s = 2(0.85)^2 - (0.85)^4 = 2(0.7225) - (0.52200625) = 1.445 - 0.5220 = 0.923$ ✓

**Step 3: Comparison with simple configurations.**

- **Series of 3:** $R_{series,3} = (0.85)^3 = 0.6141$
- **Parallel of 2:** $R_{parallel,2} = 1 - (0.15)^2 = 1 - 0.0225 = 0.9775$
- **Bridge:** $R_{bridge} = 0.9230$

**Interpretation:**
- The bridge (0.923) is **far superior** to a 3-series (0.614) — by 50% relative.
- The bridge (0.923) is **slightly inferior** to a 2-parallel (0.978) — but the bridge uses 5 components vs. 2 for the parallel, so it's not a fair cost comparison.

For a fair comparison with **same number of components (5)**, the bridge topology is more reliable than any 5-element series combination and is a reasonable alternative to 5-element parallel arrangements (which would have $R = 1 - 0.15^5 = 0.9999$, but at much higher cost). **(7 marks — marks distributed: 3 for conditional setup, 2 for case-by-case reduction, 2 for comparison)**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module**
>
> 1. **Confusing RBD and Fault Tree orientations** — Students often use OR gates where AND is needed (or vice versa). **Memory aid:** AND gate → all events required → corresponds to PARALLEL branches in RBD; OR gate → any event triggers → corresponds to SERIES elements in RBD.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the independence assumption** — Series/parallel formulas $R_s = \prod R_i$ or $R_s = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$ are valid **only** for independent components. KTU questions sometimes embed a dependent scenario disguised in the problem statement.
>
> 3. **Bridge configuration — wrong case reduction** — When the bridge element fails, the network is **not** a simple series or parallel — it is two series chains **in parallel**. Mistaking this for a series chain gives wrong answers.
>
> 4. **Markov steady-state vs. transient** — Many students confuse $A_{ss} = \mu/(\lambda + \mu)$ (steady-state) with $R(t)$ (reliability, no repair). Make sure to use the right one for the question asked.
>
> 5. **Standby redundancy — forgetting the summation** — $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}\sum_{i=0}^{k} \frac{(\lambda t)^i}{i!}$ — students often write only the first 2 terms. For $k$ standbys, the sum goes up to $i = k$, not $i = 1$.
>
> 6. **Units and decimal precision** — In numerical problems, KTU expects at least 4 decimal places for reliabilities and proper units for $\lambda$ and $\mu$ (per hour, per year, etc.).
>
> 7. **Optimization — not stating assumptions** — When solving redundancy allocation, students forget to state which cost model is assumed (linear, polynomial, geometric). Always state assumptions explicitly.
>
> 8. **Repairable vs. non-repairable** — Markov models **with** $\mu > 0$ describe repairable systems (availability). Markov models **with** $\mu = 0$ describe non-repairable systems (reliability). Mixing these up leads to wrong answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3**

### Core Formulas to Memorize
- $\square$ **Series:** $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$
- $\square$ **Parallel:** $R_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1-R_i)$
- $\square$ **Equal parallel:** $R_s = 1 - (1-R)^n$
- $\square$ **$k$-out-of-$n$:** $R_s = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$
- $\square$ **Bridge (identical):** $R_s = 2R^2 - R^4$
- $\square$ **Standby (1 standby):** $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$
- $\square$ **Standby ($k$ standbys):** $R_s = e^{-\lambda t} \sum_{i=0}^{k} \frac{(\lambda t)^i}{i!}$
- $\square$ **MTTF series:** $\text{MTTF}_s^{-1} = \sum \lambda_i$
- $\square$ **MTTF active parallel ($n$ identical):** $\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda} H_n$ (harmonic number)
- $\square$ **MTTF standby ($n+1$ units, 1 standby):** $\text{MTTF}_s = (n+1)/\lambda$
- $\square$ **Steady-state availability (1 component):** $A_{ss} = \mu/(\lambda + \mu)$
- $\square$ **Steady-state availability (2 active parallel):** $A_{ss} = 1 - \lambda^2/(\lambda + \mu)^2$

### Key Concepts to Recall
- $\square$ Series system = weakest link; parallel = strongest link.
- $\square$ RBD uses **series** for "all must work" and **parallel** for "at least one must work."
- $\square$ Fault Tree is **dual** of RBD: AND ↔ Parallel, OR ↔ Series.
- $\square$ Conditional reliability method works for **any** complex system by conditioning on a single element.
- $\square$ Markov models require the **memoryless** (exponential) assumption.
- $\square$ **Steady-state** solutions need $\pi Q = 0$ with $\sum \pi_i = 1$.
- $\square$ **Transient** solutions need $dP/dt = P \cdot Q$ with initial conditions.
- $\square$ Standby redundancy requires **perfect switch** for maximum benefit; imperfect switch reduces $R_s$.
- $\square$ Standby MTTF > Active Parallel MTTF (always, for $n \ge 1$).
- $\square$ Optimization is a **constrained** non-linear programming problem — typically solved by Lagrangian, GP, or DP.
- $\square$ Repairable systems use **availability**; non-repairable use **reliability**.

### Exam Pattern Recognition Hints
- $\square$ "At least one must work" → **Parallel** configuration.
- $\square$ "All must work" → **Series** configuration.
- $\square$ "k of n" → $k$-out-of-$n$ system (binomial expansion).
- $\square$ "Bridge / lattice / non-series-parallel" → **Conditional** method.
- $\square$ "Failure rate constant, repairable" → **Markov** chain (states + rates).
- $\square$ "Maximum reliability for given cost" → **Optimization** (redundancy allocation).
- $\square$ "Backup unit activated on failure" → **Standby** redundancy.
- $\square$ "Two components working together, switch on failure" → Standby with $R_s = e^{-\lambda t}(1 + \lambda t)$.

### Common Engineering Constants (Kerala Context)
- $\square$ Typical $\lambda$ for industrial electronics: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$/hr.
- $\square$ Typical $\mu$ for repairable equipment: $10^{-1}$ to $10^0$/hr (MTTR of 1–10 hours).
- $\square$ Mission times: 100 hr, 1000 hr, 1 year (8760 hr) are common in KTU problems.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Reliability Engineering (PESFT525)**

topics.title: Reliability-Cost Trade-Off, Introduction to Repairable Systems, Instantaneous Repair Rate, MTTR, Reliability and Availability Functions, Important Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT525 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reliability-Cost Trade-Off, Introduction to Repairable Systems, Instantaneous Repair Rate, MTTR, Reliability and Availability Functions, Important Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Reliability-Cost Trade-Off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The **Reliability-Cost Trade-Off** is the engineering decision-making framework that seeks the *optimal balance* between the **cost of achieving higher reliability** (design redundancy, premium components, robust testing) and the **cost of unreliability** (downtime losses, warranty claims, safety hazards, customer dissatisfaction). The optimum occurs where the *marginal cost of reliability improvement* equals the *marginal cost saving from reduced failures*.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine you own a Maruti car. A normal tyre lasts ~30,000 km and costs ₹3,500. A premium Michelin tyre lasts ~70,000 km but costs ₹9,000. Is the premium tyre "worth it"? If you drive on rough Kerala roads and have high puncture-repair costs (₹500 each), maybe yes. If you drive rarely, no. The reliability engineer makes *exactly this kind of decision* for entire systems — weighing manufacturing cost against the cost of failures across the product's life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Reliability Cost:**
> 1. **Cost of Achieving Reliability (C_r):** Increases monotonically (often exponentially) with target reliability.
> 2. **Cost of Unreliability (C_u):** Decreases monotonically as reliability increases.
> 3. **Total Life-Cycle Cost (C_T):** $C_T = C_r + C_u$ → minimized at the optimum point.

---

## 1.2 Introduction to Repairable Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A **Repairable System** is an engineered system which, upon failure, can be **restored to a fully operational state** through corrective maintenance actions (repair or replacement of components), rather than being discarded. Such systems are characterized by **alternating cycles of operation and repair**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Your household ceiling fan: when it fails, you call a mechanic, replace a capacitor (₹200), and it runs again. It is **repairable**. In contrast, a cheap incandescent bulb that simply burns out and is thrown away is **non-repairable**. Industrial machinery, aircraft engines, computer servers, and even smartphones are all *repairable systems* in engineering analysis.

**Key Distinction — Non-Repairable vs Repairable:**

| Aspect | Non-Repairable System | Repairable System |
|---|---|---|
| After failure | Discarded | Restored to function |
| Performance measure | Reliability $R(t)$ | Availability $A(t)$ |
| Key metric | MTTF | MTBF (= MTTF + MTTR) |
| Lifetime | Single mission | Multiple renewal cycles |
| Example | Light bulb, missile | Pump, server, aircraft engine |

---

## 1.3 Instantaneous Repair Rate — $\mu(t)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The **Instantaneous Repair Rate** $\mu(t)$ is defined as the *rate at which repairs are completed per unit time*, conditional on the system **not yet having been repaired** at time $t$. Mathematically,
> $$\mu(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \le t+\Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f_R(t)}{1 - F_R(t)} = \frac{f_R(t)}{R_R(t)}$$
> where $T$ is the time-to-repair random variable, $f_R(t)$ its probability density function (PDF), and $R_R(t) = 1 - F_R(t)$ is the *reliability of the repair process*.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of $\mu(t)$ as the **"urgency of getting the system back online."** A well-stocked service center with trained technicians has a *high* $\mu$ (fast repairs). A rural location with no spare parts has a *low* $\mu$ (slow repairs). $\mu$ is the **mirror image of failure rate $\lambda$** — but applies to the *repair process* instead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Units:** *If $t$ is in hours*, $\mu(t)$ is in **per hour (h⁻¹)**. If $t$ is in years, $\mu$ is in **per year (yr⁻¹)**. The reciprocal $1/\mu$ is the **MTTR** for constant repair rate.

---

## 1.4 Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** **MTTR** is the *expected (average) time required to restore a failed system to fully operational condition*, including active repair time, logistics delay, and administrative overhead.
> $$\text{MTTR} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f_R(t) \, dt$$
> For a *constant repair rate* (exponential repair-time distribution), this simplifies dramatically to:
> $$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Suppose you call a plumber 100 times and record the time taken each visit. The MTTR is simply the **average of those 100 times**. If MTTR = 2 hours, *on average* your kitchen sink is unusable for 2 hours after every leak — this is the **cost of unreliability in time units**.

---

## 1.5 Reliability and Availability Functions for Repairable Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The **Reliability Function** $R(t)$ of a repairable system is the probability that the system *operates without failure* in the interval $(0, t]$, assuming it started working at $t=0$. The **Point Availability Function** $A(t)$ is the probability that the system is *operational at instant $t$*, regardless of how many failures and repairs it has undergone.
> $$R(t) = P(\text{no failure in } (0,t]) \quad ; \quad A(t) = P(\text{system up at time } t)$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
$R(t)$ is like a student's **streak of consecutive days without falling sick**. $A(t)$ is the **probability the student is in class at a random moment on day $t$** (sick students can recover and return, so they can be "available" again). For non-repairable systems, $R(t) = A(t)$. For repairable systems, $A(t) \ge R(t)$ always.

**Steady-State Availability:**
$$A_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} A(t) = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTBF}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** $A_{ss}$ represents the **long-run fraction of time** the system is operational. A value $A_{ss} = 0.99$ ("two-nines") means the system is down ~3.65 days/year. $A_{ss} = 0.9999$ ("four-nines") means ~52 minutes/year — the gold standard for telecom switches.

---

## 1.6 Important Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Domains Where These Concepts Are Critical:**
> 1. **Aircraft Engines** — Rolls-Royce *Power-by-the-Hour* contracts sell availability, not engines.
> 2. **Nuclear Power Plants** — Every hour of unplanned shutdown costs ~₹1 crore; high availability is non-negotiable.
> 3. **Data Centers (AWS, Google Cloud)** — 99.999% SLA requires careful MTTR/MTTF design.
> 4. **Railway Signaling Systems** — Kerala's Kochi Metro demands $A_{ss} > 0.999$.
> 5. **Oil Refineries (BPCL Kochi)** — Unscheduled shutdown costs millions per day.
> 6. **Medical Equipment (Ventilators, MRI)** — Reliability directly affects human life.
> 7. **Telecommunication Networks** — Cell towers must achieve "five-nines" availability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Reliability-Cost Trade-Off

The total life-cycle cost of a system has two competing components:

### 2.1.1 Cost of Achieving Reliability ($C_r$)
This includes:
- **Development & Design Cost:** Engineering hours, prototyping, simulation, FMEA studies.
- **Component Premium Cost:** Higher-grade parts, derating, military-spec (MIL-SPEC) components.
- **Manufacturing & Quality Control Cost:** Tighter tolerances, burn-in testing, ISO 9001 audits.
- **Redundancy Cost:** Standby units, parallel channels, N+1 configurations.
- **Maintenance Infrastructure Cost:** Spare parts inventory, training of technicians.

The relationship is **convex and monotonically increasing**:
$$C_r(R) = C_0 + a \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-R}\right)$$
where $C_0$ is the *baseline cost at minimum reliability*, and $a$ is a system-specific constant.

### 2.1.2 Cost of Unreliability ($C_u$)
This is the **expected loss due to system failures** over the operating life $T_L$:
$$C_u = \lambda \cdot T_L \cdot C_f$$
where $C_f$ is the *cost per failure* (downtime cost + repair cost + warranty + lost-customer cost). Since $\lambda$ decreases as reliability $R$ increases:
$$C_u(R) = \frac{- \ln(R)}{T_{\text{avg}}} \cdot T_L \cdot C_f$$

### 2.1.3 The Optimum
Minimize $C_T = C_r + C_u$:
$$\frac{dC_T}{dR} = \frac{dC_r}{dR} + \frac{dC_u}{dR} = 0$$
$$\Rightarrow \frac{dC_r}{dR} = -\frac{dC_u}{dR}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Optimum Reliability $R^*$ is where the marginal cost of adding reliability equals the marginal saving from reducing failures.** Operating below $R^*$ ⇒ "penny wise, pound foolish" (too many failures). Operating above $R^*$ ⇒ "gold-plating" (wasted engineering budget).

### 2.1.4 Real-World Decision Factors
- **Safety-critical systems** (aircraft, nuclear, medical): Operate **far above** $R^*$ because *human life* has unbounded cost.
- **Consumer electronics**: Operate **near** $R^*$ because market price caps the budget.
- **Defence/military**: Often use a *dual-criterion* — meet reliability target **and** achieve minimum cost.

---

## 2.2 Repairable System Modelling — Renewal Theory Fundamentals

A repairable system experiences **alternating up-down cycles**. Define:
- **Up-time (Operating Time):** Time between consecutive failures → random variable $U$.
- **Down-time (Repair Time):** Time to restore after failure → random variable $D$.
- **Cycle Time:** $C = U + D$.

For a renewal process, the **mean cycle time** is:
$$E[C] = E[U] + E[D] = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = \text{MTBF}$$

The number of renewals in time $t$ follows a **Renewal Process** $N(t)$, and by the **Elementary Renewal Theorem**:
$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{N(t)}{t} = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$$

---

## 2.3 Instantaneous Repair Rate — Detailed Behaviour

The repair rate is **the dual of the failure rate**. It applies to the *cumulative distribution function of repair times* $F_R(t)$:

| Repair Time Distribution | $f_R(t)$ | $R_R(t) = 1-F_R(t)$ | $\mu(t)$ | MTTR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exponential (rate $\mu$) | $\mu e^{-\mu t}$ | $e^{-\mu t}$ | $\mu$ (constant) | $1/\mu$ |
| Weibull (shape $\beta$, scale $\alpha$) | $\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ | $e^{-(t/\alpha)^\beta}$ | $\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $\alpha \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1+\tfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ |
| Lognormal ($\mu_{ln}, \sigma$) | — | — | complex | $e^{\mu_{ln}+\sigma^2/2}$ |
| Deterministic (constant $T_0$) | $\delta(t - T_0)$ | $1-t/T_0$ for $t \le T_0$ | $\frac{1}{T_0-t}$ | $T_0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Exponential Repair Times Are Universally Used in KTU Problems:**
> 1. Memoryless property simplifies Markov analysis.
> 2. Constant $\mu$ implies **MTTR = 1/$\mu$** — a single parameter fully describes the repair process.
> 3. Most textbook problems (and the KTU 2024 syllabus) use this assumption.

---

## 2.4 MTTR — Derivation from Repair Distribution

Starting from the definition:
$$\text{MTTR} = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f_R(t) \, dt$$

**For Exponential Repair Time** with constant rate $\mu$:
$$\text{MTTR} = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \mu e^{-\mu t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\mu}$$

**Useful General Identity (integration by parts):**
$$\text{MTTR} = \int_0^\infty R_R(t) \, dt = \int_0^\infty [1 - F_R(t)] \, dt$$

This is the **dual** of $\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t) \, dt$.

---

## 2.5 Reliability and Availability Functions — Full Mathematical Treatment

### 2.5.1 For a Single Repairable Unit (Exponential Failure & Repair)
Let $\lambda$ = constant failure rate, $\mu$ = constant repair rate. The system can be in one of two states:
- **State 0:** Operating (Up)
- **State 1:** Failed (Down)

Using **Markov analysis** (or solving the Kolmogorov differential equations):

$$A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} e^{-(\lambda + \mu) t}$$

The first term is the *steady-state* value, the second is the *transient* that decays exponentially.

**Steady-State Availability:**
$$A_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Unavailability (Down Probability):**
$$U_{ss} = 1 - A_{ss} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

### 2.5.2 Mean Down Time Per Unit Time
$$d_{ss} = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} \quad \text{(fraction of time down)}$$

### 2.5.3 Frequency of Failures
$$f_{ss} = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\lambda \mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

### 2.5.4 Expected Downtime in Time $T$
$$D(T) = U_{ss} \cdot T = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} \cdot T$$

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total life-cycle cost | $C_T = C_r + C_u$ | Currency (₹, \$) |
| 2 | Cost of unreliability (per failure) | $C_f = C_d \cdot T_d + C_{\text{repair}}$ | Currency |
| 3 | Optimum reliability | $\frac{dC_r}{dR} = -\frac{dC_u}{dR}$ | Solve numerically |
| 4 | Instantaneous repair rate | $\mu(t) = \dfrac{f_R(t)}{1-F_R(t)}$ | per hour (h⁻¹) |
| 5 | MTTR (general) | $\text{MTTR} = \int_0^\infty R_R(t)\, dt$ | hours |
| 6 | MTTR (exponential) | $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$ | hours |
| 7 | MTTR (Weibull repair) | $\alpha \cdot \Gamma(1+1/\beta)$ | hours |
| 8 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | hours |
| 9 | Point availability | $A(t) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} + \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Steady-state availability | $A_{ss} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTBF}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 11 | Steady-state unavailability | $U_{ss} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Failure frequency | $f_{ss} = \dfrac{\lambda\mu}{\lambda+\mu} = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | failures/hour |
| 13 | Expected downtime in $T$ | $D(T) = \dfrac{\lambda T}{\lambda+\mu}$ | hours |
| 14 | Expected downtime cost in $T$ | $C_d(T) = C_d \cdot \dfrac{\lambda T}{\lambda+\mu}$ | currency |
| 15 | Renewal rate (long-run) | $\displaystyle\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{N(t)}{t} = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | renewals/hour |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid (KTU Examiners Love This):** "**$A_{ss} = \mu / (\lambda + \mu)$**" — *the bigger the repair rate $\mu$ relative to failure rate $\lambda$, the higher the availability*. If you can repair 100× faster than you fail, availability is excellent.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why These Equations Matter

- **Telecom industry** uses $A_{ss}$ to define **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)** with customers.
- **Aircraft engine manufacturers** (Rolls-Royce, GE) charge customers *per flying hour* because they are paid for *availability*, not ownership.
- **Data center designers** compute *expected annual downtime* using $D(T)$ to size UPS systems and backup generators.
- **Insurance companies** use $C_u$ to price warranty products.
- **Defense procurement** uses $R^*$ to set reliability specifications in tender documents.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: MTTR for Exponential Repair Distribution

**Given:** Repair time $T$ follows exponential distribution with constant rate $\mu$.

**Step 1: Write the PDF.**
$$f_R(t) = \mu e^{-\mu t}, \quad t \ge 0$$

**Step 2: Apply the definition of expected value.**
$$\text{MTTR} = E[T] = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f_R(t) \, dt = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \mu e^{-\mu t} \, dt$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integral using integration by parts.**
Let $u = t \Rightarrow du = dt$ and $dv = \mu e^{-\mu t} dt \Rightarrow v = -e^{-\mu t}$.

$$\text{MTTR} = \left[-t e^{-\mu t}\right]_0^\infty + \int_0^\infty e^{-\mu t} \, dt$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the boundary term.**
$$\lim_{t \to \infty} t e^{-\mu t} = 0 \quad \text{(exponential decays faster than linear growth)}$$
$$-0 \cdot e^{-\mu \cdot 0} = 0$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the second integral.**
$$\int_0^\infty e^{-\mu t} \, dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\mu} e^{-\mu t}\right]_0^\infty = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\mu}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}$$

**Step 6: Combine.**
$$\boxed{\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **duality principle**: MTTF for constant failure rate $\lambda$ is $1/\lambda$; MTTR for constant repair rate $\mu$ is $1/\mu$. The mathematical structure is identical.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Point Availability $A(t)$ from Markov Model

**Model:** Single-unit repairable system with two states. State 0 = Operating, State 1 = Failed.

**Step 1: Define state probabilities.**
- $P_0(t)$ = Probability the system is operating at time $t$.
- $P_1(t)$ = Probability the system is failed at time $t$.

By total probability:
$$P_0(t) + P_1(t) = 1$$

**Step 2: Write the Kolmogorov forward equations.**
In a small interval $\Delta t$:
- Probability of moving from State 0 to State 1: $\lambda \cdot \Delta t$.
- Probability of moving from State 1 to State 0: $\mu \cdot \Delta t$.

Therefore:
$$P_0(t + \Delta t) = P_0(t)(1 - \lambda \Delta t) + P_1(t)(\mu \Delta t)$$
$$P_1(t + \Delta t) = P_0(t)(\lambda \Delta t) + P_1(t)(1 - \mu \Delta t)$$

**Step 3: Take the limit $\Delta t \to 0$.**
$$\frac{dP_0(t)}{dt} = -\lambda P_0(t) + \mu P_1(t)$$
$$\frac{dP_1(t)}{dt} = \lambda P_0(t) - \mu P_1(t)$$

**Step 4: Note the point availability equals the up-state probability.**
$$A(t) = P_0(t)$$

**Step 5: Eliminate $P_1(t)$ using $P_1(t) = 1 - P_0(t)$.**
$$\frac{dP_0}{dt} = -\lambda P_0 + \mu(1 - P_0) = -(\lambda + \mu) P_0 + \mu$$

**Step 6: This is a first-order linear ODE of the form $\frac{dy}{dt} + ky = c$.**
The general solution is:
$$P_0(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + C \cdot e^{-(\lambda + \mu) t}$$

**Step 7: Apply the initial condition $P_0(0) = 1$ (system starts operational).**
$$1 = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + C \cdot e^0 \;\Rightarrow\; C = 1 - \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Step 8: Write the final form.**
$$\boxed{A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} e^{-(\lambda + \mu) t}}$$

**Step 9: Identify the steady-state value.**
$$\lim_{t \to \infty} e^{-(\lambda + \mu) t} = 0$$
$$\boxed{A_{ss} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}}$$

**Step 10: Express in terms of MTTF and MTTR.**
Since $\lambda = 1/\text{MTTF}$ and $\mu = 1/\text{MTTR}$:
$$A_{ss} = \frac{1/\text{MTTR}}{1/\text{MTTF} + 1/\text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTR} + \text{MTTF}} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTBF}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The availability starts at 1 (just turned on), dips slightly as failures occur, then rises back to $A_{ss}$. The *time constant* of the transient is $\tau = 1/(\lambda + \mu)$ — after $4\tau$ the system is within 2% of steady state.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Expected Annual Downtime and Cost

**Problem Setup:** A repairable system with $\lambda$, $\mu$, operating for $T$ hours/year. Downtime cost rate = $C_d$ (currency per hour of downtime).

**Step 1: Steady-state unavailability.**
$$U_{ss} = 1 - A_{ss} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Step 2: Expected downtime per year.**
$$D_{\text{year}} = U_{ss} \cdot T = \frac{\lambda T}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Step 3: Expected annual downtime cost.**
$$C_{\text{down}} = C_d \cdot D_{\text{year}} = \frac{C_d \lambda T}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Step 4: Total expected cost including repair.**
$$C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{capex}} + C_{\text{opex}} + \frac{C_d \lambda T}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Step 5: To find optimum $\lambda$ (i.e., optimum reliability), differentiate and equate to zero.**
$$\frac{d C_{\text{total}}}{d\lambda} = -\frac{C_d \mu T}{(\lambda + \mu)^2} + \frac{dC_{\text{capex}}}{d\lambda} = 0$$

This gives the **cost-reliability balance point**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

**Problem:** A CNC machine has constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour and constant repair rate $\mu = 0.05$ repairs/hour. The machine operates 2000 hours/year. Downtime costs ₹8,000/hour. Find (i) MTTF, (ii) MTTR, (iii) MTBF, (iv) $A_{ss}$, (v) Expected annual downtime, (vi) Expected annual downtime cost.

**Solution:**

**Step (i): MTTF.**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step (ii): MTTR.**
$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20 \text{ hours}$$

**Step (iii): MTBF.**
$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 2000 + 20 = 2020 \text{ hours}$$

**Step (iv): Steady-state availability.**
$$A_{ss} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.05}{0.0005 + 0.05} = \frac{0.05}{0.0505} = 0.9901$$

**Step (v): Expected annual downtime.**
$$D_{\text{year}} = \frac{\lambda T}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.0005 \times 2000}{0.0505} = \frac{1}{0.0505} = 19.80 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step (vi): Expected annual downtime cost.**
$$C_{\text{down}} = 8000 \times 19.80 = ₹1,58,416 \text{ per year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** In 2000 hours, the system experiences $\lambda T = 1$ failure on average, each taking 20 hours to repair. So 20 hours of downtime — close to our 19.80 (slight difference due to rounding). ✓

---

## 3.5 Two-Unit Parallel Repairable System

**Model:** Two identical units in parallel; both must fail for system failure. One repair crew.

**Using Markov states** (0 = both up, 1 = one up + one down, 2 = both down = system failure):
- $0 \to 1$: rate $2\lambda$
- $1 \to 2$: rate $\lambda$
- $1 \to 0$: rate $\mu$
- $2 \to 1$: rate $\mu$

**Steady-state probabilities** (solving $\pi Q = 0$):
$$\pi_0 = \frac{\mu^2}{(\lambda+\mu)^2}, \quad \pi_1 = \frac{2\lambda\mu}{(\lambda+\mu)^2}, \quad \pi_2 = \frac{\lambda^2}{(\lambda+\mu)^2}$$

**System availability (system up = states 0 or 1):**
$$A_{ss}^{\text{parallel}} = \pi_0 + \pi_1 = 1 - \left(\frac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu}\right)^2 = 1 - (U_{ss,\text{single}})^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dramatic Improvement:** If single-unit $A_{ss} = 0.99$ (i.e., $U = 0.01$), then *two in parallel* gives $A_{ss} = 1 - 0.01^2 = 0.9999$ — i.e., *four-nines availability*! This is why **redundancy** is the engineer's primary tool to boost availability (at a cost).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Instantaneous repair rate $\mu(t)$
(b) Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)
(c) Steady-state availability $A_{ss}$

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **(a) Instantaneous Repair Rate $\mu(t)$:** The instantaneous repair rate is the rate at which repairs are completed at time $t$, conditional on the repair not being complete until time $t$. For exponential repair time with constant rate $\mu$, it equals $\mu$ for all $t$. *Example:* A field service team that can complete an average of 2 repairs per hour has $\mu = 2$ h⁻¹. **(1 mark)**

> **(b) MTTR:** The Mean Time To Repair is the expected value of the random variable representing time to restore a failed system to working condition. For constant repair rate $\mu$, $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$. *Example:* If MTTR = 4 hours, the system is on average out of service for 4 hours per failure. **(1 mark)**

> **(c) Steady-State Availability $A_{ss}$:** The long-run fraction of time a repairable system is operational. For constant $\lambda$ and $\mu$, $A_{ss} = \mu/(\lambda + \mu) = \text{MTTF}/(\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR})$. *Example:* $A_{ss} = 0.99$ means the system is operational 99% of the time over its life. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Distinguish between reliability and availability for a repairable system. Why is availability the more meaningful metric for repairable systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Reliability $R(t)$ | Availability $A(t)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | P(no failure in $(0,t]$) | P(system up at instant $t$) |
| Effect of repair | Ignored | Accounted for |
| Decay | Monotonically decreasing | Approaches steady-state value |
| After repair | Not applicable | System can be available again |

**Key Insight (1 mark):** For a **repairable system**, after a failure the system can be brought back to operation; hence $R(t)$ (which counts only first-failure survival) understates the system's *real* performance. **Availability** correctly accounts for the fact that the system is usable again after repair, making it the appropriate metric. *Example:* An aircraft that fails mid-flight, lands safely, is repaired overnight, and flies again next day is *available* to the airline the next morning — even though $R(24\text{h}) < 1$ would have considered it "failed."

---

Each unit has constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$/h and constant repair rate $\mu = 0.05$/h. Calculate: (i) MTTF and MTTR of each unit, (ii) Steady-state unavailability of a *single* unit, (iii) Steady-state availability of the *parallel* system, (iv) Expected annual downtime of the parallel system if it operates 5000 hours/year, (v) Comment on the improvement in availability due to parallel redundancy.

## ✅ COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Reliability-Cost Trade-Off [7 marks]**

> The reliability-cost trade-off is a fundamental engineering decision framework.

**Step 1: Identify the two cost components. (2 marks)**
- **Cost of Achieving Reliability ($C_r$):** Includes design cost, premium components, redundancy, manufacturing quality control, testing & burn-in, maintenance infrastructure. Mathematically modeled as a convex increasing function of target reliability $R$.
- **Cost of Unreliability ($C_u$):** Includes downtime cost, warranty claims, repair labor, spare parts, lost production, customer goodwill loss, and (in safety-critical systems) potential liability. Modeled as a decreasing function of $R$.

**Step 2: Total life-cycle cost. (1 mark)**
$$C_T(R) = C_r(R) + C_u(R)$$

**Step 3: Derive the optimum condition. (2 marks)**
$$\frac{dC_T}{dR} = \frac{dC_r}{dR} + \frac{dC_u}{dR} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dC_r}{dR} = -\frac{dC_u}{dR}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation. (2 marks)**
- The optimum reliability $R^*$ is where the *marginal cost of adding reliability* equals the *marginal saving from reducing failures*.
- For **safety-critical** systems (aircraft, nuclear), $R^*$ is forced to a *very high* value regardless of cost, because human-life cost is unbounded.
- For **commercial systems** (smartphones, appliances), $R^*$ is the *market-driven* optimum where price and warranty costs balance.

*(A block diagram showing $C_r$ increasing, $C_u$ decreasing, and $C_T$ with a minimum should be drawn. Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]**

**Given:** $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ /h, $\mu = 0.04$ /h, $T = 3000$ h/year, $C_d = ₹10{,}000$/h.

**(i) MTTF [1 mark]:**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) MTTR [1 mark]:**
$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{0.04} = 25 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) MTBF [1 mark]:**
$$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 2000 + 25 = 2025 \text{ hours}$$

**(iv) Steady-state availability [1 mark]:**
$$A_{ss} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.04}{0.0005 + 0.04} = \frac{0.04}{0.0405} = 0.9877$$

**(v) Expected number of failures per year [1 mark]:**
$$N = \lambda T = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3000 = 1.5 \text{ failures/year}$$

**(vi) Expected annual downtime [1 mark]:**
$$D = N \times \text{MTTR} = 1.5 \times 25 = 37.5 \text{ hours/year}$$
*Or equivalently:* $D = U_{ss} \cdot T = (1 - 0.9877) \times 3000 = 0.0123 \times 3000 = 36.9$ hours (minor rounding) ✓

**(vii) Expected annual downtime cost [1 mark]:**
$$C_{\text{down}} = C_d \times D = 10{,}000 \times 37.5 = ₹3{,}75{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing MTTF with MTBF. MTTF = mean time to **first** failure; MTBF = mean time *between* failures (= MTTF + MTTR for repairable systems). **−2 marks** if swapped.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting units in $\lambda$ and $\mu$. They are per-hour; MTTF and MTTR come out in hours. **−1 mark** if not stated.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Computing MTTR as 25 hours *for the year*. MTTR is **per repair event**, not per year. Multiply by expected number of failures to get annual downtime. **−1 mark** if misinterpreted.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Using $\lambda + \mu$ incorrectly in availability formula. The correct formula is $A_{ss} = \mu/(\lambda + \mu)$, *not* $1 - \lambda/(\lambda + \mu)$ written carelessly.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Instantaneous Repair Rate and MTTR [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Define $\mu(t)$. (1 mark)**
$$\mu(t) = \frac{f_R(t)}{1 - F_R(t)} = \frac{f_R(t)}{R_R(t)}$$
where $f_R(t)$ is the PDF of repair time, $F_R(t)$ is its CDF, and $R_R(t) = 1 - F_R(t)$ is the *reliability of the repair process*.

**Step 2: MTTR definition. (1 mark)**
$$\text{MTTR} = \int_0^\infty t \, f_R(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3: Apply exponential distribution. (1 mark)**
For exponential: $f_R(t) = \mu e^{-\mu t}$.

$$\text{MTTR} = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \mu e^{-\mu t} \, dt$$

**Step 4: Integration by parts. (2 marks)**
Using $u = t$, $dv = \mu e^{-\mu t} dt$:
$$\text{MTTR} = \left[-t e^{-\mu t}\right]_0^\infty + \int_0^\infty e^{-\mu t} \, dt = 0 + \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu}$$

**Step 5: Duality principle. (2 marks)**
Just as **failure rate $\lambda(t)$** describes how *unreliable* a system is (higher $\lambda$ ⇒ lower $R(t)$), the **repair rate $\mu(t)$** describes how *reliable the recovery process* is (higher $\mu$ ⇒ lower MTTR). The two rates are mathematical duals:

| Aspect | Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$ | Repair Rate $\mu(t)$ |
|---|---|---|
| PDF | $f(t)$ | $f_R(t)$ |
| Conditional prob. | $\frac{f(t) \, dt}{R(t)}$ | $\frac{f_R(t) \, dt}{R_R(t)}$ |
| For exponential | $\lambda$ (constant) | $\mu$ (constant) |
| Reciprocal | MTTF $= 1/\lambda$ | MTTR $= 1/\mu$ |
| Goal | **Minimize** | **Maximize** |

A high-reliability, high-availability system is one with **low $\lambda$ and high $\mu$** — i.e., it rarely fails *and* is quickly repaired when it does.

---

**Part (b) — Parallel Redundancy Numerical [7 marks]**

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.001$/h, $\mu = 0.05$/h, parallel redundancy with single repair crew.

**(i) MTTF and MTTR per unit [1 mark]:**
$$\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = 1000 \text{ h}; \quad \text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu} = 20 \text{ h}$$

**(ii) Single-unit unavailability [1 mark]:**
$$U_{ss} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.001}{0.051} = 0.01961$$

**(iii) Parallel system availability [2 marks]:**
$$A_{ss}^{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (U_{ss})^2 = 1 - (0.01961)^2 = 1 - 0.000385 = 0.999615$$

**(iv) Annual downtime [1 mark]:**
$$D_{\text{year}} = (1 - A_{ss}^{\text{parallel}}) \times T = 0.000385 \times 5000 = 1.924 \text{ hours/year}$$

**(v) Comment on improvement [2 marks]:**
- Single-unit $A_{ss} = 0.98039$ (~98% — "two-nines minus").
- Parallel $A_{ss} = 0.99962$ (~99.96% — "three-nines plus").
- **Improvement:** The parallel system is *unavailable* ~19× less often than a single unit.
- **Cost:** Double the hardware cost, plus the complexity of switching and single repair-crew bottleneck.
- This illustrates the **fundamental availability-redundancy-cost triangle**: more redundancy → higher availability → higher cost. Engineers must balance all three.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Adding availabilities $(A_1 + A_2)$ instead of multiplying unavailabilities. Correct: $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1-A_1)(1-A_2) = 1 - U_1 \cdot U_2$. **−3 marks** if added.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using the *same* $\lambda$ for both units in $0 \to 1$ transition *and* the $1 \to 2$ transition. With two units operating, the failure rate *doubles* to $2\lambda$ in the first step. (For $A_{ss}$ result this often cancels out, but the full Markov analysis is different.)
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting the **single repair crew constraint** — both failed units compete for the *one* repair team, which slightly degrades the availability. In the simplified $A_{ss} = 1 - U^2$ formula, we assume the repair crew is *always* available, which is the standard KTU assumption.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not stating the assumption of **exponential failure and repair distributions**. KTU expects this to be mentioned explicitly. **−1 mark** if missing.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 3 Topics):**

### **A. Reliability-Cost Trade-Off**
- ✅ Total cost $C_T = C_r(R) + C_u(R)$ → minimized at $R^*$
- ✅ Cost of unreliability: $C_u = \lambda T_L C_f$ (failures × life × cost-per-failure)
- ✅ Optimum condition: $\frac{dC_r}{dR} = -\frac{dC_u}{dR}$
- ✅ Safety-critical systems operate *above* the cost-optimum $R^*$

### **B. Repairable Systems**
- ✅ Distinguished from non-repairable by *cycle structure*: Up → Down → Up
- ✅ Performance metric is **Availability** $A(t)$, not just $R(t)$
- ✅ Cycle time: $C = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = \text{MTBF}$

### **C. Instantaneous Repair Rate**
- ✅ Formula: $\mu(t) = f_R(t)/R_R(t)$ (dual of failure rate)
- ✅ For exponential repair: $\mu(t) = \mu$ = constant
- ✅ Units: **h⁻¹** (or **yr⁻¹** depending on time scale)

### **D. MTTR**
- ✅ Definition: $\text{MTTR} = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f_R(t) \, dt = \int_0^\infty R_R(t) \, dt$
- ✅ For exponential: $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$
- ✅ Dual of MTTF

### **E. Availability Functions**
- ✅ **Point availability:** $A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} e^{-(\lambda + \mu) t}$
- ✅ **Steady-state availability:** $A_{ss} = \mu/(\lambda + \mu) = \text{MTTF}/\text{MTBF}$
- ✅ **Unavailability:** $U_{ss} = \lambda/(\lambda + \mu) = \text{MTTR}/\text{MTBF}$
- ✅ **Expected downtime in time $T$:** $D(T) = \lambda T/(\lambda + \mu)$
- ✅ **Frequency of failures:** $f_{ss} = \lambda \mu/(\lambda + \mu) = 1/\text{MTBF}$
- ✅ **Time constant to steady state:** $\tau = 1/(\lambda + \mu)$

### **F. Parallel Redundancy**
- ✅ Two-unit parallel: $A_{ss} = 1 - (\lambda/(\lambda + \mu))^2 = 1 - U_{ss}^2$
- ✅ Redundancy dramatically improves availability but at hardware cost
- ✅ Repair crew capacity can be a bottleneck

### **G. Industry "Nines" Reference**
| Nines | $A_{ss}$ | Downtime/Year | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two | 0.99 | 3.65 days | Commercial servers |
| Three | 0.999 | 8.77 hours | Enterprise systems |
| Four | 0.9999 | 52.6 min | Telecom, banking |
| Five | 0.99999 | 5.26 min | Military, aircraft |

### **H. Most-Asked KTU Question Patterns**
1. *Derive $A_{ss} = \mu/(\lambda + \mu)$ from first principles* (6–7 marks)
2. *Numerical: Given $\lambda$, $\mu$, $T$, $C_d$ → find all six derived quantities* (7 marks)
3. *Compare single-unit vs parallel-redundant system numerically* (7 marks)
4. *Explain reliability-cost trade-off with sketch* (7 marks)
5. *Define $\mu(t)$ and derive MTTR for exponential* (4–6 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **🎯 Final KTU Examination Tip:**
> Always **state the assumptions** explicitly at the start of any solution: (1) constant failure rate, (2) constant repair rate, (3) exponential distributions, (4) statistically independent units (for redundancy), (5) single repair crew (if applicable). Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** purely for the right assumptions — missing them costs easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault-Tree Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT525 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fault-Tree Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive (top-down) failure analysis methodology used in reliability and safety engineering, in which an undesired state of a system (the **TOP EVENT** or **Root Event**) is analyzed using Boolean logic to combine a series of lower-level events (basic events and intermediate events) connected through logic gates (AND, OR, etc.). The **Fault-Tree Construction** phase is the systematic, graphical process of representing the logical relationships among system-level failures and their contributing causes using standardized symbols, in accordance with **IEEE Std 352** and **IEC 61025**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT525 - Module 4):**
> Fault-Tree Construction is the **preliminary step** that must be completed *before* any qualitative (Minimal Cut Set analysis) or quantitative (probability evaluation) work can be performed. Without a correctly built fault tree, all subsequent reliability calculations become invalid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor investigating the cause of a patient's fever. You don't immediately prescribe medicine. Instead, you ask:
- "Is it an infection?" (OR) — could be viral **OR** bacterial.
- "If infection, are both lungs affected?" (AND) — fever **AND** cough **AND** chest pain indicates pneumonia.

A **Fault Tree** works exactly the same way:
- The **TOP** of the tree is the "patient has a fever" (system failure).
- The **branches** are diagnostic questions (gates).
- The **leaves** are the root causes (basic events like "virus present" or "bacteria present").

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Fault-Tree Construction is **deductive** (you start from the consequence and work backward to causes), as opposed to **inductive** methods like FMEA (Failure Modes and Effects Analysis), which start from causes and work forward to consequences.

### 1.3 Why Fault-Tree Construction Matters in Engineering

In industries such as **nuclear power**, **aerospace**, **chemical processing**, and **automotive safety (ISO 26262)**, engineers must certify that catastrophic failures are "incredibly unlikely." The construction step provides:
- A **visual logic map** of all failure pathways.
- A **common language** between design, safety, and operations teams.
- The structural input for **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** determination and **importance factor** calculations.

The standard failure probability target is often **< 10⁻⁹** for nuclear and avionics systems, with the **Top Event** serving as the metric.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Standardized Fault-Tree Symbols (Per IEEE 352 / IEC 61025)

Fault-Tree Construction relies on a strict vocabulary of symbols. Memorizing them is essential for KTU questions.

#### 2.1.1 Event Symbols (Shapes)

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangle | **Intermediate Event / Top Event** | A fault event that results from a combination of lower-level events through a logic gate. |
| Circle | **Basic Event** | A primary, unresolved failure at the lowest level of the tree (component failure). |
| Diamond | **Undeveloped Event** | A failure event not analyzed further (insufficient information or deemed negligible). |
| House | **Normal (House) Event** | An event that is *expected* to occur (e.g., power ON). |
| Oval / Ellipse | **Conditioning Event** | A constraint or condition applied to another gate. |

#### 2.1.2 Logic Gate Symbols (The Operators)

| Gate Symbol | Name | Output Logic | Boolean Expression |
|---|---|---|---|
| `&` shape (D-shape) | **AND Gate** | Output fails **only if ALL** inputs fail. | $Y = A \cdot B$ |
| Shield (curved) | **OR Gate** | Output fails **if ANY** input fails. | $Y = A + B$ |
| Hexagon with "1" | **Exclusive OR (XOR)** | Output fails if **exactly one** input fails. | $Y = A \oplus B$ |
| Diamond with bar | **Inhibit Gate** | Output fails if input fails **AND** condition is present. | $Y = A \cdot C$ |
| Oval with "=1" | **Priority AND** | AND gate with input sequence constraint. | $Y = A \cdot B$ (ordered) |

### 2.2 The Six Steps of Fault-Tree Construction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks "Construct the Fault Tree for…", these six steps are the *exact* framework examiners use to award marks. Skipping Step 1 or Step 2 typically costs 2–3 marks.

1. **Define the Top Event Precisely** — A single, well-stated undesirable event. *Bad:* "System fails." *Good:* "Pump P1 fails to deliver water flow > 50 L/min within 30 s of start command."
2. **Define System Boundaries** — Specify what is included (mission profile, environment, operating time) and what is excluded.
3. **Identify Existing System Conditions** — House events like "Power Supply ON" must be declared.
4. **Develop the Immediate (Level-1) Causes** — Using engineering knowledge, list all the *direct* reasons the top event can occur.
5. **Decompose Each Immediate Cause** — Recursively break each event into contributing lower-level events using AND/OR logic.
6. **Continue Until Basic Events Are Reached** — Stop when every lowest branch is a circle (basic event), diamond (undeveloped), or house event.

### 2.3 Constructive Logic: The AND vs. OR Decision

A student must master this rule — it is the single most common source of marks lost:

- Use an **OR gate** when the top event can result from **any one** of several **independent** failure mechanisms (e.g., pump fails due to bearing seizure OR motor burnout OR shaft fracture).
- Use an **AND gate** when the top event requires **simultaneous occurrence** of **multiple** conditions (e.g., a flood requires both river overtopping AND levee failure).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Boolean structure function (OR gate) | $\Phi = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} X_i = X_1 + X_2 + \dots + X_n$ | Modeling parallel redundancy paths. |
| 2 | Boolean structure function (AND gate) | $\Phi = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} X_i = X_1 \cdot X_2 \dots X_n$ | Modeling required simultaneous subsystems. |
| 3 | Probability via AND gate (independent) | $P(\Phi) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} q_i$ | Used after MCS to compute top event probability. |
| 4 | Probability via OR gate (independent) | $P(\Phi) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - q_i)$ | Used for non-repairable OR configurations. |
| 5 | De Morgan's Law (for simplification) | $\overline{A + B} = \bar{A} \cdot \bar{B}$ | Converting OR of complements into AND. |
| 6 | Distributive Law (cut-set expansion) | $A + (B \cdot C) = (A + B)(A + C)$ | Expanding an OR-AND tree into MCS form. |
| 7 | Minimal Cut Set (MCS) order | Order = number of basic events in the cut set. | Higher order = less likely = more reliable system. |
| 8 | Top Event boundary condition | $0 \le P(\text{Top}) \le 1$ | Always sanity-check probabilities after construction. |

> **Units used in reliability:** Failure rate $\lambda$ in **failures/hour** or **FIT** (Failures In Time, 1 FIT = $10^{-9}$/hour). For non-repairable systems in mission time $t$, $q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \approx \lambda t$ for small $\lambda t$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fault-Tree Construction is foundational in:
- **Nuclear reactor safety** (Reactor Safety Study, WASH-1400, 1975) — established FTA as a regulatory standard.
- **Aerospace**: NASA and Boeing use FTA on every fly-by-wire system.
- **Chemical plants**: OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 mandates process hazard analysis, often using FTA.
- **Automotive functional safety**: ISO 26262 uses FTA-derived ASIL decomposition.

The tree's *qualitative* output (MCS list) tells designers *which components* to upgrade, while the *quantitative* output (top event probability) tells regulators *how safe* the system is.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Converting a Fault Tree into a Boolean Structure Function

**Problem Setup:**
Suppose we have a fault tree with the **Top Event T** decomposed as follows:
- T = "System Failure"
- T occurs if **Sub-system A** fails **OR** **Sub-system B** fails.
- Sub-system A fails only if **Component 1** fails **AND** **Component 2** fails.
- Sub-system B fails if **Component 3** fails **OR** **Component 4** fails.

We want to derive the structure function $\Phi(X)$ in both **expanded (sum-of-products)** and **Minimal Cut Set** form.

**Step 1 — Translate each gate into a Boolean expression.**

The OR gate connecting sub-systems A and B gives the first-level expression:
$$T = A + B$$

The AND gate under sub-system A gives:
$$A = X_1 \cdot X_2$$

The OR gate under sub-system B gives:
$$B = X_3 + X_4$$

**Step 2 — Substitute lower-level expressions into the top expression.**

Substitute the expressions for A and B back into the equation for T:
$$T = (X_1 \cdot X_2) + (X_3 + X_4)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Distributive Law to expand into Sum-of-Products (SOP) form.**

Apply the rule $P + (Q + R) = P + Q + R$ and $P + (Q \cdot R) = (P+Q)(P+R)$ to convert the nested expression into a flat SOP. We use the distributive law on $(X_1 \cdot X_2) + (X_3 + X_4)$:

$$T = X_1 \cdot X_2 + X_3 + X_4$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Minimal Cut Sets (MCS).**

A **Cut Set** is a set of basic events whose *simultaneous* failure causes the top event. A **Minimal** Cut Set is one that cannot be reduced further (no proper subset is itself a cut set).

Examining $T = X_1 \cdot X_2 + X_3 + X_4$, we identify three cut sets:
- **Cut Set 1:** $\{X_1, X_2\}$ — both must fail together (order = 2).
- **Cut Set 2:** $\{X_3\}$ — a single failure causes T (order = 1).
- **Cut Set 3:** $\{X_4\}$ — a single failure causes T (order = 1).

None of these is a superset of another that can be removed, so all three are **Minimal**.

**Step 5 — Write the canonical Boolean expression in MCS form.**

$$T = K_1 \cup K_2 \cup K_3 = (X_1 \cap X_2) \cup (X_3) \cup (X_4)$$

where $K_i$ is the i-th minimal cut set.

**Step 6 — Compute the Top Event Probability (quantitative evaluation).**

Let $q_i = P(X_i = 1)$ be the failure probability of basic event $X_i$. For independent events, the probability of an AND cut set is the product of probabilities, and the OR combination of cut sets uses the inclusion-exclusion principle.

First, the probability that cut set $K_1$ causes failure:
$$P(K_1) = q_1 \cdot q_2$$

The probability that the OR of three cut sets causes failure is:
$$P(T) = 1 - P(\bar{K_1}) \cdot P(\bar{K_2}) \cdot P(\bar{K_3})$$

(assuming cut sets are independent, which is an approximation — see KTU caveat below)

Substituting:
$$P(T) = 1 - (1 - q_1 q_2)(1 - q_3)(1 - q_4)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Caveat:** Independence of cut sets is rarely *strictly* true because basic events often share common causes (e.g., a single power supply failure may cause multiple components to fail). In board problems, students are expected to **state the independence assumption explicitly** before applying the formula. Marks are deducted for silent assumptions.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Types

| Problem Type | Construction Step Required | Marks Typically Allocated |
|---|---|---|
| Draw the fault tree for a 2-component parallel system | Steps 1–6 for a simple OR gate | 7 marks |
| Compute Top Event probability given component failure rates | Steps 1–6 + Boolean derivation | 14 marks |
| Identify the Minimal Cut Sets from a given tree | Step 6 only | 3–7 marks |
| Convert an event-tree scenario into a fault tree | Steps 1–4 with engineering judgment | 14 marks |
| Apply De Morgan's Law to simplify a tree | Algebraic manipulation of structure function | 3–7 marks |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example for KTU Practice

**System:** A dual-pump water supply system. The top event is *"Insufficient Water Flow to Customer."* Flow is insufficient if **both** pumps fail. Each pump fails if either its **motor** burns out **OR** its **impeller** jams.

**Step 1 — Identify Top Event.**
$$T = \text{Insufficient Water Flow}$$

**Step 2 — First-Level Decomposition (AND gate).**
Both pumps must fail:
$$T = P_1 \cdot P_2$$

**Step 3 — Second-Level Decomposition of each pump (OR gate).**
Each pump fails if motor OR impeller fails:
$$P_1 = M_1 + I_1, \quad P_2 = M_2 + I_2$$

**Step 4 — Final Boolean structure function.**
$$T = (M_1 + I_1)(M_2 + I_2)$$

**Step 5 — Expand to SOP form using distributive law.**

Apply $(M_1 + I_1)(M_2 + I_2) = M_1 M_2 + M_1 I_2 + I_1 M_2 + I_1 I_2$:
$$T = M_1 M_2 + M_1 I_2 + I_1 M_2 + I_1 I_2$$

**Step 6 — Identify Minimal Cut Sets.**

The four cut sets are:
- $K_1 = \{M_1, M_2\}$ — both motors fail.
- $K_2 = \{M_1, I_2\}$ — pump 1 motor + pump 2 impeller.
- $K_3 = \{I_1, M_2\}$ — pump 1 impeller + pump 2 motor.
- $K_4 = \{I_1, I_2\}$ — both impellers jam.

All are of order 2, so all are **Minimal**.

**Step 7 — Probability calculation.**

Let $q_M = 0.01$ and $q_I = 0.005$ for all motors and impellers.

Probability of each cut set:
$$P(K_i) = (0.01)(0.005) = 5 \times 10^{-5}$$

If we assume independence and use the rare-event approximation (sum of cut-set probabilities):
$$P(T) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{4} P(K_i) = 4 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A dual-pump system with a 1% motor failure rate and 0.5% impeller failure rate has a $2 \times 10^{-4}$ (1 in 5,000) mission-failure probability — acceptable for many industrial water systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Fault Tree: The Dual-Pump System

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **functional architecture** of the fault tree built in Section 3.3. Per the safeguards, every node ID is alphanumeric, and labels contain only clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
graph TD
    topEvent["TOP EVENT INSUFFICIENT WATER FLOW"]
    andGate1["AND GATE BOTH PUMPS MUST FAIL"]
    subA["PUMP 1 FAILS"]
    subB["PUMP 2 FAILS"]
    orGate1["OR GATE MOTOR OR IMPELLER"]
    orGate2["OR GATE MOTOR OR IMPELLER"]
    motor1["BASIC EVENT MOTOR 1 BURNS OUT"]
    impeller1["BASIC EVENT IMPELLER 1 JAMS"]
    motor2["BASIC EVENT MOTOR 2 BURNS OUT"]
    impeller2["BASIC EVENT IMPELLER 2 JAMS"]

    topEvent --> andGate1
    andGate1 --> subA
    andGate1 --> subB
    subA --> orGate1
    subB --> orGate2
    orGate1 --> motor1
    orGate1 --> impeller1
    orGate2 --> motor2
    orGate2 --> impeller2

    classDef topStyle fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef gateStyle fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef basicStyle fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class topEvent topStyle
    class andGate1,orGate1,orGate2,subA,subB gateStyle
    class motor1,impeller1,motor2,impeller2 basicStyle
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Generic Construction Workflow

This diagram maps the six-step construction process as a functional flow, suitable for answering "Explain the steps of Fault-Tree Construction" type questions.

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1["STEP 1 DEFINE TOP EVENT"]
    step2["STEP 2 SET BOUNDARIES"]
    step3["STEP 3 IDENTIFY HOUSE EVENTS"]
    step4["STEP 4 DECOMPOSE IMMEDIATE CAUSES"]
    step5["STEP 5 APPLY AND OR GATES"]
    step6["STEP 6 REACH BASIC EVENTS"]
    output["FAULT TREE COMPLETE"]

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> output

    classDef processNode fill:#95e1d3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#f38181,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class step1,step2,step3,step4,step5,step6 processNode
    class output outputNode
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: AND vs OR Gate Logic Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    andTop["AND GATE OUTPUT FAILS"]
    andIn1["INPUT A FAILS"]
    andIn2["INPUT B FAILS"]
    andRule["BOTH MUST FAIL TOGETHER"]

    orTop["OR GATE OUTPUT FAILS"]
    orIn1["INPUT C FAILS"]
    orIn2["INPUT D FAILS"]
    orRule["EITHER ONE ALONE SUFFICES"]

    andTop --- andIn1
    andTop --- andIn2
    andTop --- andRule
    orTop --- orIn1
    orTop --- orIn2
    orTop --- orRule

    classDef andStyle fill:#a8e6cf,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef orStyle fill:#ffd3b6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class andTop,andIn1,andIn2,andRule andStyle
    class orTop,orIn1,orIn2,orRule orStyle
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define the following terms used in Fault-Tree Construction: (a) Top Event, (b) Basic Event, (c) Undeveloped Event.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(a) Top Event (1 mark):** The undesired system-level failure event placed at the root (apex) of the fault tree. It is the event whose causes the analysis seeks to determine. Example: "Reactor core temperature exceeds 1500 °C."
- **(b) Basic Event (1 mark):** The lowest-level component failure event represented by a **circle** in the tree. It is not decomposed further because it represents a well-understood root cause. Example: "Pump P1 bearing seizure."
- **(c) Undeveloped Event (1 mark):** A failure event represented by a **diamond** symbol whose further analysis is either unnecessary (low contribution) or unavailable (insufficient data). Example: "Software bug in controller."

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between an AND gate and an OR gate in a fault tree, giving one engineering example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | AND Gate | OR Gate |
|---|---|---|
| **Logic** | Output fails only if **all** inputs fail (1 mark). | Output fails if **any one** input fails (1 mark). |
| **Boolean form** | $Y = A \cdot B$ | $Y = A + B$ |
| **Engineering example** | A flood warning system requires both the rain sensor to fail **AND** the backup sensor to fail. | A pump fails if the motor burns out **OR** the impeller jams. (1 mark for example) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Choose ONE of the following:**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

A chemical reactor cooling system consists of two **pumps (P1 and P2)** operating in **parallel** to deliver coolant. A **temperature sensor (T)** monitors the coolant outlet. An **alarm (A)** is triggered if the temperature exceeds the safe limit.

System logic:
- The reactor overheats if the coolant flow is insufficient **AND** the alarm fails to sound.
- Coolant flow is insufficient if both pumps fail.
- Each pump fails if its motor fails OR its seal leaks.
- The alarm fails if its power supply fails OR its speaker fails.

**(a) Construct the complete Fault Tree for the Top Event "Reactor Overheats" using proper symbols and gates. (7 marks)**

**(b) Derive the Boolean structure function and identify all Minimal Cut Sets. Compute the Top Event probability if each basic event has a failure probability of 0.01 and assume independence. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Fault Tree Construction (7 marks):**

The construction follows the six steps.

**Step 1 (1 mark): Top Event defined as** "Reactor Overheats" — a single, well-stated, undesirable event.

**Step 2 (1 mark): System boundaries:**
- Mission time: 1 year of continuous operation.
- Excluded: External power grid failure (modeled as a house event).
- Included: Pumps, motor, seals, alarm, speaker.

**Step 3 (1 mark): House Event** = "External Power Supply ON" (assumed true during normal operation).

**Step 4–6 (4 marks):** Decomposition:

Level 1 — Reactor Overheats = **AND** gate of {Coolant Flow Insufficient, Alarm Fails to Sound}.
- $T = F \cdot A$ (where F = insufficient flow, A = alarm failure).

Level 2 — Coolant Flow Insufficient = **AND** gate of {P1 fails, P2 fails}.
- $F = P_1 \cdot P_2$.

Level 2 (Alarm) — Alarm Fails = **OR** gate of {Power Supply Fails, Speaker Fails}.
- $A = S_{ps} + S_{sp}$.

Level 3 — Each pump fails = **OR** gate of {Motor Fails, Seal Leaks}.
- $P_1 = M_1 + L_1$ and $P_2 = M_2 + L_2$.

The tree is represented below in functional form (since the textual diagram is already shown in Section 4.1 in an analogous structure, students should redraw with proper rectangle/circle/AND/OR symbols):

```
T (Rectangle) = [AND] (F, A)
F (Rectangle) = [AND] (P1, P2)
P1 (Rectangle) = [OR] (M1, L1)        M1 (Circle), L1 (Circle)
P2 (Rectangle) = [OR] (M2, L2)        M2 (Circle), L2 (Circle)
A (Rectangle) = [OR] (Sps, Ssp)        Sps (Circle), Ssp (Circle)
```

(1 mark for each correctly drawn level with appropriate gate symbol.)

---

**Part (b) — Boolean Derivation and MCS Identification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the structure function.
$$T = (P_1 \cdot P_2) \cdot A = (M_1 + L_1)(M_2 + L_2)(S_{ps} + S_{sp})$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Expand using distributive law.
First expand the two pump ORs:
$$(M_1 + L_1)(M_2 + L_2) = M_1 M_2 + M_1 L_2 + L_1 M_2 + L_1 L_2$$

Then multiply by the alarm OR:
$$T = (M_1 M_2 + M_1 L_2 + L_1 M_2 + L_1 L_2)(S_{ps} + S_{sp})$$

Expanding:
$$T = M_1 M_2 S_{ps} + M_1 M_2 S_{sp} + M_1 L_2 S_{ps} + M_1 L_2 S_{sp} + L_1 M_2 S_{ps} + L_1 M_2 S_{sp} + L_1 L_2 S_{ps} + L_1 L_2 S_{sp}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Identify Minimal Cut Sets.**

Eight Minimal Cut Sets, each of order 3:
- $K_1 = \{M_1, M_2, S_{ps}\}$
- $K_2 = \{M_1, M_2, S_{sp}\}$
- $K_3 = \{M_1, L_2, S_{ps}\}$
- $K_4 = \{M_1, L_2, S_{sp}\}$
- $K_5 = \{L_1, M_2, S_{ps}\}$
- $K_6 = \{L_1, M_2, S_{sp}\}$
- $K_7 = \{L_1, L_2, S_{ps}\}$
- $K_8 = \{L_1, L_2, S_{sp}\}$

**Step 4 (3 marks): Compute Top Event probability.**

Each cut set has probability:
$$P(K_i) = (0.01)(0.01)(0.01) = 10^{-6}$$

Using the rare-event approximation (sum of cut-set probabilities, since $10^{-6} \ll 1$):
$$P(T) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{8} P(K_i) = 8 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A reactor overheat probability of $8 \times 10^{-6}$ per mission year is acceptable for industrial use but not for nuclear-grade systems (which require $< 10^{-7}$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

An aircraft has a **dual hydraulic actuator** system providing pressure to its control surfaces. The actuator fails if **either** of two pumps fails. Each pump fails if its motor fails **OR** its check valve sticks. Additionally, a **pressure sensor** must function for the system to detect a failure. A warning light is illuminated if the sensor detects low pressure. The aircraft crashes (Top Event) if the actuators fail **AND** the warning system fails.

**(a) Construct the complete Fault Tree, identifying all events with proper symbols. (7 marks)**

**(b) Derive the structure function in SOP form and compute the Top Event probability given that each basic event has failure probability $q = 0.02$. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Key Steps):**

**Part (a) (7 marks):**
- Top Event: "Aircraft Crashes" (1 mark).
- AND gate: Actuator Fails × Warning System Fails (1 mark).
- Actuator Fails = OR(Pump 1 Fails, Pump 2 Fails) (1 mark).
- Each pump fails = OR(Motor, Check Valve) (1 mark).
- Warning System Fails = AND(Pressure Sensor Fails, Light Bulb Fails) (1 mark).
- Six basic events with circles, proper gate symbols, house event for "Power ON" (1 mark for symbol usage).
- MCS list and probability (1 mark).

**Part (b) (7 marks):**
$$T = (M_1 + C_1)(M_2 + C_2)(S + B)$$

Expanding (1 mark):
$$T = M_1 M_2 S + M_1 M_2 B + M_1 C_2 S + M_1 C_2 B + C_1 M_2 S + C_1 M_2 B + C_1 C_2 S + C_1 C_2 B$$

(8 Minimal Cut Sets, each of order 3) (1 mark).

Each cut set probability (1 mark):
$$P(K_i) = (0.02)^3 = 8 \times 10^{-6}$$

Top Event probability (3 marks):
$$P(T) \approx 8 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 6.4 \times 10^{-5}$$

This is *unacceptably high* for aviation — the designer must improve component reliability or add triple-redundancy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the independence assumption:** The formula $P(T) = \sum P(K_i)$ is valid *only* for independent cut sets with low probabilities. Always state the assumption (1 mark reserved in 14-mark problems).
> 2. **Misclassifying AND vs. OR:** Students often draw an OR gate where an AND is required (e.g., a series system requiring both components to function). Re-read the problem statement twice.
> 3. **Incomplete decomposition:** Stopping at intermediate events without reaching basic events (circles) costs 1–2 marks. Every branch must terminate in a circle, diamond, or house event.
> 4. **Not writing the structure function explicitly:** A common 1-mark deduction. Always translate your tree into Boolean algebra before expanding.
> 5. **Top Event ambiguity:** "System fails" is *not* acceptable as a top event. Specify: voltage, flow rate, time threshold, etc.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Fault-Tree Construction:**
> - Fault-Tree Construction is **deductive** (top-down) — start with the consequence, work to causes.
> - The **Top Event** must be a single, precisely worded, undesirable event with a defined mission time and boundary.
> - **AND gate** = all inputs must fail; **OR gate** = any one input suffices.
> - **Rectangle** = intermediate/top event, **Circle** = basic event, **Diamond** = undeveloped event, **House** = normal event.
> - The six construction steps are: (1) Top Event, (2) Boundaries, (3) House events, (4) Level-1 causes, (5) Decompose with gates, (6) Stop at basic events.
> - **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** = smallest set of basic events whose simultaneous failure causes the top event. Lower order = more critical.
> - Boolean structure function: $\Phi = \bigcup K_i$ (OR of cut sets) where each $K_i$ is an AND of its events.
