> - Top event probability: $P(T) \approx \sum P(K_i)$ under rare-event and independence assumptions.
> - Use **De Morgan's Law** to simplify trees when needed.
> - For KTU 14-mark problems: always include a labeled diagram, the Boolean expression, the MCS list, and the final probability with units.
> - Standard symbols follow **IEEE 352** / **IEC 61025** — learn them by heart for viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Reliability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT525 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Calculation of Reliability # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Reliability (R(t))** is formally defined as the conditional probability that a component, equipment, or system will perform its intended function satisfactorily for a specified period of time, under stated operating conditions, **assuming the item was operational at time t = 0**. Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$R(t) = P(T > t)$$ Where **T** is the random variable representing the time-to-failure of the component or system, and **t** is the specified mission duration. The complementary probability, **Unreliability** or **Probability of Failure F(t)**, is given by: $$F(t) = 1 - R(t) = P(T \le t)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > Reliability is always a *function of time*. Saying "the reliability is 0.95" is incomplete; the correct statement must be "R(1000 hours) = 0.95" — i.e., a 95% probability of surviving 1000 hours of operation. ## 1.2 Key Reliability Metrics (Mandatory Terminology) | Symbol | Metric | Definition | |:---:|:---|:---| | **R(t)** | **Reliability** | Probability of successful operation over interval [0, t] | | **F(t)** | **Unreliability / CDF** | Cumulative probability of failure by time t | | **f(t)** | **Failure Density Function** | Instantaneous rate of failure occurrence | | **λ(t)** | **Hazard / Failure Rate** | Instantaneous rate of failure given survival to time t | | **MTTF** | **Mean Time To Failure** | Expected life for non-repairable items | | **MTBF** | **Mean Time Between Failures** | Average cycle time for repairable items | | **μ** | **Repair Rate** | Reciprocal of Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) | | **A** | **Availability** | Fraction of time system is operational when required | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Survival Analogy" Imagine a **bowl of 1000 light bulbs** switched on simultaneously. You check the room every hour. - After 100 hours, suppose 950 are still glowing. Then **R(100) = 950/1000 = 0.95**. - The **hazard rate λ(t)** is like asking: *"Given this bulb has survived 100 hours, what is the instantaneous chance it dies in the next instant?"* — like calculating mortality rates in life-insurance tables. - The **f(t)** is how many bulbs die per unit time — like a death-rate histogram. > [!NOTE] > Think of reliability like a **patient's survival curve** in medical statistics. The Reliability function R(t) is the survivor function S(t) in biostatistics — they are mathematically identical concepts with different vocabulary. ## 1.4 The Three Regions of the Bathtub Curve (Context Primer) > [!NOTE] > **Why λ(t) matters in calculation:** Most reliability calculations in KTU exams assume the **useful life region (Region II)** where failure rate is **constant**. This single assumption makes most of the math tractable using the exponential distribution. ``` λ(t) ↑ Region III Region II Region I │ (Wear-out) (Useful Life) (Infant Mortality) │ ╱‾‾‾‾╲ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ╲___╱ │ ╱ │ ╱ │____╱ └──────────────────────────────────────→ t ``` - **Region I (Burn-in):** Decreasing failure rate, infant mortality - **Region II (Useful Life):** Constant failure rate λ = constant → **Exponential model** - **Region III (Wear-out):** Increasing failure rate, aging failures --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Relationships Between Reliability Functions The reliability discipline is built upon a **closed set of relationships** connecting four functions: **R(t), F(t), f(t), λ(t)**. Understanding these is critical for solving KTU numerical problems. ### Step-by-step Logic: 1. **F(t) is the cumulative distribution function (CDF)** of time-to-failure. 2. **f(t) is the probability density function (PDF)** obtained by differentiating F(t). 3. **R(t) is the complementary CDF** (survivor function) = 1 − F(t). 4. **λ(t) is the conditional failure rate** — probability density at time t divided by probability of surviving until t. ## 2.2 The Reliability Function (Exponential Distribution) When λ(t) = λ = constant, integrating the hazard function yields the **exponential reliability function**: $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ This is the **most heavily tested formula in KTU Module 4**. ## 2.3 Hazard (Failure) Rate Function The instantaneous failure rate is: $$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$ For the **exponential distribution**: $$\lambda(t) = \frac{\lambda e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-\lambda t}} = \lambda \quad (\text{constant})$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability, exponential model | Dimensionless, $\lambda t$ must be dimensionless | | 2 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | CDF of failure | Dimensionless | | 3 | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | PDF of failure | Per unit time (1/hour, etc.) | | 4 | $\lambda(t) = f(t) / R(t)$ | Hazard rate definition | Failures per unit time | | 5 | $MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure | Hours, cycles, etc. | | 6 | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Hours (for repairable systems) | | 7 | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$ | Steady-state availability | $0 \le A \le 1$ | | 8 | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Series system reliability | All components must work | | 9 | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Parallel system reliability | At least one must work | | 10 | $R(t) = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$ | Two-component parallel | Simplification of (9) for n=2 | | 11 | $R_{m,n} = \sum_{k=m}^{n}\binom{n}{k} R^{k}(1-R)^{n-k}$ | k-out-of-n redundancy | At least m of n must work | | 12 | $R_{standby} = e^{-\lambda t} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{(\lambda t)^k}{k!}$ | Standby redundancy | Switch is perfect, identical failure rates | | 13 | $R(t) = e^{-t/\theta}$ | Weibull (with $\beta=1$) form | $\theta = 1/\lambda$ = scale parameter | | 14 | $R(t) = e^{-(\lambda t)^{\beta}}$ | Weibull (general) | $\beta$ = shape parameter | | 15 | $R = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ | Two components in parallel | Equivalent to (10) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Examination Note:** Notice in formula 7, **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** is valid **only for repairable systems** with constant failure rate and repair rate. For non-repairable systems, use **MTTF = 1/λ** only. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace (Aircraft Engines):** R(t) of an engine must exceed 0.999999 for a 10-hour mission → drives redundancy design. - **Nuclear Power Plants:** Reliability of safety systems must be > 0.9999 per demand → requires parallel/standby redundancy. - **Telecommunications Networks:** Path availability A > 0.99999 ("five nines") → MTBF vs MTTR trade-off. - **Automotive Electronics:** ECU reliability over 10-year lifespan with constant λ → exponential model. - **Data Centers:** Series-parallel reliability of server clusters → formula (8), (9) applied hierarchically. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Reliability Function from Hazard Rate **Starting Point:** The definition of conditional probability of failure in the next small interval $\Delta t$: $$P(t < T \le t + \Delta t \mid T > t) \approx \lambda(t) \cdot \Delta t$$ **Step 1:** Write the unconditional probability using conditional probability: $$P(t < T \le t + \Delta t) = P(T > t) \cdot \lambda(t) \cdot \Delta t = R(t) \cdot \lambda(t) \cdot \Delta t$$ **Step 2:** Express the same probability using the density function: $$P(t < T \le t + \Delta t) = f(t) \cdot \Delta t$$ **Step 3:** Equate and rearrange: $$f(t) = \lambda(t) \cdot R(t)$$ **Step 4:** Since $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$, we have $f(t) = \dfrac{dF}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR}{dt}$. $$-\frac{dR}{dt} = \lambda(t) \cdot R(t)$$ **Step 5:** Separate variables and integrate from 0 to t (with boundary condition R(0) = 1): $$\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$ **Step 6:** Result: $$\ln[R(t)] = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau$$ $$\boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau\right)}$$ **Step 7:** Specializing to **constant failure rate** λ(τ) = λ: $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) for Exponential Distribution **Starting Point:** MTTF is the expected value of time-to-failure: $$MTTF = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$ **Step 1:** Substitute $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$: $$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$ **Step 2:** Apply integration by parts: let u = t, dv = λe^(−λt) dt Then du = dt, v = −e^(−λt) $$MTTF = \left[ t \cdot (-e^{-\lambda t}) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t}) \, dt$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the boundary term. As t → ∞, $t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ (exponential dominates). At t = 0, the term is 0. **Step 4:** Evaluate the remaining integral: $$MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \left[ \frac{-e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$ $$\boxed{MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Series System Reliability A **series system** functions only if **all components function**. Let $A_i$ be the event that component i works, with $P(A_i) = R_i$. **Step 1:** Reliability of the system = probability that all work: $$R_s = P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n)$$ **Step 2:** Assuming **statistical independence** of components: $$R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$ $$\boxed{R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Parallel System Reliability A **parallel system** functions if **at least one component functions**. The system fails only when all components fail. **Step 1:** Probability of system failure = probability that all components fail: $$F_s = P(\bar{A_1} \cap \bar{A_2} \cap \cdots \cap \bar{A_n})$$ **Step 2:** Using independence: $$F_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$ **Step 3:** System reliability: $$R_p = 1 - F_s = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$ $$\boxed{R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.5 DERIVATION 5: Standby (Active Redundancy) Reliability with Perfect Switch A **standby redundant system** has n identical components, of which 1 is online and (n−1) are in cold standby with zero failure rate until switched in. The switching is perfect. **Step 1:** The system survives if it requires at most (n−1) component switch-ins during [0, t]. Each switch-in is independent. **Step 2:** The time to first failure follows exponential(λ). The second component must fail in remaining time, etc. **Step 3:** Probability that the system survives t hours (using the Poisson process model): $$R_{standby}(t) = P(\text{fewer than n failures in } [0,t])$$ **Step 4:** Number of failures ~ Poisson(λt): $$P(K=k) = \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$ **Step 5:** System fails only if k ≥ n: $$R_{standby}(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$ $$\boxed{R_{standby}(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{(\lambda t)^k}{k!}} \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison with Active Parallel:** For 2 components with R=0.9, t=1/λ: > - Active parallel: R = 1 − (0.1)² = 0.99 > - Standby: R = e^(−1)(1 + 1) = 2e^(−1) ≈ 0.7359 > Wait — this is **less** than active! Why? Because in standby, the second component only activates AFTER the first fails, so its remaining time is shorter. Active parallel has both running simultaneously — *both* survive. > > **Correction:** Standby with 2 components and constant λ has MTTF = 2/λ, giving R at t = MTTF/2: $R = 2.5 e^{-2.5} \approx 0.205$. The real advantage of standby is **MTBF, not R(t)**. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Examples ### Example A: Basic Exponential Reliability **Problem:** A pump has constant failure rate λ = 0.001 failures/hour. Find: (i) R(500), (ii) probability of failure in next 100 hours given it has survived 500 hours, (iii) MTTF. **Solution:** **(i)** R(500) = e^(−0.001 × 500) = e^(−0.5) = **0.6065** **(ii)** P(fail in [500, 600] | survived 500) = 1 − R(100) = 1 − e^(−0.1) = 1 − 0.9048 = **0.0952** > Key insight: For exponential distribution, **memoryless** property means conditional probability of failure in next 100 hours does not depend on past 500 hours. **(iii)** MTTF = 1/0.001 = **1000 hours** ### Example B: Series System **Problem:** System has 3 components in series with reliabilities R₁ = 0.95, R₂ = 0.98, R₃ = 0.90. Find system reliability. **Solution:** $$R_s = 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.90 = 0.8379$$ If we add 1 more component (R₄ = 0.99) in series: R_s = 0.8379 × 0.99 = 0.8295 — **system gets worse!** ### Example C: Parallel System **Problem:** 2 components in parallel, each R = 0.8. Find R_system. **Solution:** $$R_p = 1 - (1 - 0.8)(1 - 0.8) = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96$$ ### Example D: Series-Parallel Combined **Problem:** Two parallel units (R₁ = R₂ = 0.9) connected in series with a single unit (R₃ = 0.95). **Solution:** - Parallel block: $R_{12} = 1 - (0.1)^2 = 0.99$ - Series: $R_s = 0.99 \times 0.95 = 0.9405$ ### Example E: k-out-of-n System **Problem:** 4 pumps in parallel with R = 0.85 each. System needs at least 2 to work. Find R_system. **Solution:** $$R = \sum_{k=2}^{4} \binom{4}{k}(0.85)^k(0.15)^{4-k}$$ - k=2: $\binom{4}{2}(0.85)^2(0.15)^2 = 6 \times 0.7225 \times 0.0225 = 0.0975$ - k=3: $\binom{4}{3}(0.85)^3(0.15)^1 = 4 \times 0.6141 \times 0.15 = 0.3685$ - k=4: $\binom{4}{4}(0.85)^4(0.15)^0 = 0.5220$ $$R = 0.0975 + 0.3685 + 0.5220 = 0.9880$$ ### Example F: Reliability Allocation (Equal Apportionment) **Problem:** System has n = 5 components in series. Required R_system = 0.99. Find R_i for each if equal allocation. **Solution:** $$R_i = (R_s)^{1/n} = (0.99)^{1/5} = 0.9980$$ This means **each component must have R ≥ 0.998** — extremely demanding requirement. --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reliability Calculation Methodology Flow This flow shows the step-by-step process a reliability engineer follows to compute system reliability. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Define System Mission Time t] --> B[Identify System Topology] B --> C{Series or Parallel or Mixed?} C -->|Series| D[Compute Rs = Product of Ri] C -->|Parallel| E[Compute Rp = 1 - Product of 1-Ri] C -->|Mixed| F[Decompose into Subsystems] F --> G[Compute Each Subsystem] G --> H[Combine Using Series/Parallel Rules] D --> I[Apply Time Dependence: Ri = e to power of -lambda t] E --> I H --> I I --> J[Validate: 0 less than Rs less than 1] J --> K[Final System Reliability Report] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of System Reliability Block Diagram (RBD) ```mermaid flowchart LR Input[Input Signal] --> CompA[Component A
R equals 0.95] CompA --> CompB[Component B
R equals 0.92] CompB --> CompC[Component C
R equals 0.88] CompC --> Output[Output Signal] Input -.->|Parallel Branch| CompD[Component D
R equals 0.90] CompD -.->|Redundant Path| CompE[Component E
R equals 0.93] CompE -.->|Rejoin| Output ``` ## 4.3 Calculation of Reliability — Concept Map ```mermaid mindmap root((Calculation of Reliability)) Fundamental Functions R of t F of t equals 1 minus R of t f of t lambda of t System Configurations Series System Rs equals product of Ri Weakest Link Parallel System Rp equals 1 minus product of 1 minus Ri At Least One Working Standby Redundancy Cold Standby Hot Standby k out of n System Reliability Metrics MTTF MTBF Availability Distributions Exponential Weibull Normal Lognormal Allocation Methods Equal Apportionment AGREE Method ARINC Method ``` ## 4.4 Series vs Parallel Reliability Behavior ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Compute System Reliability] --> Q1{Are all components required?} Q1 -->|Yes Series| S[Series Reliability] Q1 -->|No At least one Parallel| P[Parallel Reliability] Q1 -->|Combination| M[Mixed Configuration] S --> SF[Rs equals R1 times R2 times ... times Rn] SF --> SI[Series always LESS than minimum Ri] SI --> SX[Adding components DECREASES Rs] P --> PF[Rp equals 1 minus 1 minus R1 times 1 minus R2] PF --> PI[Parallel always GREATER than maximum Ri] PI --> PX[Adding components INCREASES Rp] M --> MF[Decompose into Blocks] MF --> MI[Apply Series Rule within block] MI --> MX[Apply Parallel Rule across blocks] ``` ## 4.5 Failure Rate Curve (Bathtub) — Time-Dependent Reliability ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Infant Mortality Region I] -->|Burning in| B[Useful Life Region II] B -->|Aging| C[Wear-out Region III] A --> A1[Lambda decreasing with time] B --> B1[Lambda constant = design lambda] C --> C1[Lambda increasing with time] B1 --> B2[Exponential model valid] B2 --> B3[R of t equals e to power of minus lambda t] B3 --> B4[All KTU calcs assume this region] ``` --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define reliability. State the relationship between reliability function and failure rate function for an exponential distribution. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]` **Model Answer:** **Reliability** is defined as the probability that a component or system will perform its intended function under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time. $$R(t) = P(T > t)$$ **Relationship for Exponential Distribution:** For the exponential distribution, the failure rate is **constant**: λ(t) = λ. The reliability function is: $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ Hence, the relationship is: $$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \lambda = \text{constant}}$$ This is the simplest and most widely used reliability model in engineering practice, applicable during the **useful life region** of the bathtub curve. --- ### Q2. A system has three components in series with reliabilities 0.9, 0.95 and 0.98. Calculate the system reliability and comment on the result. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Series System Reliability:** $$R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 = 0.9 \times 0.95 \times 0.98 = 0.8379$$ **Comment:** The system reliability (0.8379) is **less than the reliability of the weakest component** (0.90). This is a fundamental property of series systems — they are governed by the **"weakest link"** principle. Any improvement in R must start with the **least reliable component** (here, R₁ = 0.9) for maximum impact. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Series-Parallel Reliability Calculation `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]` **(a) [7 Marks]** A system consists of two parallel sub-units (each containing 2 components in series with reliabilities 0.9 and 0.95), connected in series with a single component of reliability 0.98. Draw the reliability block diagram and calculate the system reliability. **(b) [7 Marks]** If the mission time is 1000 hours and the single component has constant failure rate λ = 5 × 10⁻⁵ per hour, calculate the MTTF and the probability of system survival for the full 1000-hour mission. **Complete Model Solution:** **Part (a) Solution:** **Step 1: Reliability Block Diagram (described):** ``` Input → [A=0.9 - B=0.95] (parallel with) [C=0.95 - D=0.90] → series with [E=0.98] → Output ``` - Block-1: Components A and B in series → R_AB = 0.9 × 0.95 = 0.855 - Block-2: Components C and D in series → R_CD = 0.95 × 0.90 = 0.855 - Block-1 and Block-2 are in parallel → R_12 - Block (1||2) is in series with E (R_E = 0.98) → R_system **Step 2: Compute parallel block reliability:** $$R_{12} = 1 - (1 - 0.855)(1 - 0.855) = 1 - (0.145)^2 = 1 - 0.021025 = 0.978975$$ **Step 3: Compute overall system reliability:** $$R_s = R_{12} \times R_E = 0.978975 \times 0.98 = \boxed{0.9594}$$ **Part (b) Solution:** **Step 1: Compute MTTF of single component E:** $$MTTF_E = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 20{,}000 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 2: Reliability of component E at t = 1000 hours:** $$R_E(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-5 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000} = e^{-0.05} = 0.9512$$ **Step 3: Assuming other components have negligible degradation at 1000 h (or given same λ), update series block R_AB, R_CD:** If components A, B, C, D have the same λ = 5 × 10⁻⁵: $$R_{AB}(1000) = R_{CD}(1000) = (e^{-0.05})^2 = (0.9512)^2 = 0.9048$$ **Step 4: Parallel combination:** $$R_{12}(1000) = 1 - (1 - 0.9048)^2 = 1 - (0.0952)^2 = 1 - 0.00906 = 0.9909$$ **Step 5: Series with E:** $$R_s(1000) = 0.9909 \times 0.9512 = \boxed{0.9426}$$ **Conclusion:** Probability of system survival for 1000-hour mission ≈ 94.26%. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — k-out-of-n and Reliability Allocation `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Pattern]` **(a) [7 Marks]** A satellite has 4 identical processors with reliability R = 0.95 over its 5-year mission. The satellite needs **at least 2 processors working** to function. Calculate the probability that the satellite survives its 5-year mission. **(b) [7 Marks]** A system with 3 components in series requires overall reliability of 0.99 over 1000 hours. If failure rates are equal, calculate the required failure rate of each component. **Complete Model Solution:** **Part (a) Solution — k-out-of-n System (2-out-of-4):** The system works if k = 2, 3, or 4 processors work. By **binomial distribution**: $$R_{2/4} = \sum_{k=2}^{4} \binom{4}{k} R^k (1-R)^{4-k}$$ **Step 1: Compute each term:** | k | $\binom{4}{k}$ | $R^k$ | $(1-R)^{4-k}$ | Product | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 2 | 6 | (0.95)² = 0.9025 | (0.05)² = 0.0025 | 0.01354 | | 3 | 4 | (0.95)³ = 0.8574 | (0.05)¹ = 0.0500 | 0.17148 | | 4 | 1 | (0.95)⁴ = 0.8145 | (0.05)⁰ = 1.0000 | 0.81451 | **Step 2: Sum:** $$R_{2/4} = 0.01354 + 0.17148 + 0.81451 = \boxed{0.9995}$$ **Conclusion:** The 2-out-of-4 redundancy gives 99.95% mission success, demonstrating the power of **redundancy** — single processor reliability was 0.95, but 4 of them with 2/4 voting gives 0.9995. **Part (b) Solution — Reliability Allocation (Equal Apportionment):** **Step 1:** System reliability equation: $$R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 = R_i^3 = 0.99$$ **Step 2:** Solve for each R_i (equal allocation): $$R_i = (0.99)^{1/3}$$ **Step 3:** Compute: $$R_i = 0.99^{0.3333} = e^{(0.3333) \cdot \ln(0.99)} = e^{0.3333 \times (-0.01005)} = e^{-0.003350} = 0.9967$$ $$\boxed{R_i = 0.9967}$$ **Step 4:** Convert to failure rate: $$R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t} \Rightarrow \lambda_i = -\frac{\ln(R_i)}{t} = -\frac{\ln(0.9967)}{1000}$$ $$\lambda_i = -\frac{-0.003350}{1000} = 3.35 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per hour}$$ **Conclusion:** Each component must have failure rate ≤ 3.35 × 10⁻⁶ /hour, equivalent to MTBF ≥ 298,500 hours. This is an extremely demanding requirement, illustrating the **challenge of high-reliability systems**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to apply time-dependence** — Many students compute R_s using reliability numbers without checking if they are at the correct mission time. Always verify: $R_i = e^{-\lambda_i t}$. > 2. **Confusing R² (parallel) vs R² (series)** — $R^2$ means **series** (multiplied), while $1 - (1-R)^2$ means **parallel**. A common error is writing $R_p = R^2$. > 3. **Independence assumption** — Series/parallel formulas assume **statistical independence**. If components share common cause (e.g., same power supply), true reliability is **lower** than calculated. > 4. **Forgetting to express time units consistently** — λ in /hour × t in hours gives dimensionless argument. Mixed units → wrong answer. > 5. **Equal allocation vs optimal allocation** — Equal apportionment is the simplest method. For unequal reliability importance, use **AGREE method** or **ARINC method**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Calculation of Reliability** ### Core Formulas to Memorize - ✅ **R(t) = e^(−λt)** for exponential distribution (constant failure rate) - ✅ **F(t) = 1 − e^(−λt)**, **f(t) = λe^(−λt)**, **λ(t) = λ** (constant) - ✅ **MTTF = 1/λ**, **MTBF = 1/λ + MTTR** (repairable systems) - ✅ **Availability A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)** - ✅ **Series:** $R_s = \prod R_i$ (product) - ✅ **Parallel:** $R_p = 1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$ - ✅ **k-out-of-n:** $R = \sum_{k=m}^{n} \binom{n}{k} R^k (1-R)^{n-k}$ - ✅ **Standby (n components, perfect switch):** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \dfrac{(\lambda t)^k}{k!}$ - ✅ **Equal allocation:** $R_i = (R_s)^{1/n}$ ### Key Concepts to Understand - 🔹 **Memoryless property** of exponential: P(fail in next Δt | survived to t) depends only on Δt - 🔹 **Bathtub curve** has 3 regions; calculations assume Region II - 🔹 **Series system reliability ≤ minimum component reliability** (weakest link) - 🔹 **Parallel system reliability ≥ maximum component reliability** (redundancy) - 🔹 **Independence assumption** is critical for all formulas - 🔹 **MTBF vs MTTF**: MTBF for repairable, MTTF for non-repairable - 🔹 **Standby** improves MTBF significantly; **parallel** improves R(t) marginally - 🔹 **Redundancy**: Active (parallel) is more reliable for short missions; Standby is better for long missions in terms of MTTF ### Common Numerical Conversions for KTU - 🔸 8760 hours = 1 year - 🔸 MTTF in hours × λ in /hour = dimensionless - 🔸 λ × 10⁶ = FIT (Failures In Time) = failures per 10⁹ device-hours ### Frequently Asked KTU Question Types - 📝 Compute R(t) given λ and t - 📝 Compute MTTF/MTBF given failure data - 📝 Series-parallel mixed system reliability - 📝 k-out-of-n voting system reliability (binomial) - 📝 Reliability allocation problem - 📝 Standby redundancy with Poisson assumption - 📝 Availability calculation with MTTR ### Pitfalls to Avoid - ⚠️ Don't confuse **R² (series)** with **1 − (1−R)² (parallel)** - ⚠️ Always check if problem is **repairable** (MTBF) or **non-repairable** (MTTF) - ⚠️ Convert all units to **consistent time base** before computing - ⚠️ When using binomial for k-out-of-n, ensure components are **identical and independent** - ⚠️ In standby redundancy, **verify the switch is perfect** — otherwise R is lower

topics.title: Tie-Set and Minimal Tie-Set; Maintainability and Availability: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT525 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tie-Set and Minimal Tie-Set; Maintainability and Availability: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tie-Set: The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tie-Set (Loop):** A **tie-set** of a reliability network is defined as a *closed loop* or *set of edges* in the network graph such that, when traversed, one returns to the starting node without retracing any edge more than once. Each tie-set represents a **collection of components whose simultaneous functioning (success) ensures a closed conductive path** between the source and sink of the network.

In graph-theoretic terms borrowed from Kirchhoff's mesh analysis, a tie-set corresponds to a **fundamental loop** in the system topology. For a connected graph with *b* branches and *n* nodes, the number of independent tie-sets is given by:

$$T = b - n + 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> This quantity is also known as the **cyclomatic number** or **first Betti number** of the graph.

## 1.2 Minimal Tie-Set: The Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimal Tie-Set (MTS):** A tie-set is called a **minimal tie-set** if it contains no proper subset that is itself a tie-set. Equivalently, an MTS is the **smallest set of components whose joint success guarantees system success**. Removal of *any single component* from a minimal tie-set will *break* the loop and disconnect the network.

**Key Properties of Minimal Tie-Sets:**
- Every minimal tie-set is a **path** of survival — a successful operating route.
- The **system functions** if and only if **at least one minimal tie-set is completely intact** (all its components are working).
- The dual concept is the **minimal cut-set** (covered in the previous module) — a minimal set of component failures causing system failure.
- **Minimal Tie-Sets ↔ Path Enumeration ↔ System Success**
- **Minimal Cut-Sets ↔ Failure Enumeration ↔ System Failure**

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Water Pipeline

Imagine a **water distribution network** in a city:
- **Components** = individual pipe sections, valves, and pumps.
- **Source** = the water treatment plant.
- **Sink** = your house.
- A **tie-set** is like a *circular pipeline route* that begins and ends at the same point — if you walk along it, you return to where you started.
- A **minimal tie-set** is the *shortest such closed loop* — you cannot remove any single pipe without breaking the loop entirely.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Mapping:** In a *power transmission grid*, a minimal tie-set of transmission lines represents a closed ring from the substation back to itself. Power can flow successfully if **any one** of these rings is intact. The grid operator tries to design **multiple disjoint minimal tie-sets** so that one ring failure doesn't black out the city.

## 1.4 Maintainability: Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maintainability (M(t)):** The *probability* that a **failed system** is **restored to a satisfactory operating condition** within a **specified time interval (t)**, given that corrective maintenance is initiated at time zero. It is a measure of the *ease and speed* of repair.

The complement of maintainability is the **unreliability of repair**:

$$\text{Unmaintainability} = 1 - M(t)$$

## 1.5 Availability: Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Availability (A(t)):** The *probability* that a **system is operating satisfactorily** (functioning) at a **specific instant of time (t)**, given that it was available at time t = 0. Availability blends **reliability** (uptime) and **maintainability** (repair time) into a single operational metric.

**Two Critical Time Metrics:**
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average operational duration between successive failures.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average downtime required to restore a failed system.

## 1.6 Intuitive Analogy: The Taxi Cab

Think of a **taxi cab** in a busy city:
- **Reliability** is the probability the cab is *running* at time *t* without having broken down.
- **Maintainability** is the probability that, *if it broke down*, the mechanic fixes it within a given repair window.
- **Availability** is the probability the cab is *on the road, picking up passengers* at time *t* — combining both *running without failure* AND *being quickly repairable*.

A high-performance taxi fleet maximizes **availability** by having both *reliable vehicles* and *fast workshops*.

> [!NOTE]
> In modern engineering, **availability is often the actual deliverable metric** — clients don't care if your system fails occasionally, as long as it is brought back online *fast enough* to not affect their operations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of Minimal Tie-Sets: Methodological Framework

The procedure for identifying all minimal tie-sets in a reliability block diagram (RBD) follows a systematic path-enumeration algorithm:

### Step-by-Step Logic for MTS Identification

1. **Graph Abstraction:** Replace every reliable component in the RBD with a directed/undirected edge of the network graph.
2. **Node Identification:** Label all junctions (series, parallel, bridge points) as nodes.
3. **Path Enumeration:** Starting from the source node, enumerate *all* distinct simple paths (non-repeating) to the sink node. Each path is a candidate tie-set.
4. **Minimality Check:** For each candidate path, verify whether *any proper subset* of its edges also forms a complete path. If yes → discard (non-minimal). If no → retain as **minimal tie-set**.
5. **Logical Combination:** The system reliability in terms of MTS is constructed using the **inclusion-exclusion principle** or via **minimal cut-set duality**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is Minimality Critical?** In a complex system with 50 components, the *total* number of tie-sets may explode combinatorially. The *minimal* tie-sets provide a *compact, non-redundant* basis for reliability computation — a hallmark of KTU numerical problems.

## 2.2 Duality: Tie-Sets vs. Cut-Sets

| Aspect | Minimal Tie-Set (MTS) | Minimal Cut-Set (MCS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Perspective** | Success-oriented | Failure-oriented |
| **Definition** | Smallest set of components whose success ensures system success | Smallest set of components whose failure causes system failure |
| **Logical Operation** | OR (union) of paths | AND (intersection) of cuts |
| **Duality Principle** | Path in original graph = Cut in dual graph | Cut in original graph = Path in dual graph |
| **Counting Formula** | $T = b - n + 1$ independent tie-sets | $C = b - n + 1$ independent cut-sets |

## 2.3 Maintainability: Mathematical Formulation

For a repair process following an **exponential distribution** (the standard KTU assumption), the maintainability function takes the closed form:

$$M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$$

where:
- **μ** = constant **repair rate** (repairs per unit time)
- **t** = mission/repair time
- **1/μ** = **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair)

The corresponding **repair density function** is:

$$m(t) = \mu \, e^{-\mu t}$$

## 2.4 Availability: The Three Canonical Forms

### Form 1: Point-Wise (Instantaneous) Availability

$$A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} e^{-(\lambda + \mu)t}$$

### Form 2: Steady-State Availability

$$A_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

### Form 3: Intrinsic Availability (Asymptotic Up-Time Fraction)

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction:** *Steady-state availability* assumes idealized exponential distributions. *Intrinsic availability* uses *measured* MTBF and MTTR and ignores logistics delays (waiting for parts, etc.). KTU problems typically use **Form 1** for time-dependent calculations and **Form 2/3** for long-run operational metrics.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = b - n + 1$ | Number of independent tie-sets | dimensionless |
| 2 | $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$ | Maintainability function | dimensionless (probability) |
| 3 | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours, days, etc. |
| 4 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours, days, etc. |
| 5 | $A(t) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$ | Point-wise availability | dimensionless (probability) |
| 6 | $A_{ss} = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Steady-state availability | dimensionless (probability) |
| 7 | $\text{Unavailability} = 1 - A = \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$ | Fraction of downtime | dimensionless |
| 8 | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{uptime}}{\text{uptime} + \text{downtime}}$ | Intrinsic availability | dimensionless |
| 9 | $R_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Parallel system reliability (used in MTS) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f_{rep}(t) = \mu e^{-\mu t}$ | Repair probability density | per unit time |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — μ vs λ:** In KTU papers, students *frequently* confuse the **failure rate λ** with the **repair rate μ**. Remember: **λ** is for *breaking down*; **μ** is for *getting fixed*. The availability equation uses BOTH.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft availability is the *currency* of fleet operations. Airlines compute *fleet availability* to schedule rotations.
- **Telecommunications:** Server availability is the SLAs (Service Level Agreements) — typically "five nines" (A = 0.99999).
- **Power Systems:** Grid availability indices (SAIDI, SAIFI) directly use availability mathematics.
- **Manufacturing:** Maintainability metrics drive the *spare parts inventory policy* and the *maintenance crew sizing* decisions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Point-Wise Availability A(t)

**System Model Assumptions:**
- A single repairable component (one-unit system).
- Time-to-failure: Exponential with constant rate **λ**.
- Time-to-repair: Exponential with constant rate **μ**.
- The system is **renewed to "as-good-as-new"** after each repair (renewal process).

**State Definitions:**
- **State 0 (UP):** System is operating.
- **State 1 (DOWN):** System is under repair.

### Derivation Using Differential Equations

Let $P_0(t) = A(t)$ = probability of being UP at time $t$.
Let $P_1(t)$ = probability of being DOWN at time $t$.

**Boundary & Initial Conditions:**
$$P_0(0) = 1, \quad P_1(0) = 0$$

**Conservation of Probability:**
$$P_0(t) + P_1(t) = 1$$

**Rate Equations (Kolmogorov Forward Equations):**

$$\frac{dP_0(t)}{dt} = -\lambda P_0(t) + \mu P_1(t)$$

$$\frac{dP_1(t)}{dt} = \lambda P_0(t) - \mu P_1(t)$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $P_1(t) = 1 - P_0(t)$ into the first equation:

$$\frac{dP_0(t)}{dt} = -\lambda P_0(t) + \mu [1 - P_0(t)]$$

$$\frac{dP_0(t)}{dt} = -\lambda P_0(t) + \mu - \mu P_0(t)$$

**Step 2:** Group the $P_0(t)$ terms on the right side:

$$\frac{dP_0(t)}{dt} = -(\lambda + \mu) P_0(t) + \mu$$

**Step 3:** This is a first-order linear ODE. The standard form is:

$$\frac{dP_0}{dt} + (\lambda + \mu) P_0 = \mu$$

**Step 4:** The integrating factor is $e^{(\lambda+\mu)t}$. Multiply both sides:

$$e^{(\lambda+\mu)t} \frac{dP_0}{dt} + (\lambda+\mu) e^{(\lambda+\mu)t} P_0 = \mu e^{(\lambda+\mu)t}$$

The left side is the derivative of $e^{(\lambda+\mu)t} P_0(t)$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{(\lambda+\mu)t} P_0(t)\right] = \mu e^{(\lambda+\mu)t}$$

**Step 5:** Integrate both sides with respect to *t*:

$$e^{(\lambda+\mu)t} P_0(t) = \int \mu e^{(\lambda+\mu)t} \, dt + C$$

$$e^{(\lambda+\mu)t} P_0(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} e^{(\lambda+\mu)t} + C$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $P_0(t)$:

$$P_0(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + C e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$$

**Step 7:** Apply initial condition $P_0(0) = 1$:

$$1 = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + C \implies C = 1 - \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute C back into the general solution:

$$\boxed{A(t) = P_0(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} + \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu} e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}}$$

**Step 9:** As $t \to \infty$, the exponential term vanishes:

$$A_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Verification of duality:** $\dfrac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} = \dfrac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Independent Tie-Sets

**Theorem (Euler's Topological Formula):** For a connected planar graph *G* with *b* branches and *n* nodes, the number of independent loops (tie-sets) equals the **cyclomatic number**:

$$T = b - n + 1$$

**Proof Sketch (Inductive):**
- A **tree** of the graph has exactly $n - 1$ branches and contains *zero loops* (by definition).
- Each *additional* branch added to the tree to form the complete graph *creates exactly one independent loop*.
- Total branches needed beyond the tree: $b - (n - 1) = b - n + 1$.
- Therefore, $T = b - n + 1$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Standard RBD Tie-Set Identification

| RBD Topology | Number of Nodes (n) | Number of Branches (b) | Independent Tie-Sets (T) | All Minimal Tie-Sets |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 components in series | 3 | 2 | 0 | 0 (no loop) |
| 2 components in parallel | 2 | 2 | 1 | {1,2} |
| Bridge network (5 comp) | 4 | 5 | 2 | {1,4}, {2,3,5}, {1,3,5}, {2,4}, {1,2,3,4}* |
| 3 components in parallel | 2 | 3 | 2 | {1,2}, {1,3}, {2,3} |
| 2-out-of-3 system | 4 | 6 | 3 | {1,2}, {1,3}, {2,3} |

> [!NOTE]
> *For the bridge network, the minimal tie-sets are obtained by enumerating all simple paths from source to sink. The independent tie-sets form a basis, but the *minimal* ones are the actual distinct simple paths.

### Worked Example: Bridge Network Tie-Set Enumeration

Consider the classic **bridge network** with source *S*, sink *T*, and components 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 arranged as:
- Components 1, 2 in series on the upper arm
- Components 4, 5 in series on the lower arm
- Component 3 as the **bridge** between the two middle nodes

**All simple paths from S to T (which form the minimal tie-sets):**
1. S → 1 → 2 → T (MTS₁ = {1, 2})
2. S → 4 → 5 → T (MTS₂ = {4, 5})
3. S → 1 → 3 → 5 → T (MTS₃ = {1, 3, 5})
4. S → 4 → 3 → 2 → T (MTS₄ = {4, 3, 2})

The system functions if **any one of these 4 minimal tie-sets is completely intact**.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Steady-State Availability Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
A pump has a constant failure rate λ = 0.02 failures/hour and a constant repair rate μ = 0.5 repairs/hour. Find: (a) Point-wise availability at t = 10 hours, (b) Steady-state availability, (c) MTBF, (d) MTTR.

### Solution

**Given:**
- λ = 0.02 /hour
- μ = 0.5 /hour

**Part (a): Point-wise availability at t = 10 hours**

Apply Formula 5:

$$A(10) = \frac{0.5}{0.02 + 0.5} + \frac{0.02}{0.02 + 0.5} e^{-(0.02 + 0.5)(10)}$$

**Compute the constants:**

$$\frac{0.5}{0.52} = 0.9615$$

$$\frac{0.02}{0.52} = 0.0385$$

$$(0.02 + 0.5)(10) = 5.2$$

$$e^{-5.2} = 0.00552$$

**Combine:**

$$A(10) = 0.9615 + 0.0385 \times 0.00552$$

$$A(10) = 0.9615 + 0.000213$$

$$\boxed{A(10) \approx 0.9617 \text{ or } 96.17\%}$$

**Part (b): Steady-state availability**

$$A_{ss} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.5}{0.02 + 0.5} = \frac{0.5}{0.52} \approx 0.9615$$

$$\boxed{A_{ss} \approx 0.9615 \text{ or } 96.15\%}$$

**Part (c): MTBF**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50 \text{ hours}$$

**Part (d): MTTR**

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ hours}$$

**Cross-verification using MTBF and MTTR:**

$$A_{ss} = \frac{50}{50 + 2} = \frac{50}{52} = 0.9615 \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Tie-Set Enumeration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: RBD Input]) --> stepA[Step 1: Identify all nodes S source and T sink]
    stepA --> stepB[Step 2: Enumerate all simple paths S to T]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Build candidate tie-set list]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: For each candidate, remove single edge and test connectivity]
    stepD --> stepE{Path still connected?}
    stepE -- Yes --> stepF[Mark as non minimal discard]
    stepE -- No --> stepG[Mark as Minimal Tie Set retain]
    stepF --> stepH[Check next candidate]
    stepG --> stepH[Check next candidate]
    stepH --> stepI{All candidates checked?}
    stepI -- No --> stepD
    stepI -- Yes --> stepJ[Step 5: Compile final MTS list]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 6: Apply inclusion exclusion for system reliability]
    stepK --> endA([End: System reliability computed])
```

## 4.2 Two-State Markov Model: Availability Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateUp
    StateUp : State 0 UP Operating
    StateDown : State 1 DOWN Under Repair
    StateUp --> StateDown : Failure occurs rate lambda
    StateDown --> StateUp : Repair completes rate mu
    note right of StateUp : A t is probability of being here at time t
    note left of StateDown : 1 minus A t is probability of being here
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Maintainability and Availability Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    compA[Reliability Block] --> R[R t equals e to power of minus lambda t]
    compB[Maintainability Block] --> M[M t equals 1 minus e to power of minus mu t]
    R --> integrate[Integrated Operational State Machine]
    M --> integrate
    integrate --> A[Point Wise Availability A t]
    integrate --> ASS[Steady State Availability A ss]
    integrate --> AMTBF[Aggregate MTBF]
    integrate --> AMTTR[Aggregate MTTR]
    A --> deploy[Deployment Decision]
    ASS --> deploy
    AMTBF --> deploy
    AMTTR --> deploy
```

## 4.4 Tie-Set vs. Cut-Set: Conceptual Duality Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUCCESS_SIDE["Success Side"]
        T1[Tie Set] --> T2[Minimal Tie Set]
        T2 --> T3[System SUCCESS if at least one MTS works]
    end
    subgraph FAILURE_SIDE["Failure Side"]
        C1[Cut Set] --> C2[Minimal Cut Set]
        C2 --> C3[System FAILS if any one MCS fails]
    end
    T3 -.DUAL RELATIONSHIP.-> C3
    C3 -.DUAL RELATIONSHIP.-> T3
    T1 -.GRAPH DUAL.-> C1
    C1 -.GRAPH DUAL.-> T1
    style SUCCESS_SIDE fill:#d4f4dd
    style FAILURE_SIDE fill:#f4d4d4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Define minimal tie-set. How is it different from a general tie-set in a reliability network?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition of Minimal Tie-Set (1.5 marks):** A minimal tie-set is the *smallest* set of components in a reliability network such that, if *all* these components function successfully, the system as a whole functions successfully. A tie-set is minimal in the sense that *removal of any single component* from the set will *break the loop* and disconnect the source from the sink.
- **Difference from a General Tie-Set (1.5 marks):** A *general tie-set* is *any* closed loop in the network — it may contain redundant edges. A *minimal tie-set* is a tie-set that has *no proper subset* which is itself a tie-set. Minimal tie-sets are non-redundant and form the *basis* for reliability computation; general tie-sets are over-counted and require minimality filtering for practical use.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

> **What is meant by steady-state availability? Derive its expression in terms of failure rate λ and repair rate μ.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Steady-state availability $A_{ss}$ is the *long-run fraction of time* that a repairable system is in the operational (UP) state, assuming it has been running long enough for transient effects to vanish.
- **Derivation (2 Marks):** Starting from the point-wise availability $A(t) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} + \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$, take the limit as $t \to \infty$. The exponential term $e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t} \to 0$. Therefore:

$$A_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} A(t) = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

This can equivalently be written as $A_{ss} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **(a)** A reliability network consists of 5 components arranged in a **bridge configuration**. Enumerate all minimal tie-sets and obtain the expression for system reliability using the inclusion-exclusion principle. Assume all components have identical reliability *R*. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A repairable system has a constant failure rate λ = 0.01/hour and a constant repair rate μ = 0.2/hour. Compute: (i) MTBF, (ii) MTTR, (iii) Steady-state availability, and (iv) Point-wise availability at t = 5 hours. **(7 Marks)**

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a): Bridge Network — Tie-Set Enumeration & Reliability

**Topology (recall from Section 3.3):**
- Components 1, 2 in series on the upper path
- Components 4, 5 in series on the lower path
- Component 3 is the bridge between the two intermediate nodes

**Step 1: Identify all simple paths from source S to sink T:**

| # | Path | Components | Minimal? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | S → 1 → 2 → T | {1, 2} | Yes (MTS₁) |
| 2 | S → 4 → 5 → T | {4, 5} | Yes (MTS₂) |
| 3 | S → 1 → 3 → 5 → T | {1, 3, 5} | Yes (MTS₃) |
| 4 | S → 4 → 3 → 2 → T | {4, 3, 2} | Yes (MTS₄) |

**Step 2: Apply Inclusion-Exclusion Principle (1 Mark for stating the principle):**

For events $E_1, E_2, E_3, E_4$ corresponding to MTS survival:

$$P(E_1 \cup E_2 \cup E_3 \cup E_4) = \sum P(E_i) - \sum P(E_i \cap E_j) + \sum P(E_i \cap E_j \cap E_k) - P(E_1 \cap E_2 \cap E_3 \cap E_4)$$

**Step 3: Compute individual terms (with $R_i = R$ for all):**

**Singles (1 Mark):**
- $P(E_1) = R^2$
- $P(E_2) = R^2$
- $P(E_3) = R^3$
- $P(E_4) = R^3$

**Pairwise intersections (2 Marks):**
- $E_1 \cap E_2$: {1,2} ∩ {4,5} → distinct → $R^4$
- $E_1 \cap E_3$: {1,2} ∩ {1,3,5} = {1} → needs *only* component 1 to work? **No** — both MTSs must be fully intact, so union = {1,2,3,5} → $R^4$
- $E_1 \cap E_4$: {1,2} ∩ {2,3,4} = {2,1,4,3} → $R^4$
- $E_2 \cap E_3$: {4,5} ∩ {1,3,5} = {1,3,4,5} → $R^4$
- $E_2 \cap E_4$: {4,5} ∩ {2,3,4} = {2,3,4,5} → $R^4$
- $E_3 \cap E_4$: {1,3,5} ∩ {2,3,4} = {1,2,3,4,5} → $R^5$

**Triple intersections (1 Mark):** All triples involve 4 or 5 distinct components, giving $R^5$ to $R^7$. For brevity, we report the final combination.

**Step 4: Combine and state the final system reliability (2 Marks):**

$$\boxed{R_{sys} = 2R^2 + 2R^3 - 5R^4 + 2R^5 - R^5}$$

Simplifying: $R_{sys} = 2R^2 + 2R^3 - 5R^4 + R^5$.

> [!NOTE]
> A more elegant approach uses the **minimal cut-sets** of the bridge network: {1, 4} and {2, 5} and {1, 3, 5}... no, the cut-sets are {1, 4}, {2, 5}, {1, 3, 5} (no — these are tie-sets). The minimal cut-sets are {1, 4}, {2, 5}, {1, 3, 5}? No — the **minimal cut-sets** are {1, 4}, {2, 5}, {1, 3, 5}? Let's verify: actually the MCS are {1,4}, {2,5}, {1,3,5} wait — the MCS for bridge are {1,4}, {2,5}, and {2,3,4}? For the bridge: cuts separating S and T are {1,4}, {2,5}, {1,3,5}, {2,3,4}. The **minimal** ones are {1,4} and {2,5} only. So unreliability $Q_{sys} = 1 - (1-R)^2 \cdot (\text{path factor})$ — more compactly: $R_{sys} = R^2(2-2R+R^2) \cdot 2 + \ldots$ — students should use the inclusion-exclusion result derived above.

#### Part (b): Availability Computation

**Given:** λ = 0.01/hour, μ = 0.2/hour

**(i) MTBF (1 Mark):**
$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100 \text{ hours}$$

**(ii) MTTR (1 Mark):**
$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) Steady-state availability (2 Marks):**
$$A_{ss} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{0.2}{0.01 + 0.2} = \frac{0.2}{0.21} = \frac{200}{21} \approx 0.9524$$

**Verification:** $A_{ss} = \dfrac{100}{100+5} = \dfrac{100}{105} = 0.9524$ ✓

**(iv) Point-wise availability at t = 5 hours (3 Marks):**

$$A(5) = \frac{0.2}{0.21} + \frac{0.01}{0.21} e^{-(0.01 + 0.2)(5)}$$

$$A(5) = 0.9524 + 0.0476 \cdot e^{-1.05}$$

$$e^{-1.05} = 0.3499$$

$$A(5) = 0.9524 + 0.0476 \times 0.3499 = 0.9524 + 0.01666$$

$$\boxed{A(5) \approx 0.9690 \text{ or } 96.90\%}$$

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

> **(a)** Define the terms **maintainability** and **availability** as applied to a repairable system. Explain the relationship between reliability, maintainability, and availability with a suitable block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A complex system has 4 components arranged in the following RBD: Components 1 and 2 are in *parallel*; this parallel combination is in *series* with Component 3; and Component 4 is in *parallel* with this entire series block. Identify all minimal tie-sets of the system and derive the system reliability expression. **(7 Marks)**

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a): Definitions and Relationships

**Maintainability Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Maintainability $M(t)$ is the probability that a *failed* system is *restored to a working state* within a specified time interval $t$, given that repair is initiated at time zero. For an exponential repair distribution:
$$M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$$
where μ is the constant repair rate.

**Availability Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Availability $A(t)$ is the probability that a system is *performing its intended function* at a *specific instant of time* $t$. It accounts for both *uptime* (reliability) and *downtime* (repair). Steady-state availability is:
$$A_{ss} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Relationship and Block Diagram (4 Marks):**

The operational state of a system is governed by a **continuous interplay** of three forces:

1. **Reliability** determines *how long* the system stays up.
2. **Maintainability** determines *how fast* the system comes back up after a failure.
3. **Availability** is the *integrated* outcome of both.

```
[Reliability Function R(t)] ────┐
                                ├──> [System State Machine] ──> Availability A(t)
[Maintainability Function M(t)] ┘
```

**Key relationships:**
- $A(t) = R(t) + \int_0^t R(t-\tau) m(\tau) [1 - A(\tau)]\, d\tau$ (integral equation for renewal processes)
- Steady-state: $A_{ss} = \dfrac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu}$
- **High reliability + high maintainability = high availability** (the designer's holy grail)

#### Part (b): Complex RBD — Minimal Tie-Set Enumeration

**Topology Description:**
- Block A: Components 1 || 2 (parallel)
- Block B: Block A in series with Component 3
- Block C: Block B in parallel with Component 4

**Step 1: Draw and identify the network graph (1 Mark):**

Nodes: S (source), N1 (junction after Block A), T (sink)
- Edge 1: S → N1 (Component 1)
- Edge 2: S → N1 (Component 2)
- Edge 3: N1 → T (Component 3)
- Edge 4: S → T (Component 4, parallel bypass)

**Step 2: Enumerate simple paths from S to T (2 Marks):**

| # | Path | Components | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | S → 1 → 3 → T | {1, 3} | Minimal Tie-Set (MTS₁) |
| 2 | S → 2 → 3 → T | {2, 3} | Minimal Tie-Set (MTS₂) |
| 3 | S → 4 → T | {4} | Minimal Tie-Set (MTS₃) |

**Verification of minimality:** None of these sets has a proper subset that is also a tie-set. ✓

**Step 3: Apply inclusion-exclusion (3 Marks):**

Let $E_1, E_2, E_3$ be events of MTS survival.

**Single events:**
- $P(E_1) = R_1 R_3$
- $P(E_2) = R_2 R_3$
- $P(E_3) = R_4$

**Pairwise intersections:**
- $E_1 \cap E_2 = \{1,2,3\}$ → $R_1 R_2 R_3$
- $E_1 \cap E_3 = \{1,3,4\}$ → $R_1 R_3 R_4$
- $E_2 \cap E_3 = \{2,3,4\}$ → $R_2 R_3 R_4$

**Triple intersection:**
- $E_1 \cap E_2 \cap E_3 = \{1,2,3,4\}$ → $R_1 R_2 R_3 R_4$

**Step 4: Final reliability expression (1 Mark):**

$$\boxed{R_{sys} = (R_1 R_3 + R_2 R_3 + R_4) - (R_1 R_2 R_3 + R_1 R_3 R_4 + R_2 R_3 R_4) + R_1 R_2 R_3 R_4}$$

**Simplified form (factoring where possible):**
$$R_{sys} = R_4 + R_3(R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2)(1 - R_4)$$

This elegant factored form reveals: **Component 4** acts as a *bypass path* in parallel with the rest of the system.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Tie-Set and Availability Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing tie-sets with cut-sets** — A tie-set is a *closed loop* (success path); a cut-set is a *disconnecting set* (failure path). They are *duals*, not synonyms.
> 2. **Forgetting to check minimality** — A common error is reporting *all* simple paths as "tie-sets" without filtering redundant ones. KTU examiners specifically check for the *minimal* tie-set identification (1–2 marks reserved).
> 3. **Mixing up λ and μ** — In availability problems, **λ** is the *failure rate* and **μ** is the *repair rate*. A 14-mark problem can become a 0-mark disaster if these are swapped.
> 4. **Forgetting the exponential in M(t)** — Maintainability requires $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$, NOT $1 - e^{-\lambda t}$. Mixing these up is a frequent deduction.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — λ in /hour means MTBF in hours. MTTR = 1/μ must match the time unit of MTBF.
> 6. **Incorrect limit application** — For steady-state, the exponential term $e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$ goes to 0 as $t \to \infty$. Some students forget this and keep the transient term in the steady-state answer.
> 7. **Path enumeration errors** — In a bridge network, students miss path {1,3,5} or {2,3,4} — the bridge itself creates *non-obvious* paths. Always use a systematic enumeration (left-to-right or matrix method).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- ☐ **Tie-Set:** A closed loop in the reliability network graph. Represents a *path of survival*.
- ☐ **Minimal Tie-Set (MTS):** A tie-set that has *no proper subset* which is also a tie-set. *Non-redundant* survival path.
- ☐ **Tie-Set Count:** $T = b - n + 1$ (Euler's formula, where *b* = branches, *n* = nodes).
- ☐ **Duality:** Tie-sets (original graph) ↔ Cut-sets (dual graph). Same count, different interpretation.
- ☐ **MTS for System Reliability:** System works if **at least one MTS** is fully intact. Use inclusion-exclusion.
- ☐ **Maintainability:** $M(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$. Probability of *repair* within time *t*.
- ☐ **Repair Rate μ:** Constant rate parameter. MTTR = 1/μ.
- ☐ **Availability Definition:** Probability system is *operational* at time *t*. Combines reliability + maintainability.
- ☐ **Point-Wise A(t):** $A(t) = \dfrac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} + \dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda+\mu} e^{-(\lambda+\mu)t}$
- ☐ **Steady-State A_ss:** $\dfrac{\mu}{\lambda+\mu} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- ☐ **MTBF:** Mean Time Between Failures = 1/λ.
- ☐ **MTTR:** Mean Time To Repair = 1/μ.
- ☐ **Engineering Insight:** Availability is the *operational* metric; reliability alone is insufficient for repairable systems.
- ☐ **Real-World Relevance:** Used in power grids, telecom networks, aircraft fleets, and any maintainable engineering system.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"M-T-A" = Maintainability - Time - Availability"**. Remember: **M**aintainability fixes the *time after failure*; **A**vailability is the *overall operational readiness*. Both depend on **time** in their arguments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT525 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maintenance Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Maintenance Planning** is the systematic, pre-emptive engineering and managerial process of defining, scheduling, allocating, and controlling all resources (manpower, materials, tools, spares, downtime, and budget) required to keep a physical asset, system, or production facility in a desired state of operational reliability and availability throughout its life cycle.

Mathematically, it is the optimization problem:

$$
\min \; C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{PM}} + C_{\text{CM}} + C_{\text{Downtime}} + C_{\text{Spares}}
$$

subject to the availability constraint:

$$
A(t) = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \;\geq\; A_{\text{required}}
$$

where the parameters are defined precisely in the Formula Sheet (Section 2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PESFT525 – Module 4):**
> Maintenance Planning integrates **preventive maintenance (PM)**, **predictive maintenance (PdM)**, **corrective maintenance (CM)**, and **reliability-centered maintenance (RCM)** into a single coherent life-cycle strategy. It is the operational bridge between *reliability design* and *real-world plant performance*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Body Annual Health Check"

Think of an industrial plant the way a family doctor treats a patient:

- **The Patient (Plant):** has many "organs" (pumps, motors, valves, bearings).
- **The Doctor (Maintenance Engineer):** does not wait for a heart attack (catastrophic failure). Instead, the doctor:
  - Schedules **periodic check-ups** → *Preventive Maintenance*.
  - Runs **blood tests / scans** that detect early warning signs → *Predictive / Condition-Based Maintenance*.
  - Reacts immediately if the patient falls sick → *Corrective / Breakdown Maintenance*.
  - Prescribes lifestyle changes to extend life → *Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)*.
  - Allocates a yearly **health budget** → *Maintenance Budget Planning*.

**Maintenance Planning** is the "treatment calendar and budget book" of that plant.

## 1.3 Core Maintenance Strategies — At a Glance

| Strategy | Trigger | When Applied | KTU Key Concept |
|----------|---------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Reactive (BM)** | Failure occurs | Non-critical, low-cost items | Run-to-failure |
| **Preventive (PM)** | Calendar / Usage | Critical wear items | Scheduled overhaul |
| **Predictive (PdM)** | Condition Monitoring | High-value assets | Vibration / Oil analysis |
| **Corrective (CM)** | After fault detection | Restorative | Repair after failure |
| **RCM** | Function-based logic | Complex systems | FMEA-driven |
| **TPM** | Operator-driven | Production lines | Autonomous maintenance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definitions (3-Mark Favorites):**
> - **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures (reliability measure).
> - **MTTR** = Mean Time To Repair (maintainability measure).
> - **Availability** = Uptime fraction = MTBF ⁄ (MTBF + MTTR).
> - **OEE** = Overall Equipment Effectiveness = Availability × Performance × Quality.

---

# SECTION 1 END
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Maintenance Planning Process (5 Logical Phases)

Maintenance Planning is executed in five tightly linked phases. Each phase feeds the next with quantitative data.

### Phase 1 — Asset Identification & Criticality Ranking
- List every maintainable item (bill of materials + functional location).
- Assign each a **criticality index (CI)**:

$$
CI = F \times P \times C
$$

where *F* = failure consequence (1–10), *P* = failure probability (1–10), *C* = cost impact (1–10).
- Items with **CI > 500** → PM mandatory; **CI < 100** → run-to-failure.

### Phase 2 — Maintenance Task Selection
Choose the right task type per item based on its failure mode (from FMEA):
- **On-condition task** (PdM): vibration analysis, thermography, oil debris.
- **Scheduled restoration task** (PM): overhaul at fixed hours.
- **Scheduled discard task**: replace before life limit (e.g., O-ring at 8000 h).
- **Failure-finding task**: for hidden/protective functions (e.g., relief valve).

### Phase 3 — Interval Determination (Key Engineering Step)
Intervals are derived from **reliability theory** or from the **bath-tub curve** regime.

For a constant failure rate $\lambda$ (random-failure region):

$$
T_{\text{PM}} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot k
$$

where *k* is a safety / economic multiplier (commonly 0.5 to 1.0 of the P-F interval).

For a Weibull-distributed wear-out region (shape parameter $\beta > 1$):

$$
T_{\text{PM}} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

where $\eta$ = scale (characteristic life), $\Gamma$ = gamma function.

### Phase 4 — Resource Allocation & Scheduling
Resources are matched against the planned work:
- **Man-hours**, **craft skills**, **spares**, **special tools**, **downtime window**.
- A **Gantt chart** or **CPM/PERT** is used to schedule overlapping tasks to minimize plant shutdown.

### Phase 5 — Cost Optimization & Feedback
The optimal PM interval minimizes the **total cost per unit time**:

$$
C_{\text{total}}(T) = \frac{C_{\text{PM}} + C_{\text{CM}} \cdot \left( \dfrac{T}{T_{\text{MPBF}}} \right)}{T}
$$

where $T_{\text{MPBF}}$ = mean time between PM-induced hidden failures.

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$
\frac{dC_{\text{total}}}{dT} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; T_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_{\text{PM}} \cdot T_{\text{MPBF}}}{C_{\text{CM}}}}
$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Phase

| Phase | Why It Exists | Engineering Insight |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Can't maintain what you haven't listed | Pareto 80/20: 20 % items cause 80 % downtime |
| 2 | Different failure modes need different tactics | A bearing needs oil, not a software update |
| 3 | Wrong interval = over-maintenance or under-maintenance | Both waste money |
| 4 | Downtime = lost production = lost revenue | Scheduling prevents resource conflicts |
| 5 | Planning without measurement drifts | KPIs force continuous improvement |

## 2.3 Maintenance Cost Components (KTU Cost Model)

$$
C_{\text{life-cycle}} = C_{\text{acq}} + \sum_{i=1}^{N}\!\left( C_{\text{PM},i} + C_{\text{CM},i} + C_{\text{down},i} \right) + C_{\text{disposal}}
$$

- **Direct costs:** labour, materials, spares, contractor.
- **Indirect costs:** lost production, late delivery penalties, safety/environmental risk.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning | Units |
|---|-------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Steady-state availability | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | For exponential life (constant hazard) | hours |
| 3 | $\text{MTTR} = M_{\text{ct}} + M_{\text{pt}}$ | Corrective + Preventive repair time | hours |
| 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function | – |
| 5 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | CDF of failure | – |
| 6 | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \lambda$ | Hazard rate (exponential case) | 1/h |
| 7 | $\text{WEFF} = \dfrac{\text{Planned Downtime}}{\text{Planned} + \text{Unplanned}}$ | Maintenance effectiveness | – |
| 8 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | – |
| 9 | $T_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, C_{\text{PM}} \, T_{\text{MPBF}}}{C_{\text{CM}}}}$ | Economic PM interval | hours |
| 10 | $\text{MDT} = \dfrac{\sum t_{\text{down}}}{\text{number of failures}}$ | Mean Downtime | hours |
| 11 | $\text{MDT} = \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT}$ | Logistical + active repair | hours |
| 12 | $CI = F \cdot P \cdot C$ | Criticality Index | score 1–1000 |
| 13 | $\text{PM Compliance} = \dfrac{\text{PMs done on time}}{\text{PMs scheduled}} \times 100$ | Schedule adherence | % |
| 14 | $\text{SLE} = \dfrac{\text{Stock value}}{\text{Annual usage value}}$ | Spare-part stocking level | – |
| 15 | $\text{Inventory Turn Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Annual issues}}{\text{Average inventory}}$ | Spare-part turnover | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for tables:** all vertical absolute-value bars are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power plants:** scheduled boiler-tube PM every 18 000 h.
- **Aviation (MRO):** A-check, B-check, C-check, D-check intervals.
- **Automotive OEM:** TPM pillars (5S, Jishu-Hozen, Kaizen).
- **Oil & Gas offshore:** RCM used on FPSO topside rotating equipment.
- **Manufacturing:** CMMS-driven PM work orders in SAP-PM / Maximo.

---

# SECTION 2 END
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **exhaustive**. No step is skipped. Each algebraic transition is followed by a plain-English row.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal PM Interval $T_{\text{opt}}$

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
We consider a single component that is renewed either by:
- **Preventive replacement** at age $T$ (cost $C_{\text{PM}}$ per replacement), OR
- **Corrective replacement** upon failure (cost $C_{\text{CM}}$ per failure).

Let $\lambda$ be the failure rate of the component *between* PM actions, and let $T_{\text{MPBF}}$ be the mean time between PM-induced (hidden) failures.

### 3.1.2 Cost per Unit Time Function

We construct $C_{\text{total}}(T)$ as the ratio of expected cost per cycle to expected cycle length.

**Step 1 — Expected number of failures per cycle:**
The expected number of failures of a non-replaced component in interval $[0,T]$ under hazard $\lambda$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[N_f] = \int_{0}^{T}\!\lambda\,dt = \lambda T
$$

(For exponential life: $\lambda$ is constant, hence linear.)

**Step 2 — Expected cost per cycle:**

$$
C_{\text{cycle}} = C_{\text{PM}} + C_{\text{CM}} \cdot \lambda T
$$

*Text: PM cost is paid once at the end of the cycle; each failure costs $C_{\text{CM}}$.*

**Step 3 — Expected cycle length:**

$$
L_{\text{cycle}} = T
$$

(We assume the planned replacement actually completes at age $T$.)

**Step 4 — Average cost per unit time:**

$$
C_{\text{total}}(T) = \frac{C_{\text{PM}} + C_{\text{CM}} \lambda T}{T} = \frac{C_{\text{PM}}}{T} + C_{\text{CM}} \lambda
$$

*Text: dividing the cycle cost by the cycle duration gives the long-run cost rate.*

**Step 5 — Differentiate with respect to $T$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{d C_{\text{total}}}{d T} = -\frac{C_{\text{PM}}}{T^{2}} = 0
$$

This is a paradox for a constant-$\lambda$ model — the derivative never vanishes for finite $T$. So we incorporate the *bathtub-aging* term by replacing $\lambda T \to T/T_{\text{MPBF}}$ (Welte's classical model):

$$
C_{\text{total}}(T) = \frac{C_{\text{PM}}}{T} + C_{\text{CM}} \cdot \frac{T}{T_{\text{MPBF}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate again:**

$$
\frac{d C_{\text{total}}}{d T} = -\frac{C_{\text{PM}}}{T^{2}} + \frac{C_{\text{CM}}}{T_{\text{MPBF}}}
$$

**Step 7 — Set derivative to zero:**

$$
-\frac{C_{\text{PM}}}{T_{\text{opt}}^{2}} + \frac{C_{\text{CM}}}{T_{\text{MPBF}}} = 0
$$

**Step 8 — Solve algebraically:**

$$
\frac{C_{\text{PM}}}{T_{\text{opt}}^{2}} = \frac{C_{\text{CM}}}{T_{\text{MPBF}}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{opt}}^{2} = \frac{C_{\text{PM}} \, T_{\text{MPBF}}}{C_{\text{CM}}}
$$

**Step 9 — Take the positive square root (interval must be positive):**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{\text{PM}} \, T_{\text{MPBF}}}{C_{\text{CM}}}}\,}
$$

> This is the **Welte-Whitmore optimum** — a 14-mark KTU favorite. Always quote both the cost model *and* the differentiation step.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Availability $A$

### 3.2.1 Definitions
Long-run availability is the **fraction of calendar time** the equipment is *up*. After many cycles:

$$
A = \lim_{t\to\infty}\frac{\text{uptime}(t)}{t}
$$

### 3.2.2 Cycle View
For a *renewable* system, one complete cycle is: **up for MTBF, then down for MTTR.**

**Step 1 — Uptime in one cycle:**

$$
T_{\text{up}} = \text{MTBF}
$$

**Step 2 — Downtime in one cycle:**

$$
T_{\text{down}} = \text{MTTR}
$$

**Step 3 — Calendar time of one cycle:**

$$
T_{\text{cal}} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}
$$

**Step 4 — Ratio of uptime to calendar time:**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of hazard $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}, \quad \text{MTTR} = \frac{1}{\mu}
$$

$$
A = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}
$$

**Step 6 — Inherent (perfect logistics) availability** (no MLDT, no admin delay):

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}_{\text{active}}}
$$

**Step 7 — Achieved availability** (includes logistic/administrative delay $\text{MLDT}$):

$$
A_a = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT}}
$$

**Step 8 — Operational availability** (includes preventive downtime $\text{MPDT}$ and supply delay):

$$
A_o = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT} + \text{MPDT}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** many students write $A = \text{MTBF}/\text{MTTR}$. The correct ratio is **MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)**, not the inverse. Lose 1 mark if you flip it.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Criticality Index & Pareto-Based PM Selection

### 3.3.1 Setting
A plant has $N$ functional items. Maintenance budget $B$ is fixed. Choose which items to put on PM.

### 3.3.2 Criticality Score

**Step 1 — Each item receives a score:**

$$
CI_i = F_i \, P_i \, C_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N
$$

**Step 2 — Rank items in descending order of $CI_i$:**

$$
CI_{(1)} \ge CI_{(2)} \ge \cdots \ge CI_{(N)}
$$

**Step 3 — Cumulative cost vs cumulative $CI$ curve.** The PM line is the budget line $B$ that maximizes cumulative criticality covered:

$$
\max_{k}\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} CI_{(i)} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{k} C_{\text{PM},i} \le B
$$

**Step 4 — Optimal $k^*$ is the largest index satisfying the budget.** Items $1 \dots k^*$ go on PM; the rest stay on run-to-failure.

> **Engineering Insight:** this is the **Pareto-Optimality** foundation of RCM and TPM — focus effort on the vital few.

## 3.4 Worked Example (14-Mark Type)

> **Q:** A pump has MTBF = 2000 h, MTTR = 8 h, MLDT = 12 h. PM cost ₹8000, corrective cost ₹50 000, $T_{\text{MPBF}} = 10\,000$ h. Find (a) optimal PM interval, (b) availability, (c) cost saving vs. run-to-failure.

### Solution
**(a) Optimal PM interval:**

$$
T_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{\text{PM}} \, T_{\text{MPBF}}}{C_{\text{CM}}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8000 \times 10000}{50000}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{\dfrac{8 \times 10^{7}}{5 \times 10^{4}}} = \sqrt{1600} = 40 \text{ h}
$$

**(b) Steady-state availability (active MTTR only):**

$$
A = \dfrac{2000}{2000 + 8} = \dfrac{2000}{2008} = 0.9960 = 99.60\%
$$

**(c) Annual cost comparison (assume 8760 h/year):**

- **Run-to-failure cost per year:** $\lambda \times 8760 \times C_{\text{CM}} = (1/2000) \times 8760 \times 50\,000 = ₹2.19 \times 10^6$.
- **With optimal PM at 40 h:** cycles/year $= 8760/40 = 219$. Each cycle costs $C_{\text{PM}} + C_{\text{CM}} \times (40/10\,000) = 8000 + 200 = 8200$. Total/year $= 219 \times 8200 = ₹1.80 \times 10^6$.
- **Saving per year:** $2.19 - 1.80 = ₹3.9 \times 10^5$ (~18 %).

> **Total marks awarded: 14 (a) = 7 marks, (b) + (c) = 7 marks.**

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Maintenance Strategies

| Strategy | Cost | Reliability Gain | Skill Needed | KTU Marks Weight |
|----------|------|------------------|--------------|------------------|
| Reactive (BM) | Lowest direct | Lowest | Low | 1–2 |
| Preventive (PM) | Medium | High | Medium | 3–4 |
| Predictive (PdM) | High instrument | Highest | High | 3–4 |
| Corrective (CM) | Variable | Restorative | Medium | 2 |
| RCM | High design | Optimum | Highest | 5–6 |
| TPM | Low–medium | High culture | Medium | 3–4 |


## 4.2 Maintenance Strategy Decision Tree (RCM-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Equipment Item Identified] --> B2{Can failure cause safety or environmental impact}
    B2 -- Yes --> B3[Proactive PM or Redesign Mandatory]

    B4 -- Yes --> B5[Failure Finding Task Scheduled]
    B4 -- No --> B6{Cost of PM greater than cost of failure}
    B6 -- Yes --> B7[Run to Failure Reactive]

    B13 --> B1
```

## 4.3 Maintenance Cost vs Interval Optimization Curve

```mermaid
    C3[Interval T at Topt] --> C4[Minimum Ctotal Economic optimum]
```
