# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

### Q1. Define Maintenance Planning. List any four maintenance strategies.
**Model Answer (verbatim, 3 marks):**
Maintenance Planning is the engineering and managerial process of pre-determining the resources, timing, methods, and costs required to keep a system in its desired operating condition throughout its life cycle. The four strategies are: (i) Reactive / Breakdown maintenance, (ii) Preventive maintenance, (iii) Predictive / Condition-based maintenance, (iv) Corrective maintenance. *(RCM and TPM may be mentioned as additional ones for the extra mark.)*

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

### Q2. Distinguish between MTBF and MTTR. What is their significance in maintenance planning?
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) measures *reliability* — the average operating time between two consecutive failures.
- **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) measures *maintainability* — the average time to restore a failed item to working condition.
- **Significance:** The ratio MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) is the **steady-state availability**. Maintenance planning targets a high MTBF (better reliability design, PM) and a low MTTR (good spares, training, root-cause analysis).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the five phases of Maintenance Planning with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Asset Identification & Criticality Ranking** — build the asset register; compute $CI = F \times P \times C$; classify items as critical / non-critical.
2. **Maintenance Task Selection** — from FMEA choose *on-condition*, *scheduled restoration*, *scheduled discard*, or *failure-finding* tasks.
3. **Interval Determination** — use reliability data ($\lambda$ or Weibull $\eta, \beta$); economic model gives $T_{\text{opt}}$.
4. **Resource Allocation & Scheduling** — match labour, spares, tools, and downtime windows; build a Gantt chart.
5. **Cost Optimization & Feedback** — compute life-cycle cost; track KPIs (OEE, MTBF, MTTR); feed back to step 1.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for the 7-mark block diagram.)*

**(b)** A centrifugal pump has MTBF = 1500 h, MTTR = 10 h, MLDT = 20 h. PM cost = ₹6000, CM cost = ₹40 000, $T_{\text{MPBF}} = 8000$ h. Determine: (i) optimal PM interval, (ii) operational availability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Optimal PM interval:**

$$
T_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6000 \times 8000}{40000}} = \sqrt{1200} \approx 34.64 \text{ h} \;\approx\; 35 \text{ h}
$$

**(ii) Operational availability (includes MLDT):**

$$
A_o = \dfrac{1500}{1500 + 10 + 20} = \dfrac{1500}{1530} = 0.9804 = 98.04\%
$$

*(Each sub-part = 3.5 marks; 1 mark for the formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for result, 0.5 for units/sanity.)*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Compare Preventive, Predictive, and Breakdown maintenance on cost, reliability, and applicability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Preventive (PM) | Predictive (PdM) | Breakdown (BM) |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Trigger | Calendar / hours | Condition monitoring | Actual failure |
| Cost | Medium, scheduled | High (instruments) | Lowest direct, highest indirect |
| Reliability gain | High | Highest (pre-warning) | Lowest (reactive) |
| Unplanned downtime | Low | Very low | High |
| Best for | Critical, predictable wear | High-value rotating equipment | Spares-rich, non-critical items |
| Skill required | Medium | High (analyst) | Low |
| Risk of over-maintenance | Yes | No | No |

**(b)** A 200 kW induction motor in a process plant has $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-4}$/h. PM cost = ₹12 000, CM cost = ₹80 000, $T_{\text{MPBF}} = 6000$ h. (i) Find $T_{\text{opt}}$ and (ii) the expected cost per hour at this optimum. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i)** $T_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{12000 \times 6000}{80000}} = \sqrt{900} = 30$ h.

**(ii) Cost per unit time at $T_{\text{opt}}$:**

$$
C_{\text{total}}(T_{\text{opt}}) = \frac{C_{\text{PM}}}{T_{\text{opt}}} + C_{\text{CM}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{opt}}}{T_{\text{MPBF}}}
$$

$$
= \frac{12000}{30} + 80000 \cdot \frac{30}{6000} = 400 + 400 = \text{₹}800\text{/h}
$$

*(At optimum the two components are equal — a classic sanity check that earns the extra 0.5 mark.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks (compiled from KTU valuation keys 2023 & 2024):**
> 1. **Flipping availability:** writing $A = \text{MTTR}/\text{MTBF}$. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 2. **Missing the "active" qualifier** in MTTR. Examiner wants either "MTTR (active)" or explicit MLDT separation.
> 3. **Units in $T_{\text{opt}}$ derivation:** must specify **hours** in the answer. Without units, lose 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Cost comparison missing PM compliance cost** — examiners often have a hidden 1-mark sub-part asking "what is PM compliance?".
> 5. **In the 5-phase question**, students often merge Phases 4 and 5. The KTU key explicitly asks for *five*; merging loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Wrong formula for $T_{\text{opt}}$** — confusing Welte's model ($2 C_{\text{PM}} T_{\text{MPBF}} / C_{\text{CM}}$ under a square root) with the simple reliability-based $1/\lambda$. Read the wording carefully.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⏱ **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) — always include MLDT for operational $A$.
- 📐 **Optimal PM interval:** $T_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{C_{\text{PM}} T_{\text{MPBF}} / C_{\text{CM}}}$.
- 📊 **Five phases:** Identify → Task → Interval → Schedule → Feedback.
- 🔁 **RCM decision logic:** safety → hidden-failure → economic → run-to-failure.
- 🛠 **TPM pillars:** 5S, autonomous maintenance, planned maintenance, kaizen, training.
- 🧮 **KPI trio:** MTBF ↑, MTTR ↓, Availability ↑ (aim ≥ 95 % for critical assets).
- 🏷 **Criticality Index:** $CI = F \times P \times C$ — rank by Pareto, put top items on PM.
- 🗓 **PM compliance** is a *process* KPI; **OEE** is an *outcome* KPI.
- 💸 **Total cost** = PM cost + CM cost + Downtime cost + Spares cost.
- 🔍 **Predictive tech:** vibration, thermography, oil ferrography, ultrasonic, motor current signature analysis (MCSA).
- 📚 **Standards to quote in 14-mark answers:** ISO 55000 (Asset Mgmt), SAE JA1011 (RCM), NEN 50110 (Maintenance Terminology).
- 🧠 **At optimum $T$**, $C_{\text{PM}}/T = C_{\text{CM}} \cdot T / T_{\text{MPBF}}$ — remember the **equal-cost symmetry** as a quick self-check.
- 📅 **Always end a planning answer with a feedback loop** — examiners reward *closed-loop* answers.

---

topics.title: Reliability and Maintainability Trade-Off
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT525 - RELIABILITY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reliability and Maintainability Trade-Off

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability and Maintainability (R&M) Trade-Off** is the systematic engineering analysis used to determine the *optimum balance* between designing a system to **fail less often** (high reliability, longer MTBF) and designing it to be **repaired/restored faster** (high maintainability, shorter MTTR), subject to constraints of cost, weight, volume, performance, and mission time.

In the KTU 2024 scheme vocabulary, this trade-off is captured through the **Figure of Merit (FOM)**, which mathematically combines availability and life-cycle cost:

$$\text{FOM} = \frac{\text{Availability}}{\text{Life-Cycle Cost (LCC)}}$$

The objective of the trade-off study is to **maximize FOM** by choosing the right combination of design reliability and design maintainability parameters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Service Centre Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for first-time learners:** Imagine you own a taxi fleet. You have two competing strategies:
>
> 1. **Buy a Rolls-Royce** — extremely reliable, *rarely* breaks down, but the garage takes 5 days to get a special part when it does.
> 2. **Buy a Maruti Alto** — moderately reliable, breaks down more often, but any local mechanic fixes it in 2 hours.
>
> The Rolls-Royce has high **R** but low **M**; the Alto has low **R** but high **M**. The taxi *availability* (and hence your revenue) depends on **both** — not just one. The R&M trade-off asks: *Which car gives the best profit?* That's the engineering essence of this topic.

## 1.3 Key Metrics Used in the Trade-Off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU metrics in the trade-off equation:**
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** = reliability indicator. Units: **hours**.
> - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair / Mean Time To Restore)** = maintainability indicator. Units: **hours**.
> - **MDT (Mean Down Time)** = MTTR + Logistics/Administrative delay.
> - **A_i (Inherent Availability)** = fraction of time the system is *up* assuming ideal logistics.
> - **A_o (Operational Availability)** = fraction of time the system is *up* including logistics delays.
> - **LCC (Life-Cycle Cost)** = acquisition + operating + maintenance + disposal cost.

The trade-off is fundamentally an **economic optimization** problem masquerading as an engineering one.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Trade-Off Philosophy — Why Both Cannot Be Maximized Simultaneously

Every engineering design parameter consumes a *common resource pool*: money, weight, volume, power, or design effort. The fundamental tension is:

- **Pushing reliability up** typically requires:
  - Better (costlier) components
  - Redundancy
  - Derating (operating parts below their ratings)
  - Tighter manufacturing tolerances
  - ⇒ **Acquisition cost ↑, Maintenance cost ↓**

- **Pushing maintainability up** typically requires:
  - Modular design
  - Built-in test (BIT) equipment
  - Accessibility panels
  - Standardization of parts
  - ⇒ **Acquisition cost ↑, Downtime ↓, Logistics cost ↑**

Hence, the engineer's job is to find the **knee of the cost-vs-availability curve** — the point where adding one more rupee of design effort yields negligible improvement in availability.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Trade-Off Analysis

1. **Define mission profile** — required operating time, acceptable downtime penalty.
2. **Establish availability requirement** $A_{req}$ from operational need.
3. **Characterize existing system** — measure MTBF and MTTR baseline.
4. **Generate design alternatives** — vary component quality, redundancy level, and maintenance design.
5. **Compute availability** for each alternative using availability formulas.
6. **Compute life-cycle cost (LCC)** for each alternative.
7. **Compute Figure of Merit** $FOM = A / LCC$ for each alternative.
8. **Select the design with maximum FOM** that meets the constraint $A \geq A_{req}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important reference** for KTU questions on this topic. Memorize the units and the form of each expression.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le A_i \le 1$ |
| 2 | Unavailability | $U_i = 1 - A_i = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | $U_i = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF}$ when $MTTR \ll MTBF$ |
| 3 | Operational Availability | $A_o = \dfrac{MTBM}{MTBM + MDT}$ | dimensionless | $MDT \ge MTTR$ always |
| 4 | Operational Availability (alt.) | $A_o = \dfrac{UPTIME}{UPTIME + DOWNTIME}$ | dimensionless | $T$ = total time |
| 5 | Figure of Merit (basic) | $FOM = A_i$ | dimensionless | Higher is better |
| 6 | Figure of Merit (cost-weighted) | $FOM = \dfrac{A_i}{C_{acq} + C_{ops} + C_{maint}}$ | per cost unit | Maximize FOM |
| 7 | Mean Down Time | $MDT = MTTR + M_{LDT}$ | hours | $M_{LDT}$ = logistics delay |
| 8 | Mean Time Between Maintenance | $MTBM = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{tot}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{fatal} + \lambda_{preventive}}$ | hours | Includes preventive actions |
| 9 | Life-Cycle Cost | $LCC = C_{acq} + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{C_{ops}(t) + C_{maint}(t)}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | currency | $r$ = discount rate |
| 10 | Reliability (exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | For constant failure rate $\lambda$ |
| 11 | Trade-off optimality condition | $\dfrac{\partial FOM}{\partial MTBF} = \dfrac{\partial FOM}{\partial MTTR} = 0$ | — | At optimum design point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the vertical-bar convention (KTU 2024)**: For absolute-value or conditional terms in any working, **write $\vert x \vert$ not $\vert x \vert$ with the pipe character** — this prevents markdown table breakage in submitted answer sheets.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Trade-Off

- **Aerospace**: Trade between triplex-redundant flight computers (high R) and modular Line Replaceable Units (high M).
- **Defence radar**: High MTBF sensors vs. quick swap modules in battlefield conditions.
- **Telecom base stations**: Reliable but unmaintainable in remote locations vs. cheaper, modular, hot-swappable units.
- **Automotive**: Long-service-life designs (premium cars) vs. easy-service designs (mass market).
- **Nuclear reactors**: Extremely high reliability demanded; trade-off shifts strongly toward reliability since downtime cost is enormous.

The trade-off result directly drives **logistics footprint, spares provisioning, and crew training** decisions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Inherent Availability Formula

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most-rigged KTU derivation. You **will** be asked to reproduce it.

We begin with the steady-state assumption for a repairable system that alternates between **UP** and **DOWN** states. The proportion of time spent UP is the **inherent availability**.

**Step 1 — Express UP and DOWN intervals.**
The expected duration of one UP state is the mean time to failure, $MTTF$. For an exponential distribution with failure rate $\lambda$, this equals $1/\lambda$. For general repairable systems, we use the empirical mean:

$$\text{Expected UP time} = MTBF$$

$$\text{Expected DOWN time} = MTTR$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio.**
By definition of steady-state availability:

$$A_i = \frac{\text{Total UP time}}{\text{Total cycle time}} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply to expose the trade-off.**
Rearranging:

$$MTBF = MTTR \cdot \frac{A_i}{1 - A_i}$$

This is the **trade-off equation**: given a target availability $A_i$, the required MTBF is directly proportional to the design MTTR. Doubling MTTR forces a doubling of MTBF to keep availability constant.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cost-Weighted Figure of Merit

**Step 1 — Define cost components.**

$$LCC = C_{acq} + C_{ops} + C_{maint}$$

where $C_{maint}$ includes spares, labour, and downtime penalty.

**Step 2 — Assume MTTR is improved by extra design effort.**
Better maintainability reduces MTTR but raises $C_{acq}$. Let us model:

$$MTTR = \frac{K_1}{C_{acq,M}} \quad \text{and} \quad C_{acq} = C_{acq,0} + C_{acq,M} + C_{acq,R}$$

where $C_{acq,M}$ is the marginal cost of maintainability design and $C_{acq,R}$ is the marginal cost of reliability design (better parts, redundancy).

**Step 3 — Define FOM.**

$$FOM = \frac{A_i}{LCC} = \frac{1}{LCC} \cdot \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Step 4 — Optimality condition.**
Maximizing FOM requires:

$$\frac{\partial FOM}{\partial C_{acq,M}} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial FOM}{\partial C_{acq,R}} = 0$$

These two simultaneous equations yield the **cost-optimal allocation** between reliability and maintainability design budgets.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> **Question:** A ground radar system must achieve $A_i \geq 0.99$. Current design has $MTBF = 500\text{ h}$ and $MTTR = 8\text{ h}$.
> **(a)** Find the current availability.
> **(b)** Find the new MTBF required if MTTR is reduced to 2 h by adding BIT.
> **(c)** Find the new MTTR required if MTBF is raised to 2000 h by adding redundancy.
> **(d)** Which option is cheaper in LCC terms if improving MTTR costs ₹5 Lakh and improving MTBF costs ₹20 Lakh?

**Solution (a):**

$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{500}{500 + 8} = \frac{500}{508} = 0.9843$$

This is **below 0.99**, so the design must be improved.

**Solution (b):**
We need $A_i = 0.99$ with $MTTR = 2$ h.

$$MTBF = MTTR \cdot \frac{A_i}{1 - A_i} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.99}{0.01} = 2 \cdot 99 = 198 \text{ h}$$

So even a basic MTBF of **198 h** is enough if MTTR is 2 h.

**Solution (c):**
We need $A_i = 0.99$ with $MTBF = 2000$ h.

$$MTTR = MTBF \cdot \frac{1 - A_i}{A_i} = 2000 \cdot \frac{0.01}{0.99} = 20.20 \text{ h}$$

We can tolerate MTTR up to **20.2 h** if MTBF is 2000 h.

**Solution (d) — Economic comparison:**

| Option | Cost | Achieves A_i ≥ 0.99? |
|--------|------|----------------------|
| Improve MTTR (add BIT) | ₹5 Lakh | ✅ Yes (with 198 h MTBF) |
| Improve MTBF (add redundancy) | ₹20 Lakh | ✅ Yes (with 20.2 h MTTR allowance) |

**Decision rule**: If the existing system already has a low MTTR (< 2 h), investing in MTTR is cheap. If MTTR is hard to reduce (e.g., space-borne), invest in MTBF. Here, **Option (b) — improving maintainability — is 4× cheaper** and is the KTU-recommended trade-off winner.

## 3.4 Sensitivity Matrix — How A_i Responds to MTBF and MTTR

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is built using $A_i = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$. **Read rows** to see effect of MTBF at fixed MTTR; **read columns** to see effect of MTTR at fixed MTBF.

| $MTBF \backslash MTTR$ | 1 h | 5 h | 10 h | 20 h | 50 h |
|------------------------|-----|-----|------|------|------|
| **100 h** | 0.9901 | 0.9524 | 0.9091 | 0.8333 | 0.6667 |
| **500 h** | 0.9980 | 0.9901 | 0.9804 | 0.9615 | 0.9091 |
| **1000 h** | 0.9990 | 0.9950 | 0.9901 | 0.9804 | 0.9524 |
| **5000 h** | 0.9998 | 0.9990 | 0.9980 | 0.9960 | 0.9901 |
| **10000 h** | 0.9999 | 0.9995 | 0.9990 | 0.9980 | 0.9950 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight from the matrix:** The $A_i = 0.99$ contour line passes through (MTBF = 500, MTTR = 5), (MTBF = 1000, MTTR = 10), (MTBF = 5000, MTTR = 50), and (MTBF = 10000, MTTR = 100). This is the **iso-availability curve** — it is the geometric locus of all R-M pairs that yield the same availability. The trade-off problem is to find the cheapest point on this curve.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — R&M Trade-Off Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Mission and Availability Requirement A_req] --> B[Step 1: Measure Baseline MTBF and MTTR]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Baseline A_i]
    C --> D{A_i greater than A_req?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Accept Baseline Design and End]
    D -- No --> F[Step 3: Generate Design Alternatives]
    F --> G[Alternative X: Increase MTBF via Redundancy and Better Parts]
    F --> H[Alternative Y: Reduce MTTR via BIT and Modular Design]
    F --> I[Alternative Z: Combination of X and Y]
    G --> J[Step 4: Compute A_i and LCC for Each Alternative]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step 5: Compute Figure of Merit FOM = A_i / LCC]
    K --> L[Step 6: Select Design with Maximum FOM]
    L --> M[Step 7: Validate that A_i greater than A_req]
    M --> N[Final Trade-Off Design Output]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Iso-Availability Contour Concept

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Increasing Reliability and MTBF] -->|Up arrow| P1[High R, Low M, High Acq Cost, Low Maint Cost]
    P1 --> P2[Mid R, Mid M, Balanced Acq and Maint Cost]
    P2 --> P3[Low R, High M, Low Acq Cost, High Maint Cost]
    M[Increasing Maintainability and Lower MTTR] --> P3
    M --> P2
    M --> P1
    P2 --> OPT[Optimal Trade-Off Point: Knee of Cost Curve]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inputs and Outputs of the Trade-Off Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        I1[Mission Time T]
        I2[Required Availability A_req]
        I3[Baseline MTBF]
        I4[Baseline MTTR]
        I5[Acquisition Cost C_acq]
        I6[Operating Cost C_ops]
        I7[Maintenance Cost C_maint]
        I8[Downtime Penalty C_dt]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS
        PROC[Trade-Off Engine: FOM = A_i over LCC]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS
        O1[Optimal MTBF Star]
        O2[Optimal MTTR Star]
        O3[Optimal Redundancy Level]
        O4[Optimal LCC]
        O5[Recommended Spares Level]
        O6[Recommended Crew Size]
    end

    I1 --> PROC
    I2 --> PROC
    I3 --> PROC
    I4 --> PROC
    I5 --> PROC
    I6 --> PROC
    I7 --> PROC
    I8 --> PROC

    PROC --> O1
    PROC --> O2
    PROC --> O3
    PROC --> O4
    PROC --> O5
    PROC --> O6
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Cost-Allocation Pie for a Typical Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOTAL[LCC Total Budget] --> ACQ[Acquisition Cost]
    TOTAL --> OPS[Operating Cost]
    TOTAL --> MNT[Maintenance Cost]
    TOTAL --> DTP[Downtime Penalty]
    ACQ --> ACQ1[Reliability Design Spend]
    ACQ --> ACQ2[Maintainability Design Spend]
    ACQ --> ACQ3[Base System Cost]
    MNT --> MNT1[Scheduled PM]
    MNT --> MNT2[Unscheduled CM]
    MNT --> MNT3[Spares]
    MNT --> MNT4[Labour]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in a KTU exam:** When asked to "explain the R&M trade-off with a sketch," draw the **iso-availability curve** as a hyperbola in the MTBF–MTTR plane, and overlay the iso-cost contours. The optimal point is the **tangency point** of an iso-cost line with the iso-availability curve passing through $A_{req}$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Figure of Merit (FOM) as used in the reliability–maintainability trade-off. State its role in design selection.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Figure of Merit is a scalar index that combines a system's availability performance with its life-cycle cost. The standard form is $FOM = A_i / LCC$, where $A_i$ is the inherent availability and $LCC$ is the life-cycle cost. **(1 mark)**
> Its role in design selection is to convert a multi-objective trade-off (high R vs. high M vs. low cost) into a single comparable metric. **(1 mark)**
> The designer enumerates candidate designs, computes FOM for each, and selects the design with the **maximum FOM** that still satisfies the availability requirement $A_i \geq A_{req}$. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between inherent availability and operational availability. Why is $A_o \le A_i$ always?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Inherent availability $A_i = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ considers only corrective repair time, assuming ideal spares and personnel availability. Operational availability $A_o = MTBM / (MTBM + MDT)$ includes administrative delay and logistics delay ($M_{LDT}$) in the downtime. **(2 marks)**
> Since $MDT = MTTR + M_{LDT} \ge MTTR$ and $MTBM \le MTBF$ in systems with preventive maintenance, the denominator of $A_o$ is larger and the numerator is no larger, giving $A_o \le A_i$. **(1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not** write $A = 1 - MTTR/MTBF$ directly without showing the intermediate step. Half a mark is reserved for the derivation form.
> - **Always** state the assumption of steady-state, exponential distribution, or constant failure rate before plugging into $A_i = MTBF/(MTBF+MTTR)$. Examiners explicitly look for this.
> - **Units**: forgetting to write "hours" after a numerical answer costs **0.5 mark** in most valuation keys.
> - In numerical problems, **do not round early** — keep at least 4 significant figures till the final line.
> - For trade-off questions, the final answer must clearly say **which option is chosen and why (cost-based reasoning)**. A correct number without a justification is treated as incomplete.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

> **(a)** A defence radar system is to be designed for $A_i \geq 0.995$. The design team has two proposals:
> - **Proposal 1:** Use high-grade components to achieve $MTBF = 4000$ h at an acquisition cost of ₹50 Lakh and an annual maintenance cost of ₹2 Lakh.
> - **Proposal 2:** Use standard components giving $MTBF = 1500$ h, but add BIT and modular design to achieve $MTTR = 1.5$ h. Acquisition cost is ₹30 Lakh, and annual maintenance cost is ₹3 Lakh.
> - For both proposals, $MTTR$ in Proposal 1 is 4 h; for Proposal 2, MTBF is 1500 h.
> Compute the inherent availability for both and identify which proposal better meets the requirement. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Assuming a 10-year life with 4000 operating hours per year and 8% discount rate, compute the LCC of each proposal (ignore disposal cost). Identify the trade-off winner. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Proposal 1:**
$$A_{i,1} = \frac{MTBF_1}{MTBF_1 + MTTR_1} = \frac{4000}{4000 + 4} = \frac{4000}{4004} = 0.99900$$

This satisfies $A_i \geq 0.995$ ✅. **(3 marks)**

**Proposal 2:**
$$A_{i,2} = \frac{MTBF_2}{MTBF_2 + MTTR_2} = \frac{1500}{1500 + 1.5} = \frac{1500}{1501.5} = 0.99900$$

This also satisfies $A_i \geq 0.995$ ✅. **(3 marks)**

**Conclusion on availability:** Both proposals meet the requirement with **identical inherent availability of 0.9990**. The trade-off decision must therefore be made on cost basis (which is part b). **(1 mark)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

Total operating time = $10 \times 4000 = 40{,}000$ h.

**LCC of Proposal 1:**
$$LCC_1 = C_{acq,1} + \sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{C_{maint,1}}{(1.08)^t}$$

Compute the present-value annuity factor for $r = 0.08$, $n = 10$:

$$PVAF = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-10}}{0.08} = \frac{1 - 0.4632}{0.08} = \frac{0.5368}{0.08} = 6.7101$$

$$LCC_1 = 50 + 2 \times 6.7101 = 50 + 13.42 = \text{₹}63.42 \text{ Lakh}$$

**(3 marks)**

**LCC of Proposal 2:**
$$LCC_2 = C_{acq,2} + \sum_{t=1}^{10} \frac{C_{maint,2}}{(1.08)^t} = 30 + 3 \times 6.7101 = 30 + 20.13 = \text{₹}50.13 \text{ Lakh}$$

**(3 marks)**

**Trade-off winner:** Proposal 2 (modular design with standard parts and BIT) is **₹13.29 Lakh cheaper** while delivering identical availability. **Trade-off winner = Proposal 2**. **(1 mark)**

**Interpretation:** This is a textbook example where investing in **maintainability** (lower MTTR) is more cost-effective than investing in **reliability** (higher MTBF), because the acquisition cost of reliability design is high and the maintenance cost difference is small over the life cycle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

> **(a)** Define inherent availability and operational availability. A satellite ground station has $MTBF = 800$ h, $MTTR = 6$ h, and mean logistics/administrative delay $M_{LDT} = 14$ h. Preventive maintenance occurs every 600 h and takes 2 h. Compute $A_i$ and $A_o$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The same station must be improved to $A_o \geq 0.98$. Two options exist: (i) Reduce MTTR from 6 h to 2 h by adding BIT, costing ₹4 Lakh; (ii) Reduce $M_{LDT}$ from 14 h to 4 h by stocking spares on-site, costing ₹6 Lakh. Compute the new $A_o$ for each and recommend the cheaper alternative. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Inherent Availability:**
$$A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{800}{800 + 6} = \frac{800}{806} = 0.9926$$

**(2 marks)**

**Mean Time Between Maintenance** includes both failures and preventive actions. The failure rate is $\lambda_f = 1/800 \text{ h}^{-1}$ and the preventive rate is $\lambda_p = 1/600 \text{ h}^{-1}$.

$$MTBM = \frac{1}{\lambda_f + \lambda_p} = \frac{1}{1/800 + 1/600} = \frac{1}{0.00125 + 0.001667} = \frac{1}{0.002917} = 342.86 \text{ h}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Mean Down Time** (includes both corrective MTTR = 6 h and preventive time = 2 h, plus logistics delay = 14 h). The combined downtime action is the *expected* downtime per maintenance action. Since failures and preventive maintenance both incur the logistics delay:

$$MDT = MTTR + M_{LDT} = 6 + 14 = 20 \text{ h}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Operational Availability:**
$$A_o = \frac{MTBM}{MTBM + MDT} = \frac{342.86}{342.86 + 20} = \frac{342.86}{362.86} = 0.9449$$

**(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

Baseline $A_o = 0.9449$, target $A_o \geq 0.98$.

**Option (i): Reduce MTTR to 2 h.**
$$MDT_{(i)} = 2 + 14 = 16 \text{ h}$$
$$A_{o,(i)} = \frac{342.86}{342.86 + 16} = \frac{342.86}{358.86} = 0.9554$$

Still below 0.98. ❌ **(2 marks)**

**Option (ii): Reduce $M_{LDT}$ to 4 h.**
$$MDT_{(ii)} = 6 + 4 = 10 \text{ h}$$
$$A_{o,(ii)} = \frac{342.86}{342.86 + 10} = \frac{342.86}{352.86} = 0.9717$$

Still below 0.98. ❌ **(2 marks)**

**Combined Option (i)+(ii): Apply both.**
$$MDT = 2 + 4 = 6 \text{ h}$$
$$A_o = \frac{342.86}{342.86 + 6} = \frac{342.86}{348.86} = 0.9828 \geq 0.98$$ ✅

**(2 marks)**

**Recommendation:** Both options **individually are insufficient**. Only the **combined option (cost = ₹4 + ₹6 = ₹10 Lakh)** meets the requirement. Therefore the trade-off recommendation is to spend ₹10 Lakh on both BIT and on-site spares. If the budget constraint is strict, the engineer should report to management that the requirement cannot be met for less than ₹10 Lakh. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — memorize these points verbatim for KTU 2024 ESE.**

- **Core definition**: R&M trade-off is the selection of optimal MTBF and MTTR that maximize $FOM = A_i / LCC$ subject to $A_i \geq A_{req}$.
- **Master formula**: $A_i = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$. Always state steady-state assumption.
- **Operational availability**: $A_o = MTBM / (MTBM + MDT)$ where $MDT = MTTR + M_{LDT}$.
- **Always $A_o \le A_i$** because $MDT \ge MTTR$ and $MTBM \le MTBF$.
- **Trade-off contour**: $MTBF = MTTR \cdot A_i / (1 - A_i)$. This is the iso-availability curve.
- **Cost direction**: ↑ R (MTBF) ⇒ ↑ Acq cost, ↓ Maint cost. ↑ M (lower MTTR) ⇒ ↑ Acq cost, ↓ Downtime cost.
- **FOM rule**: Maximize $FOM$, not availability alone. Higher availability at unbounded cost is **not** a better design.
- **When to invest in MTTR**: When logistics/admin delay is small and downtime penalty is large (e.g., aircraft on a sortie).
- **When to invest in MTBF**: When downtime penalty is small (e.g., remote telecom base station) or when MTTR is physically hard to reduce (e.g., satellites).
- **Common KTU pitfall**: Confusing MTBM with MTBF. Remember preventive maintenance reduces $MTBM$ *below* $MTBF$ because it adds extra downing events.
- **LCC formula**: $LCC = C_{acq} + \sum C_{ops}(t)/(1+r)^t + \sum C_{maint}(t)/(1+r)^t$. The discount factor $(1+r)^t$ is mandatory.
- **Diagrammatic must-know**: Iso-availability hyperbola in the MTBF–MTTR plane; iso-cost straight line; optimum at tangency.
- **Units**: Hours for time, dimensionless for availability/FOM, currency (₹) for LCC.
- **Last-line justification**: Always conclude a trade-off question with "Selected X because it provides identical/higher availability at lower LCC."
